Drawing a circle of radius (say) 5 cm, we start from the topmost point and draw sectors sequentially with the calculated angles. The **Rent slice (150°)** will be the largest, occupying almost half the chart, followed by **Food (90°)** — exactly a quarter of the circle.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Pie Chart vs. Alternatives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners frequently ask: "Is a pie chart the appropriate choice for this data? Justify."**

| Data Characteristic | Recommended Chart | Reasoning |
|---------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Categorical, ≤ 6 classes, part-to-whole | **Pie Chart** | Clear proportional display |
| Categorical, > 6 classes | **Bar Chart** | Pie becomes cluttered |
| Ordinal data | **Bar Chart / Pareto** | Preserves ordering |
| Numerical continuous data | **Histogram** | Shows distribution shape |
| Time-series trends | **Line Chart** | Pie cannot show trends |
| Comparing two part-to-whole sets | **Stacked Bar Chart** | Easier side-by-side comparison |
| Data with negative values | **Bar Chart** | Pie requires non-negative values |
| Highly similar slice sizes | **Bar Chart** | Pie makes near-equal slices indistinguishable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Pie Chart Construction

The following diagram maps the complete **input–process–output** pipeline used to construct a pie chart, in compliance with the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, plain uppercase labels, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START] --> B1[STEP 1: Collect Raw Categorical Data]
    B1 --> B2[STEP 2: Tabulate Categories and Frequencies]
    B2 --> C1[STEP 3: Compute Total Frequency T]
    C1 --> D1[STEP 4: Compute Percentage Pi for Each Category]
    D1 --> E1[STEP 5: Compute Central Angle Theta i equals 3 point 6 times Pi]
    E1 --> F1[STEP 6: Verify Sum of Theta i equals 360 degrees]
    F1 --> G1{Verification Passed?}
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[STEP 7: Draw Circle with Compass]
    G1 -->|No| I1[RETURN TO STEP 3 and Recompute]
    H1 --> J1[STEP 8: Plot Sectors Sequentially Using Protractor]
    J1 --> K1[STEP 9: Label Each Slice with Name Frequency and Percentage]
    K1 --> L1[STEP 10: Add Title Legend and Source Note]
    L1 --> M1[FINAL PIE CHART READY FOR INTERPRETATION]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Layout of a Generic Pie Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[WHOLE CIRCLE 360 DEGREES] --> P1[SECTOR A Largest Category]
    P0 --> P2[SECTOR B Second Category]
    P0 --> P3[SECTOR C Third Category]
    P0 --> P4[SECTOR D Smallest Category]
    P1 -. ANGLE 150 DEGREES .-> P0
    P2 -. ANGLE 90 DEGREES .-> P0
    P3 -. ANGLE 75 DEGREES .-> P0
    P4 -. ANGLE 45 DEGREES .-> P0
```

## 4.3 Worked Example Visual Representation (Hostel Expenses)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[HOSTEL EXPENSE PIE CHART] --> Q1[FOOD 3000 RUPEES]
    Q0 --> Q2[RENT 5000 RUPEES]
    Q0 --> Q3[TRAVEL 1500 RUPEES]
    Q0 --> Q4[BOOKS 1000 RUPEES]
    Q0 --> Q5[MISC 1500 RUPEES]
    Q1 -. ANGLE 90 DEGREES .-> Q0
    Q2 -. ANGLE 150 DEGREES .-> Q0
    Q3 -. ANGLE 45 DEGREES .-> Q0
    Q4 -. ANGLE 30 DEGREES .-> Q0
    Q5 -. ANGLE 45 DEGREES .-> Q0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Practical Records:**
> The largest central angle corresponds to the category with the **highest frequency**, and the smallest central angle corresponds to the **least frequent category**. Always label your slices directly (inside or via leader lines) to avoid clutter.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: Define a pie chart. State any two situations in which a pie chart is the most suitable representation of data.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pie chart** is a circular graphical representation of qualitative (categorical) data in which the entire circle represents the **whole (100%)** and each category is depicted as a **sector** whose central angle is proportional to its share of the total.

$$\theta_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum f_i} \times 360°$$

**Two suitable situations:**
1. When displaying the **budget allocation** of a company (e.g., Materials, Labour, Overheads) where part-to-whole comparison is the primary objective.
2. When showing the **distribution of marks** obtained by students across grade boundaries (A, B, C, D, F) at a glance.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Q: The angles subtended by four sectors of a pie chart are 90°, 120°, 80°, and 70°. Verify the validity of the chart.**

**Model Answer:**

For a pie chart to be geometrically valid, the sum of all central angles must equal exactly **360°**.

$$\sum \theta_i = 90° + 120° + 80° + 70° = 360° \checkmark$$

Since the sum equals 360°, the given pie chart is **valid and constructible**. The corresponding percentages are $25\%$, $33.33\%$, $22.22\%$, and $19.45\%$ respectively.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation]`

**Q: The marks obtained by 200 students in a class test are classified into five grades as follows. Draw a pie chart for the data and interpret the result.**

| Grade | A | B | C | D | E |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|
| No. of Students | 40 | 60 | 50 | 30 | 20 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute the central angles and construct the pie chart.

**Step 1: Total frequency.**

$$T = 40 + 60 + 50 + 30 + 20 = 200$$

**Step 2: Central angle for each grade.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \frac{40}{200} \times 360° = 72° \\
\theta_B &= \frac{60}{200} \times 360° = 108° \\
\theta_C &= \frac{50}{200} \times 360° = 90° \\
\theta_D &= \frac{30}{200} \times 360° = 54° \\
\theta_E &= \frac{20}{200} \times 360° = 36°
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Verification.**

$$\sum \theta_i = 72° + 108° + 90° + 54° + 36° = 360° \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Draw the pie chart.**

Using a protractor and a circle of radius (say) 6 cm, draw sectors sequentially starting from the top: A (72°), B (108°), C (90°), D (54°), E (36°). Shade each slice with a distinct colour or pattern and label it with the grade letter, frequency, and percentage.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Interpret the pie chart and draw inferences.

1. **Grade B** is the **modal grade**, with the largest share of students (30%).
2. **Grade E** is the **least common**, indicating only 10% of students fall in the lowest bracket.
3. **Pass percentage (Grades A + B + C + D)** is 90%, while only 10% failed (Grade E).
4. The performance distribution is **roughly bell-shaped**, suggesting a balanced academic outcome.
5. The institution may consider **remedial classes** for the 30 students in Grade D and the 20 students in Grade E to reduce the failure rate.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, End Semester Evaluation]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q: A survey on the mode of transport used by 360 employees of a company yielded the following data. Represent the data using a pie chart and state two inferences.**

| Mode | Car | Two-Wheeler | Bus | Bicycle | Walk |
|------|-----|-------------|-----|---------|------|
| Employees | 90 | 120 | 60 | 54 | 36 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Pie chart construction with full computation.

**Step 1: Total employees.**

$$T = 90 + 120 + 60 + 54 + 36 = 360$$

**Step 2: Central angles.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{Car}} &= \frac{90}{360} \times 360° = 90° \\
\theta_{\text{Two-Wheeler}} &= \frac{120}{360} \times 360° = 120° \\
\theta_{\text{Bus}} &= \frac{60}{360} \times 360° = 60° \\
\theta_{\text{Bicycle}} &= \frac{54}{360} \times 360° = 54° \\
\theta_{\text{Walk}} &= \frac{36}{360} \times 360° = 36°
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Verification.**

$$90° + 120° + 60° + 54° + 36° = 360° \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Pie chart drawn** with each slice labelled, shaded distinctly, and accompanied by a legend.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Inferences.

1. **Two-wheelers dominate** the transportation pattern (33.33%), reflecting either fuel-economy preferences or traffic/congestion realities in the city.
2. **Eco-friendly transport (Bicycle + Walk)** accounts for **25%** of employees, indicating a notable environmentally conscious workforce.
3. **Private vehicles (Car + Two-Wheeler)** dominate at **58.33%**, suggesting the company may consider **carpooling incentives** or **shuttle services** to reduce parking demand.
4. **Public transport (Bus) usage is only 16.67%**, an opportunity for the company to partner with local transit authorities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Marks Lost in Verification:** Students often forget the **mandatory verification step** ($\sum \theta_i = 360°$). This single omission costs **1 full mark** in most valuations. Always end your numerical with the verification check.
> 2. **Marks Lost in Labelling:** A pie chart without **labels, title, or legend** is treated as **incomplete**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for an unlabelled chart.
> 3. **Marks Lost in Interpretation:** A common mistake is drawing a beautiful pie chart and writing *no inferences*. For a 14-mark question, interpretation typically carries **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **Marks Lost in Choosing the Wrong Tool:** If asked *"Is a pie chart appropriate?"*, students often answer yes without justification. Always state **why** or **why not**, citing the number of categories and data type.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **pie chart** is a circular diagram where each **sector's central angle** is proportional to the **category's frequency**.
- The master formula is $\theta_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum f_i} \times 360° = 3.6 \times P_i$.
- Always **verify** that $\sum \theta_i = 360°$ before drawing.
- Use pie charts **only for qualitative categorical data** with **≤ 6 categories**.
- For **numerical or ordinal data**, use **bar charts, histograms, or Pareto diagrams**.
- A well-drawn pie chart must include: **title, labels for each slice, percentages, and a legend**.
- Pie charts **cannot show trends over time** — use line charts instead.
- Pie charts **cannot handle negative values** — use bar charts instead.
- The **largest angle** corresponds to the **mode** (most frequent category) of categorical data.
- For multi-category comparison, **stacked bar charts** outperform pie charts.
- Always present **at least 2 quantitative inferences** in your exam answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scatter Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scatter Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Scatter Plots — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **scatter plot** (also called a **scatter graph**, **scatter chart**, or **scatter diagram**) is a two-dimensional graphical data visualization technique that displays the values of **two numerical variables** for a set of observations. Each observation is represented as a single point (marker) on a Cartesian plane, where:

- The **horizontal axis (x-axis)** represents the value of the **independent variable** (predictor, explanatory variable).
- The **vertical axis (y-axis)** represents the value of the **dependent variable** (response, outcome variable).
- Each point $(x_i, y_i)$ corresponds to the $i$-th paired observation from the bivariate dataset.

Scatter plots are the foundational exploratory tool in **bivariate analysis** under the KTU 2024 syllabus. They allow an analyst to qualitatively assess the **form**, **direction**, and **strength** of a relationship between two quantitative variables *before* fitting any statistical model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Scatter plots belong to the unit of **Data Visualization** in Module 1. They are explicitly listed as a prerequisite for understanding **correlation**, **regression**, and **outlier detection**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **constellation map** of the night sky. Each star has a fixed brightness and a fixed position. Now suppose the brightness of a star could predict its distance from Earth — plotting *brightness* on one axis and *distance* on another would immediately reveal a *pattern*: dim stars cluster far away, bright stars sit close. The constellation isn't drawn by us — it emerges naturally when the data is plotted.

Similarly, a scatter plot simply **lets the data reveal its own shape**. We do not connect the dots; we just observe whether the cloud of points resembles a rising line, a falling line, a curve, a cluster, or pure chaos.

**Key intuitive terms used in KTU textbooks:**

| Term | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Bivariate data** | Two measurements taken on the same subject (e.g., height & weight of a person) |
| **Trend** | The general drift of points — up, down, or flat |
| **Pattern** | The geometric shape formed by the cloud of points |
| **Outlier** | A point that sits far away from the rest of the cloud |
| **Cluster** | A visibly dense grouping of points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bivariate data** is the formal name for any dataset where each row contains two numerical measurements. A scatter plot is the **default visual idiom** for bivariate data — no other chart type is as direct.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Structural Components of a Scatter Plot

A proper scatter plot contains the following mandatory elements (commonly asked in KTU short-answer questions):

1. **Title** — describes what is being compared.
2. **X-axis label** with units (independent variable).
3. **Y-axis label** with units (dependent variable).
4. **Equal-scale gridlines** so visual distances are not distorted.
5. **One marker per observation** — *never aggregate or average* before plotting.
6. **Optional trendline overlay** — added only after visual inspection, never before.

## Types of Correlation Patterns (Visual Signatures)

When the cloud of points is examined, one of the following patterns emerges. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify these patterns from a given diagram.

### 1. Strong Positive Linear Correlation
- Points form a tight, upward-sloping band.
- As $x$ increases, $y$ increases *predictably*.
- Pearson's $r \approx +0.8$ to $+1.0$.

### 2. Moderate Positive Linear Correlation
- Points drift upward but with visible scatter.
- $r \approx +0.5$ to $+0.8$.

### 3. Strong Negative Linear Correlation
- Points form a tight, downward-sloping band.
- As $x$ increases, $y$ decreases *predictably*.
- $r \approx -0.8$ to $-1.0$.

### 4. No Correlation (Null Pattern)
- Points form a shapeless cloud — no trend visible.
- $r \approx 0$.

### 5. Curvilinear (Non-linear) Relationship
- Points trace a curve (parabola, exponential decay, etc.).
- Pearson's $r$ may be near zero, **misleading the analyst** — Spearman's rank correlation is preferred here.

### 6. Clustered Pattern
- Points form **two or more distinct blobs**, suggesting heterogeneous sub-populations.

### 7. Outliers Dominating
- A few extreme points distort the visual impression of the entire cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The golden rule of scatter plots:** *Correlation does not imply causation.* Even a perfectly straight scatter plot with $r = +1.0$ does **not** prove that $x$ *causes* $y$. KTU examiners may award partial marks for stating this caveat explicitly.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation | Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean of $X$ | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | $\bar{x}$ | Same units as $X$ |
| Mean of $Y$ | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n}$ | $\bar{y}$ | Same units as $Y$ |
| **Pearson's Correlation Coefficient** (deviation form) | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}$ | $r$ | $-1 \leq r \leq +1$ (dimensionless) |
| Pearson's $r$ (raw-score form) | $r = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n\sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}\right]\left[n\sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2}\right]}}$ | $r$ | $-1 \leq r \leq +1$ |
| Coefficient of Determination | $r^{2}$ | $r^{2}$ | $0 \leq r^{2} \leq 1$ (dimensionless) |
| Covariance (sample) | $\mathrm{cov}(X,Y) = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $s_{xy}$ | Product of $X$ and $Y$ units |
| Linear regression slope | $b = r \cdot \dfrac{s_y}{s_x}$ | $b$ | Units of $Y$ per unit of $X$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Always remember: **Pearson's $r$ is dimensionless**, but it is *not* a percentage. A value of $r = 0.6$ is **not** "60% correlation." Only $r^{2}$ (the coefficient of determination) carries a percentage-like interpretation (e.g., $r^{2} = 0.36$ means 36% of the variation in $Y$ is explained by $X$).

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Scatter plots are heavily used in:

- **Civil Engineering:** Plotting compressive strength of concrete vs. curing age.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Engine RPM vs. fuel consumption curves.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Voltage vs. current for verifying Ohm's Law on non-linear devices (diodes).
- **Computer Science (Data Science):** Feature-vs-target inspection before training a regression model.
- **Biostatistics:** Height vs. weight, blood pressure vs. age.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Pearson's Correlation Coefficient from First Principles

The **Pearson Product-Moment Correlation Coefficient** measures the *linear* association between two variables. We derive it from the conceptual notion of *co-variation*.

### Step 1 — Recognize the Need for a Scaled Measure

Raw covariance $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ is unbounded — its magnitude depends on the units of $X$ and $Y$. To obtain a unit-free measure, we *normalize* the covariance by the geometric mean of the individual variances.

### Step 2 — Write the Sample Variances

$$
s_x^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}
$$

$$
s_y^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^{2}
$$

**Logic:** These are the average squared deviations of $X$ and $Y$ around their respective means. They quantify the *spread* of each variable independently.

### Step 3 — Form the Covariance

$$
s_{xy} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})
$$

**Logic:** Each product $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ is *positive* when both deviations share the same sign (point lies in the upper-right or lower-left quadrant relative to the centroid) and *negative* when signs differ. Summing them gives a measure of how often $X$ and $Y$ move together.

### Step 4 — Normalize to Obtain Pearson's $r$

$$
r = \frac{s_{xy}}{\sqrt{s_x^{2} \cdot s_y^{2}}} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^{2}}}
$$

**Logic:** Dividing by $\sqrt{s_x^{2} \cdot s_y^{2}}$ removes units, forcing $-1 \leq r \leq +1$. By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**, the numerator can never exceed the denominator in absolute value, hence the bounded range.

### Step 5 — Convert to Computational (Raw-Score) Form

Using the algebraic identity $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = \sum x_i^{2} - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^{2}}{n}$, the deviation form simplifies to the computationally friendlier raw-score formula used in KTU numerical problems:

$$
r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}\right]\left[n \sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2}\right]}}
$$

## Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** Five students reported the number of hours they studied for an exam ($X$) and the marks they obtained ($Y$). Construct a scatter plot mentally and compute Pearson's $r$.

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|
| 2 | 10 |
| 4 | 20 |
| 6 | 35 |
| 8 | 40 |
| 10 | 50 |

### Step A — Compute the Five Sums

$$
\sum x_i = 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 = 30
$$

$$
\sum y_i = 10 + 20 + 35 + 40 + 50 = 155
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (2)(10) + (4)(20) + (6)(35) + (8)(40) + (10)(50) = 20 + 80 + 210 + 320 + 500 = 1130
$$

$$
\sum x_i^{2} = 4 + 16 + 36 + 64 + 100 = 220
$$

$$
\sum y_i^{2} = 100 + 400 + 1225 + 1600 + 2500 = 6825
$$

### Step B — Plug into the Raw-Score Formula (with $n = 5$)

$$
r = \frac{(5)(1130) - (30)(155)}{\sqrt{\left[(5)(220) - 30^{2}\right]\left[(5)(6825) - 155^{2}\right]}}
$$

**Logic:** Expand the numerator first to keep arithmetic clean.

### Step C — Evaluate the Numerator

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5650 - 4650 = 1000
$$

**Logic:** The numerator captures the *co-movement* between $X$ and $Y$. A positive value signals a positive linear trend.

### Step D — Evaluate the Denominator Components

$$
\text{Term}_X = 5(220) - 900 = 1100 - 900 = 200
$$

$$
\text{Term}_Y = 5(6825) - 24025 = 34125 - 24025 = 10100
$$

**Logic:** Each term is the *corrected sum of squares* (also written $SS_{xx}$ and $SS_{yy}$). They are the building blocks of variance.

### Step E — Compute the Denominator

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{(200)(10100)} = \sqrt{2{,}020{,}000} \approx 1421.27
$$

### Step F — Final Result

$$
r = \frac{1000}{1421.27} \approx 0.7036
$$

### Step G — Interpretation

Since $r \approx +0.70$, the data shows a **strong positive linear correlation** — students who studied more hours tended to obtain higher marks, as expected. The coefficient of determination is:

$$
r^{2} \approx 0.495
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **49.5%** of the variation in marks is explained by the variation in study hours.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Analytical Flow for Interpreting a Scatter Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Collect Bivariate Data] --> stepB[Choose Independent Variable X]
    stepB --> stepC[Choose Dependent Variable Y]
    stepC --> stepD[Plot Each Pair as One Point]
    stepD --> stepE{Identify Visual Pattern}
    stepE --> branchF[Linear Upward Trend]
    stepE --> branchG[Linear Downward Trend]
    stepE --> branchH[Curved Non Linear Shape]
    stepE --> branchI[Random Cloud No Pattern]
    stepE --> branchJ[Distinct Clusters or Outliers]
    branchF --> stepK[Compute Pearson r]
    branchG --> stepK
    branchH --> stepL[Use Spearman Rank r]
    branchI --> stepM[Conclude r near 0]
    branchJ --> stepN[Investigate Subgroups or Remove Outliers]
    stepK --> stepO{Interpret Strength}
    stepO --> stepP[r near 1 Strong Positive]
    stepO --> stepQ[r near 0 Weak or No Correlation]
    stepO --> stepR[r near minus 1 Strong Negative]
    stepL --> stepO
    stepM --> stepO
    stepN --> stepO
```

## Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Scatter Plot Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawData[(Raw Bivariate Dataset)] --> dataClean[Data Cleaning Module]
    dataClean --> axisAssign[Axis Assignment Engine]
    axisAssign --> plotEngine[Plotting Engine]
    plotEngine --> markerRender[Marker Renderer]
    markerRender --> axisLabeller[Axis and Title Labeller]
    axisLabeller --> trendOverlay{User Opts for Trendline?}
    trendOverlay --> yesPath[Overlay OLS Regression Line]
    trendOverlay --> noPath[Skip Trendline Overlay]
    yesPath --> finalChart[Final Scatter Plot]
    noPath --> finalChart
    finalChart --> statsCompute[Correlation Statistics r and r squared]
    statsCompute --> reportGen[Analytical Report]
```

## Diagram 3 — Visual Catalogue of Common Scatter Plot Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    catalogStart[Catalog of Patterns] --> patA[Pattern A: Strong Positive Linear]
    catalogStart --> patB[Pattern B: Strong Negative Linear]
    catalogStart --> patC[Pattern C: Weak Positive Scatter]
    catalogStart --> patD[Pattern D: Perfect Curvilinear U Shape]
    catalogStart --> patE[Pattern E: Random No Correlation]
    catalogStart --> patF[Pattern F: Two Distinct Clusters]
    catalogStart --> patG[Pattern G: Single Dominant Outlier]
    patA --> annotA[r between plus 0.8 and plus 1.0]
    patB --> annotB[r between minus 0.8 and minus 1.0]
    patC --> annotC[r between plus 0.3 and plus 0.6]
    patD --> annotD[r may mislead near zero use rank]
    patE --> annotE[r approximately 0]
    patF --> annotF[Investigate latent categorical variable]
    patG --> annotG[Check data entry error or rare event]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **functional/architectural** representations of the analytical reasoning a student should follow. In an actual KTU answer sheet, replace each block with a hand-drawn scatter plot illustrating that pattern (axis labels, cloud of points, and a short caption).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a scatter plot. Mention any two patterns that can be identified from it.

**Model Answer:**

A scatter plot is a two-dimensional graphical representation of bivariate data in which each observation is plotted as a single point using its $(x_i, y_i)$ coordinate pair on a Cartesian plane. It is used to study the relationship between two numerical variables.

Two commonly identified patterns are:

1. **Positive linear correlation** — points form an upward-sloping band; $y$ tends to increase as $x$ increases.
2. **Negative linear correlation** — points form a downward-sloping band; $y$ tends to decrease as $x$ increases.

*(Other acceptable patterns: no correlation, curvilinear, clustered, outlier-dominated.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State the formula for Pearson's correlation coefficient and explain the significance of its range.

**Model Answer:**

$$
r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}\right]\left[n \sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2}\right]}}
$$

**Significance of the range $-1 \leq r \leq +1$:**

- $r = +1$: perfect positive linear relationship.
- $r = -1$: perfect negative linear relationship.
- $r = 0$: no linear relationship (variables may still be non-linearly related).
- Intermediate values indicate the *strength* and *direction* of the linear association.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions offer **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the components of a scatter plot. With neat sketches, describe **any four** correlation patterns that can be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following table gives the advertising expenditure (in ₹ thousands) and the corresponding sales revenue (in ₹ lakhs) for 6 months of a startup. **(7 marks)**

| Month | Advertising $X$ | Sales $Y$ |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 5 | 12 |
| Feb | 8 | 18 |
| Mar | 10 | 22 |
| Apr | 12 | 25 |
| May | 15 | 30 |
| Jun | 18 | 36 |

Construct a scatter plot for the data and compute Pearson's correlation coefficient. Interpret the result.

#### Model Solution for (a)

A scatter plot contains:

1. **Title** describing the variables being compared.
2. **X-axis** for the independent variable with units.
3. **Y-axis** for the dependent variable with units.
4. **One marker per observation** at $(x_i, y_i)$.
5. **Equal grid spacing** so visual slopes are not distorted.
6. *(Optional)* a **trendline** added after visual inspection.

**Four correlation patterns:**

- **Positive Linear:** Points drift upward; $r > 0$.
- **Negative Linear:** Points drift downward; $r < 0$.
- **No Correlation:** Points form a shapeless cloud; $r \approx 0$.
- **Curvilinear:** Points trace a curve (parabola, exponential); Pearson's $r$ may be misleading.

*(Student should draw small sketches on the answer sheet — 2 marks reserved for diagrams.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the required sums ($n = 6$):**

$$
\sum x_i = 5 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 15 + 18 = 68
$$

$$
\sum y_i = 12 + 18 + 22 + 25 + 30 + 36 = 143
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (5)(12) + (8)(18) + (10)(22) + (12)(25) + (15)(30) + (18)(36)
$$

$$
= 60 + 144 + 220 + 300 + 450 + 648 = 1822
$$

$$
\sum x_i^{2} = 25 + 64 + 100 + 144 + 225 + 324 = 882
$$

$$
\sum y_i^{2} = 144 + 324 + 484 + 625 + 900 + 1296 = 3773
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$
n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i = 6(1822) - (68)(143) = 10932 - 9724 = 1208
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator components:**

$$
n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2} = 6(882) - 68^{2} = 5292 - 4624 = 668
$$

$$
n \sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2} = 6(3773) - 143^{2} = 22638 - 20449 = 2189
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
\sqrt{668 \times 2189} = \sqrt{1{,}462{,}252} \approx 1209.24
$$

**Step 5 — Pearson's $r$:**

$$
r = \frac{1208}{1209.24} \approx 0.999
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $r \approx +0.999$, there is a **near-perfect positive linear correlation** between advertising expenditure and sales revenue. Higher advertising spend is strongly associated with higher sales. The coefficient of determination is $r^{2} \approx 0.998$, meaning **99.8%** of the variation in sales is explained by advertising expenditure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **positive**, **negative**, and **zero** correlation. Why does a scatter plot with a perfect curve sometimes yield $r \approx 0$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the data given below, compute Pearson's correlation coefficient and interpret it. **(7 marks)**

| Observation | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 15 |
| 2 | 2 | 12 |
| 3 | 3 | 18 |
| 4 | 4 | 10 |
| 5 | 5 | 8 |
| 6 | 6 | 6 |
| 7 | 7 | 4 |

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Positive correlation:** $y$ tends to increase as $x$ increases; $r > 0$.

**Negative correlation:** $y$ tends to decrease as $x$ increases; $r < 0$.

**Zero correlation:** No discernible linear trend; $r \approx 0$.

**Why $r \approx 0$ on a perfect curve:**

Pearson's $r$ measures **only linear** association. For a perfect parabola $y = x^{2}$ centered at the mean of $x$, the positive deviations of $y$ at the extremes cancel out the negative deviation near the centre, producing $r \approx 0$ despite a deterministic relationship. This is why **Spearman's rank correlation** or visual inspection must supplement Pearson's $r$ for curvilinear data.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Sums ($n = 7$):**

$$
\sum x_i = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6 + 7 = 28
$$

$$
\sum y_i = 15 + 12 + 18 + 10 + 8 + 6 + 4 = 73
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (1)(15) + (2)(12) + (3)(18) + (4)(10) + (5)(8) + (6)(6) + (7)(4)
$$

$$
= 15 + 24 + 54 + 40 + 40 + 36 + 28 = 237
$$

$$
\sum x_i^{2} = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 = 140
$$

$$
\sum y_i^{2} = 225 + 144 + 324 + 100 + 64 + 36 + 16 = 909
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$
n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i = 7(237) - (28)(73) = 1659 - 2044 = -385
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator components:**

$$
n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2} = 7(140) - 28^{2} = 980 - 784 = 196
$$

$$
n \sum y_i^{2} - (\sum y_i)^{2} = 7(909) - 73^{2} = 6363 - 5329 = 1034
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
\sqrt{196 \times 1034} = \sqrt{202{,}664} \approx 450.18
$$

**Step 5 — Pearson's $r$:**

$$
r = \frac{-385}{450.18} \approx -0.855
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $r \approx -0.855$, there is a **strong negative linear correlation** between $x$ and $y$. As $x$ increases, $y$ decreases consistently. The coefficient of determination $r^{2} \approx 0.731$ indicates that **about 73.1%** of the variation in $y$ is explained by the linear relationship with $x$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract squared terms correctly:** Students frequently write $n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)$ instead of $n \sum x_i^{2} - (\sum x_i)^{2}$. This single missing exponent invalidates the entire denominator. *Loss: 3–4 marks.*
> 2. **Misinterpreting the sign:** A negative $r$ does not mean a "weak" relationship — it indicates *direction*. Always quote both **sign** and **magnitude** in interpretation.
> 3. **Confusing correlation with causation:** Stating "advertising causes higher sales" without qualifying wording (e.g., "is associated with") costs marks in long-answer questions.
> 4. **Skipping the scatter plot:** Even in computational problems, a quick hand-drawn scatter plot earns 1–2 marks and demonstrates understanding of the *visual* component.
> 5. **Reporting $r$ as a percentage:** Always state $r$ as a dimensionless number in $[-1, +1]$; report $r^{2}$ for any percentage-style interpretation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **scatter plot** is the standard visual tool for **bivariate numerical data**.
- Each **point** = one observation with its $(x_i, y_i)$ pair.
- Always plot the **independent variable on $X$** and the **dependent variable on $Y$**.
- Patterns to recognize: **positive linear**, **negative linear**, **no correlation**, **curvilinear**, **clustered**, **outlier-dominated**.
- **Pearson's $r$** quantifies the *strength and direction* of a **linear** relationship only.
- Range of $r$: **$-1 \leq r \leq +1$** — dimensionless, not a percentage.
- **$r^{2}$ (coefficient of determination)** is the percentage of variation in $Y$ explained by $X$.
- **Correlation $\neq$ causation** — always quote this caveat in exam answers.
- For **curvilinear** patterns, use **Spearman's rank correlation** in addition to (or instead of) Pearson's $r$.
- **Outliers** can dramatically distort both visual impression and numerical $r$ — investigate before reporting.
- For raw-score calculations, remember the identity: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = \sum x_i^{2} - \dfrac{(\sum x_i)^{2}}{n}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Line Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Line Chart** (also called a *line graph* or *trend chart*) is a fundamental two-dimensional graphical data visualization technique in which individual data points, representing ordered pairs $(x_i, y_i)$, are plotted on a Cartesian coordinate system and subsequently connected sequentially by straight line segments. Formally, a line chart is defined as a piecewise linear interpolation function $L(x)$ over a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$, where consecutive points $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$ are joined by a segment defined as:

$$L(x) = y_i + \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{x_{i+1} - x_i}(x - x_i), \quad x_i \leq x \leq x_{i+1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Line charts belong to the family of *univariate* and *bivariate* descriptive visualizations. They are classified under the **trend visualization** category because their primary purpose is to display the **rate of change** and **continuity** between observations. According to KTU's 2024 OBE framework (CO1: *Understand fundamental data visualization principles*), line charts serve as a baseline visualization for time-series and continuous-domain data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "ECG of Data"

Imagine a doctor connecting a patient to an **electrocardiogram (ECG)** machine. The machine does not merely record isolated heartbeats; it draws a continuous wiggly line on a scrolling paper. The **height** of the line at any moment tells the doctor how strong the electrical signal is, and the **shape** of the line over time tells the doctor whether the heart is healthy or in distress.

A line chart works in exactly the same way:
- The **horizontal axis (x-axis)** is the *timeline* (or any ordered independent variable).
- The **vertical axis (y-axis)** is the *measurement* (the dependent variable — temperature, revenue, voltage, population, etc.).
- The **line itself** is the *story* the data tells as it moves through time or space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A line chart is **NOT** the same as a function plot in pure mathematics. In mathematics, the curve $y = f(x)$ is a continuous analytical function. In a line chart, the connecting segments are merely a visual aid to show **sequential progression**; they do **not** necessarily imply that intermediate values exist or are meaningful. This subtle distinction is a frequently tested point in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Standard Components (KTU Terminology)

| Component | Formal Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal axis | **Abscissa (X-axis)** | Independent variable (time, category order) |
| Vertical axis | **Ordinate (Y-axis)** | Dependent variable (measurement) |
| Connecting segment | **Polyline** | Visualizes change between observations |
| Data markers | **Plotted points / Vertices** | Individual $(x_i, y_i)$ observations |
| Gridlines | **Reference lattice** | Aids value estimation |
| Tick marks | **Calibration points** | Discrete labelled values on axes |

## 1.4 When to Use a Line Chart (KTU Rule of Thumb)

A line chart is the **canonical choice** when **ALL** the following conditions are satisfied:

1. The independent variable is **continuous** or **ordinal** (time, distance, temperature gradient).
2. The number of data points is **moderately large** (typically $n \geq 5$); fewer points are better drawn as a scatter plot.
3. The goal is to communicate **trend, momentum, or rate of change**, not exact individual values.
4. There is a **single dependent variable** (or a small number of related series compared side-by-side).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using a line chart for **nominal categorical data** (e.g., "favorite fruit: apple, banana, cherry") is a fundamental visualization error. Nominal categories have no inherent order, and connecting them with lines implies a false continuity. Always check whether your x-axis variable is truly **ordinal or interval/ratio** before choosing a line chart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of a Line Chart

A line chart is governed by three primary analytical constructs that students must master for KTU examinations:

### 2.1.1 The Slope (Rate of Change)
The slope between any two consecutive data points $(x_i, y_i)$ and $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$ quantifies the **instantaneous rate of change** of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variable. Mathematically:

$$m_i = \frac{\Delta y_i}{\Delta x_i} = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{x_{i+1} - x_i}$$

The **sign** of $m_i$ tells the story:
- $m_i > 0$: Ascending trend (growth)
- $m_i < 0$: Descending trend (decline)
- $m_i = 0$: Flat (no change)

### 2.1.2 The Overall Trend (Regression Line Overlay)
To summarize the entire dataset with a single linear approximation, analysts overlay a **least-squares regression line**. Given $n$ data points, the slope $\beta_1$ and intercept $\beta_0$ of the best-fit line $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ are computed as:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\right)}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}, \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

### 2.1.3 The Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$)
To measure how well the trend line approximates the data, the $R^2$ metric is used:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

where $\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$ is the predicted value. An $R^2$ value close to **1.0** indicates that the line chart's underlying data is highly linear.

## 2.2 Variants of Line Charts (KTU Classified Taxonomy)

| Variant | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Line Chart** | Single data series plotted as one polyline | Monthly rainfall over a year |
| **Multi-Series Line Chart** | Multiple polylines on the same axes for comparison | Revenue of 3 companies over 5 years |
| **Stacked Area Chart** | Filled regions between lines, summed cumulatively | Energy consumption by source |
| **Step Line Chart** | Horizontal-then-vertical segments (no diagonal) | Discrete state changes (e.g., tax brackets) |
| **Smoothed Line Chart** | Spline or LOESS interpolation between points | Stock market price trends |
| **Dual-Axis Line Chart** | Two y-axes with different scales | Temperature vs. humidity over a day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A **step line chart** MUST be used (not a smooth line chart) when the underlying variable changes instantaneously and holds a constant value in between — for example, electricity tariff slabs, or the state of a digital logic gate. Connecting such points diagonally falsely implies intermediate values.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Comprehensive Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $L(x)$ | $y_i + m_i(x - x_i)$ | Piecewise linear interpolation between consecutive points |
| $m_i$ | $(y_{i+1} - y_i)/(x_{i+1} - x_i)$ | Local slope (segment-wise rate of change) |
| $\beta_1$ | $\frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | Slope of least-squares best-fit line |
| $\beta_0$ | $\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | Intercept of best-fit line |
| $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ | $\frac{1}{n}\sum x_i, \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i$ | Arithmetic means of $x$ and $y$ |
| $R^2$ | $1 - SS_{\text{res}}/SS_{\text{tot}}$ | Goodness-of-fit metric ($0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$) |
| $SS_{\text{res}}$ | $\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Residual sum of squares (unexplained variance) |
| $SS_{\text{tot}}$ | $\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Total sum of squares (total variance) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Tip for Tables:** Whenever the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or norm symbols appear inside a markdown table row, always use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x_i - \bar{x}\vert$) rather than the bare vertical pipe character `\vert`, because the bare pipe is interpreted by markdown as a column separator and will break the table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Line charts are ubiquitous across all engineering disciplines examined by KTU:

- **Civil Engineering:** Plotting stress–strain curves for concrete and steel under load.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Visualizing voltage transients, frequency response (Bode plots), and signal waveforms over time.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Tracking engine RPM, temperature profiles of heat exchangers, and vibration amplitudes.
- **Computer Science / Data Science:** Time-series forecasting of network traffic, CPU utilization, and user engagement metrics.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG, EEG, EMG signal displays; patient vital sign monitoring.

The line chart is therefore not merely a "chart" — it is a **universal diagnostic language of engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Least-Squares Slope $\beta_1$

The least-squares method finds the line $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ that **minimizes the sum of squared vertical residuals** $S(\beta_0, \beta_1)$:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\right)^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_0$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\right) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Simplify by distributing the negative sign and dividing by 2:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 3:** Divide through by $n$ to obtain the mean relationship:

$$\bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

**Step 4:** Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_1$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\left(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i\right) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Expand and substitute the expression for $\beta_0$ from Step 3:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \left(\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}\right)\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that $\bar{y} \sum x_i = (1/n)(\sum y_i)(\sum x_i)$ and similarly for $\bar{x}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right) - \frac{\beta_1}{n}\left(\sum x_i\right)^2 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

**Step 8:** Multiply through by $n$ to clear denominators:

$$n\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i = \left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right) - \beta_1\left(\sum x_i\right)^2 + \beta_1 \cdot n\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2$$

**Step 9:** Isolate $\beta_1$ on one side:

$$\beta_1\left[n\sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2\right] = n\sum x_i y_i - \left(\sum x_i\right)\left(\sum y_i\right)$$

**Step 10:** Divide to obtain the final closed-form expression:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{n\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\right)}{n\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^2}$$

This derivation is a **KTU high-priority question**, as it integrates calculus (partial differentiation) with descriptive statistics.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Constructing a Line Chart and Trend Line

**Problem Statement:** A Civil Engineering student records the deflection (in mm) of a simply-supported beam at 5 evenly spaced load increments (in kN). The data are:

| Load $x_i$ (kN) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deflection $y_i$ (mm) | 0.5 | 1.1 | 1.4 | 2.1 | 2.5 |

**Required:** (i) Compute the slope of the least-squares regression line. (ii) Compute the intercept. (iii) Predict the deflection at $x = 6$ kN.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the necessary sums with $n = 5$:

$$\sum x_i = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15$$
$$\sum y_i = 0.5 + 1.1 + 1.4 + 2.1 + 2.5 = 7.6$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 = 55$$
$$\sum x_i y_i = (1)(0.5) + (2)(1.1) + (3)(1.4) + (4)(2.1) + (5)(2.5)$$
$$\quad\quad\quad = 0.5 + 2.2 + 4.2 + 8.4 + 12.5 = 27.8$$

**Step 2:** Compute the means:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{15}{5} = 3.0 \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = \frac{7.6}{5} = 1.52$$

**Step 3:** Compute the slope $\beta_1$ using the closed-form formula:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{(5)(27.8) - (15)(7.6)}{(5)(55) - (15)^2} = \frac{139.0 - 114.0}{275 - 225} = \frac{25.0}{50.0} = 0.5$$

**Step 4:** Compute the intercept $\beta_0$:

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 1.52 - (0.5)(3.0) = 1.52 - 1.50 = 0.02$$

**Step 5:** Write the equation of the trend line:

$$\hat{y} = 0.02 + 0.5\,x$$

**Step 6:** Predict the deflection at $x = 6$ kN:

$$\hat{y}(6) = 0.02 + 0.5(6) = 0.02 + 3.00 = 3.02 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** Verification via dimensional sanity check — the slope has units $\text{mm/kN}$, and the intercept represents the (theoretical) deflection at zero load, which is consistent with engineering intuition (a small residual due to beam self-weight or measurement noise).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Slope Interpretation

| Slope Range | Trend Classification | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_1 \gg 0$ | Strong positive trend | Accelerated growth (e.g., compound interest, exponential wear) |
| $\beta_1 > 0$ (small) | Mild positive trend | Linear growth regime |
| $\beta_1 \approx 0$ | Stationary / Flat | Steady-state equilibrium |
| $\beta_1 < 0$ (small) | Mild negative trend | Linear decay (e.g., battery discharge) |
| $\beta_1 \ll 0$ | Strong negative trend | Catastrophic decline (structural failure, market crash) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** If asked *"Is the trend increasing or decreasing?"* in an exam, do **not** rely on visual inspection of a drawn chart (which may have distorted axes). Always compute $\beta_1$ numerically using the formula. The sign of $\beta_1$ is the mathematically rigorous answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Line Chart

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional pipeline** that a data analyst follows when constructing a line chart, including the underlying mathematical transformations that occur at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D equals x_i comma y_i] --> B[Step 1: Validate Data Type]
    B --> C{Is X ordinal or continuous?}
    C -- No --> D[Reject: Use Bar Chart instead]
    C -- Yes --> E[Step 2: Compute Descriptive Statistics]
    E --> F[Calculate Mean x_bar and y_bar]
    E --> G[Calculate Variance SS_tot]
    F --> H[Step 3: Fit Trend Line]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Slope beta_1]
    H --> J[Compute Intercept beta_0]
    I --> K[Step 4: Render Visual]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Plot Axes and Gridlines]
    K --> M[Plot Points and Connect Segments]
    K --> N[Overlay Regression Line]
    L --> O[Step 5: Evaluate Fit]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Compute R squared Metric]
    P --> Q{R squared greater than 0.8?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Fit Acceptable: Finalize Chart]
    Q -- No --> S[Apply Smoothing or Change Model]
    S --> K
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:** Each rectangular block represents a **processing stage**, while each diamond block represents a **decision point**. The arrows indicate the **flow of data and computation**. Note that the *rejection branch* (block D) embodies the validation rule that line charts must NEVER be used for nominal categorical data.

## 4.2 Structural Anatomy of a Single Line Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Title Block] --> B[Y axis Label and Units]
    B --> C[Plotting Region]
    C --> D[X axis Label and Units]
    D --> E[Legend for Multi Series]
    E --> F[Data Points as Markers]
    F --> G[Connecting Polyline Segments]
    G --> H[Optional Trend Line Overlay]
    H --> I[Gridlines and Tick Marks]
    I --> A
```

This second diagram illustrates the **anatomical components** of a single rendered line chart. Students should be able to identify and label every one of these elements when presented with a chart in a KTU question paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common KTU question presents a poorly-drawn chart and asks students to identify missing components (e.g., "Identify three elements missing from the following chart"). The above diagram is your master checklist. A complete chart must include a **title**, **axis labels with units**, **tick marks**, **data markers**, and a **legend** (for multi-series).

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: Line Chart vs. Other Trend Visualizations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Visualization Type] --> B{What is the data structure?}
    B -- Continuous over time --> C[Line Chart]
    B -- Discrete categories --> D[Bar Chart]
    B -- Part of whole --> E[Pie Chart]
    B -- Distribution of values --> F[Histogram or Box Plot]
    B -- Relationship between two variables --> G[Scatter Plot]
    C --> H{How many series?}
    H -- One --> I[Simple Line Chart]
    H -- Few --> J[Multi Series Line Chart]
    H -- Many --> K[Small Multiples or Heatmap]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a line chart. Under what conditions is a line chart the most appropriate visualization choice?**

**Model Answer:**
A line chart is a two-dimensional graphical representation in which individual data points $(x_i, y_i)$ are plotted on a Cartesian plane and connected sequentially by straight line segments to visualize the trend of a continuous variable over an ordered independent variable. It is most appropriate when (i) the independent variable is continuous or ordinal (typically time), (ii) the dataset has at least 5 data points to reveal a meaningful trend, (iii) the analytical goal is to communicate rate of change or momentum, and (iv) the dependent variable is a single measurable quantity. It is unsuitable for nominal categorical data because connecting such categories falsely implies continuity. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the four conditions)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a step line chart and a smooth line chart. Give one engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **step line chart** uses horizontal-then-vertical segments (no diagonal interpolation) to connect data points, preserving the fact that the variable holds a constant value until it changes instantaneously. A **smooth line chart** applies interpolation (linear or spline) between data points, which visually implies that intermediate values exist between observations. 

- *Engineering example of step chart:* Electricity tariff slabs — the rate per unit is constant within a slab and jumps at the boundary.
- *Engineering example of smooth chart:* Daily average temperature plotted over a month — intermediate temperatures genuinely exist between recorded days.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for differentiation + 1 mark for examples)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving Part B questions, students frequently lose marks by (1) forgetting to **state assumptions** about data type before plotting, (2) computing $\beta_1$ but omitting units in the final answer, and (3) failing to **interpret** the trend line — examiners expect a concluding sentence in plain English, not just a numerical answer. Always close your solution with: *"Therefore, the data exhibits a [trend] at a rate of [value] [units]."*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A Mechanical Engineering laboratory recorded the surface temperature (in °C) of a heated metal plate at 7 successive time intervals (in minutes). The data are:

| Time $x_i$ (min) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature $y_i$ (°C) | 30 | 35 | 42 | 48 | 53 | 61 | 68 |

Construct a **line chart description** (axes, scale, plotted points) and compute the **slope of the least-squares regression line**. Comment on whether a linear model is justified. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **intercept** of the regression line and the **predicted temperature at $t = 10$ minutes**. Also compute the **coefficient of determination $R^2$** to assess goodness of fit. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a clearly labelled **schematic block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture of a line chart**, identifying all five mandatory components. Explain why each component is necessary using an engineering example of your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Civil Engineering firm tracked the daily compressive strength gain (in MPa) of a concrete mix over 6 days. The data are $(x_i, y_i)$: $(1, 5), (2, 9), (3, 14), (4, 18), (5, 23), (6, 27)$. Compute the **equation of the best-fit line** using the least-squares method, and **predict the strength on day 8**. Justify whether the linear model is appropriate or whether a nonlinear model would be more suitable. **(7 marks)**

---

## Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Chart Description:**
- **X-axis:** Time (min), uniform scale from 0 to 8.
- **Y-axis:** Temperature (°C), uniform scale from 25 to 75.
- **Data points:** Plot $(1,30), (2,35), (3,42), (4,48), (5,53), (6,61), (7,68)$.
- **Polyline:** Connect consecutive points with straight segments.
- **Title:** "Temperature vs. Time for Heated Metal Plate."

**Step 2 — Compute sums with $n = 7$:**

$$\sum x_i = 1+2+3+4+5+6+7 = 28$$
$$\sum y_i = 30+35+42+48+53+61+68 = 337$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 1+4+9+16+25+36+49 = 140$$
$$\sum x_i y_i = 30+70+126+192+265+366+476 = 1525$$

**Step 3 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{x} = 28/7 = 4 \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = 337/7 \approx 48.14$$

**Step 4 — Compute slope $\beta_1$:**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{(7)(1525) - (28)(337)}{(7)(140) - (28)^2} = \frac{10675 - 9436}{980 - 784} = \frac{1239}{196} \approx 6.32 \text{ °C/min}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** The slope is positive and approximately constant, suggesting a **linear heating regime**. The data follows a near-arithmetic progression (increments of roughly 5, 7, 6, 5, 8, 7), which is consistent with linear behavior. *(7 marks: 2 for chart description + 3 for computation + 2 for interpretation)*

---

### Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute intercept $\beta_0$:**

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 48.14 - (6.32)(4) = 48.14 - 25.28 \approx 22.86 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Best-fit line equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 22.86 + 6.32\,x \quad \text{(units: °C, with } x \text{ in min)}$$

**Step 3 — Predicted temperature at $t = 10$ min:**

$$\hat{y}(10) = 22.86 + 6.32(10) = 22.86 + 63.20 = 86.06 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R^2$:**

Compute predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ and residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$:

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 29.18 | 0.67 |
| 2 | 35 | 35.50 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 42 | 41.82 | 0.03 |
| 4 | 48 | 48.14 | 0.02 |
| 5 | 53 | 54.46 | 2.13 |
| 6 | 61 | 60.78 | 0.05 |
| 7 | 68 | 67.10 | 0.81 |

$$SS_{\text{res}} = \sum e_i^2 \approx 3.96$$
$$SS_{\text{tot}} = \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = (30-48.14)^2 + \cdots + (68-48.14)^2 \approx 1011.43$$
$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{3.96}{1011.43} \approx 0.996$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** $R^2 \approx 0.996$ is extremely close to 1, confirming that the **linear model is an excellent fit**. The metal plate heats at approximately **6.32 °C per minute** during the observed interval. *(7 marks: 1 intercept + 1 prediction + 3 for $R^2$ + 2 for interpretation)*

---

### Solution to Question B(a)

**Block Diagram (as described in Section 4.1 above):** The five mandatory components are:

1. **Title block** — e.g., "Engine RPM vs. Throttle Position." *Necessary because:* Without a title, the viewer cannot identify the engineering scenario; this is a common audit-failure point in industrial reports.
2. **X-axis label with units** — e.g., "Throttle Opening (%)." *Necessary because:* Engineering measurements are meaningless without units (a 25 could be degrees, percent, or newtons).
3. **Y-axis label with units** — e.g., "Engine RPM." *Necessary because:* Same reason as above.
4. **Data markers and connecting polyline** — the actual data. *Necessary because:* These are the empirical observations being communicated.
5. **Legend (for multi-series)** — to distinguish between multiple engines or test conditions. *Necessary because:* Without a legend, overlapping polylines become uninterpretable.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram + 4 for engineering justification of each component)*

---

### Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute sums with $n = 6$:**

$$\sum x_i = 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21$$
$$\sum y_i = 5+9+14+18+23+27 = 96$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 1+4+9+16+25+36 = 91$$
$$\sum x_i y_i = 5+18+42+72+115+162 = 414$$

**Step 2 — Compute slope $\beta_1$:**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{(6)(414) - (21)(96)}{(6)(91) - (21)^2} = \frac{2484 - 2016}{546 - 441} = \frac{468}{105} \approx 4.457 \text{ MPa/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept $\beta_0$:**

$$\bar{x} = 21/6 = 3.5 \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = 96/6 = 16$$
$$\beta_0 = 16 - (4.457)(3.5) = 16 - 15.60 \approx 0.40 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Equation of the best-fit line:**

$$\hat{y} = 0.40 + 4.457\,x$$

**Step 5 — Prediction at day 8:**

$$\hat{y}(8) = 0.40 + 4.457(8) = 0.40 + 35.66 \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Justification of model choice:**
Examine the day-to-day increments: $9-5=4$, $14-9=5$, $18-14=4$, $23-18=5$, $27-23=4$. The increments are remarkably **constant at ~4–5 MPa/day**, strongly supporting a linear model. A nonlinear model (e.g., logarithmic or square-root) is **not justified** for this dataset, though such models become appropriate for long-term curing behavior beyond 28 days where strength asymptotically approaches a maximum. *(7 marks: 3 for slope + intercept + equation, 2 for prediction, 2 for model justification)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Line Charts (KTU Module 1)**

- **Definition:** A line chart is a piecewise-linear visualization connecting sequential data points $(x_i, y_i)$ on a 2D Cartesian plane.
- **Use it when:** X is continuous/ordinal, $n \geq 5$, goal is trend communication.
- **Do NOT use it when:** X is nominal categorical (use a bar chart instead).
- **Five mandatory components:** Title, X-axis label with units, Y-axis label with units, data markers, legend (if multi-series).
- **Slope formula:** $m_i = (y_{i+1} - y_i)/(x_{i+1} - x_i)$ — segment-wise; signs indicate trend direction.
- **Least-squares slope formula:** $\beta_1 = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$.
- **Intercept formula:** $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$.
- **Goodness of fit:** $R^2 = 1 - SS_{\text{res}}/SS_{\text{tot}}$, ranges from 0 to 1; values above 0.8 indicate a strong linear relationship.
- **Variants to remember:** Simple, multi-series, stacked area, step, smoothed, dual-axis — each has a specific use case.
- **Step chart vs. smooth chart:** Step for instantaneous discrete changes; smooth for genuinely continuous data.
- **Always include units** in slope ($\text{mm/kN}$, $\text{°C/min}$, $\text{MPa/day}$) — losing units = losing marks.
- **Interpretation is mandatory:** After computing $\beta_1$, write one concluding sentence in plain English explaining what the trend means in the engineering context.
- **Pitfall:** Connecting nominal categories with a line falsely implies continuity and is a frequent visualization error penalized by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bubble Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bubble Plot (A.K.A. Bubble Chart)** — A two-dimensional graphical data visualization technique in which each data marker is represented by a **circle (bubble)** whose **horizontal position (x-coordinate)**, **vertical position (y-coordinate)**, and **area (bubble size)** collectively encode **three distinct numerical variables** of a dataset. An optional fourth variable can be encoded through the **fill color** of the bubble.

In the formal KTU 2024 scheme vocabulary for *Introduction to Data Analysis*, a bubble plot is classified as a **multivariate extension of the scatter plot** used during the **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** phase. It belongs to the family of **bivariate/multivariate relational plots** that allow an analyst to spot correlations, clusters, and outliers in three (or four) dimensions simultaneously.

Let the underlying dataset consist of $n$ observations, each with three measured attributes:

$$
D = \{(x_i, \, y_i, \, z_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, n\}
$$

where:
- $x_i$ → mapped to the **horizontal axis** (independent variable on X-axis)
- $y_i$ → mapped to the **vertical axis** (dependent or second independent variable on Y-axis)
- $z_i$ → mapped to the **area of the bubble** (third quantitative variable)

The plotting engine then renders each triple $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ as a single disk centered at $(x_i, y_i)$ with an area $A_i$ that is a monotonic function of $z_i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Marketplace of Cities**

Imagine you are standing on top of a giant map of the world, and you want to compare major cities using three pieces of information at a single glance:

1. **Average annual rainfall** (X-axis, in cm)
2. **Average annual temperature** (Y-axis, in °C)
3. **Population** (encoded as the size of the bubble)

Instead of writing tiny numbers next to each city, you **pin a balloon over each city**. The *horizontal position* of the balloon's string tells you the rainfall, the *vertical position* tells you the temperature, and the *size of the balloon* tells you the population. A quick glance immediately reveals that "large balloon" cities (highly populated) cluster in *warm, low-rainfall* zones, while "tiny balloon" cities (sparsely populated) are scattered in cold, high-rainfall zones.

This is precisely what a **Bubble Plot** does — it is a "**three-in-one**" scatter plot where the **third dimension rides inside the marker itself**.

## 1.3 Key Properties of a Bubble Plot

| Property | Encoded Variable | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| X-axis position | Variable 1 ($x_i$) | Continuous numerical |
| Y-axis position | Variable 2 ($y_i$) | Continuous numerical |
| Bubble area | Variable 3 ($z_i$) | Continuous numerical (positive) |
| Bubble color (optional) | Variable 4 (categorical or continuous) | Qualitative palette |
| Bubble transparency (optional) | Uncertainty or weight | $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 PECHT416 syllabus (Module 1 – *Introduction to Data Analysis*), bubble plots are introduced under the unit "**Graphical Techniques for Data Visualization**" as a direct application of **multivariate EDA**.

## 1.4 Why Bubble Plots Are Used (Engineering Motivation)

- **Quick Pattern Discovery:** Engineers can visually detect **correlation trends** (positive, negative, or no correlation) in a single chart rather than scanning multiple scatter plots.
- **Resource Allocation Visualisation:** Useful for plotting **budget vs. impact vs. team size** in project portfolios.
- **Demographic and Geographic Studies:** Plotting **latitude vs. longitude vs. population** of cities.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing:** Plotting **temperature vs. pressure vs. defect rate** of a process.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Bubble Plot Construction

The construction of a bubble plot from raw data follows a structured, deterministic pipeline:

1. **Step 1 — Variable Selection:** The analyst selects **three (or four)** numerical variables from the dataset $D$.
2. **Step 2 — Axis Assignment:** Two variables are assigned to the X and Y axes; the third is reserved for bubble area.
3. **Step 3 — Size Normalisation:** Raw $z_i$ values are normalised to a plotting-friendly range (typically $[s_{\min}, s_{\max}] = [2, 50]$ in points) to prevent giant or invisible bubbles.
4. **Step 4 — Radius Computation:** The pixel radius of the $i$-th bubble is calculated using a scaling rule (see Section 2.2).
5. **Step 5 — Plotting:** A circle (filled, semi-transparent) is rendered at $(x_i, y_i)$ with the computed radius.
6. **Step 6 — Encoding Enhancement:** Optional color, transparency, and border are added to encode additional attributes.

## 2.2 The Critical Scaling Rule — Area vs. Radius

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Student Error:** Many students incorrectly assume that the **radius** of the bubble should be directly proportional to the data value $z_i$. This is **perceptually wrong** because the human eye perceives the **area**, not the radius, of a circle.

The **perceptually accurate** and academically endorsed rule is:

$$
A_i \;=\; k \cdot z_i
$$

where $A_i$ is the area of the $i$-th bubble and $k$ is a positive scaling constant. Using $A = \pi r^2$, the **radius scaling formula** becomes:

$$
r_i \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot z_i}{\pi}}
$$

## 2.3 Normalised Radius Equation (For Practical Use)

In most statistical software, the raw $z_i$ values are first **min-max normalised** to the interval $[0, 1]$:

$$
z_i^{\text{norm}} \;=\; \dfrac{z_i - z_{\min}}{z_{\max} - z_{\min}}
$$

Then the actual pixel radius is computed as:

$$
r_i \;=\; r_{\min} + z_i^{\text{norm}} \cdot (r_{\max} - r_{\min})
$$

Typical values are $r_{\min} = 3$ pixels and $r_{\max} = 50$ pixels.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dataset representation | $D = \{(x_i, y_i, z_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ | Three-variable data triple |
| 2 | Perceptually correct area scaling | $A_i = k \cdot z_i$ | Area proportional to data value |
| 3 | Radius from area (perceptual) | $r_i = \sqrt{k \cdot z_i / \pi}$ | Square-root scaling |
| 4 | Min-Max normalisation | $z_i^{\text{norm}} = (z_i - z_{\min}) / (z_{\max} - z_{\min})$ | Normalises $z_i \in [0, 1]$ |
| 5 | Pixel radius mapping | $r_i = r_{\min} + z_i^{\text{norm}} \cdot (r_{\max} - r_{\min})$ | Maps normalised to pixels |
| 6 | Standard scaling (alternative) | $z_i^{std} = (z_i - \mu_z) / \sigma_z$ | Z-score normalisation |
| 7 | Color hue mapping (4th variable) | $H_i = 360 \cdot (c_i - c_{\min}) / (c_{\max} - c_{\min})$ | Maps color to circular hue |
| 8 | Total chart visual area (approx) | $A_{\text{total}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \pi r_i^2$ | Sum of bubble areas |
| 9 | Visual density indicator | $\rho = A_{\text{total}} / (W \cdot H)$ | Total bubble area over plot area |
| 10 | Log scaling (for skewed $z_i$) | $r_i = \sqrt{k \cdot \log(1 + z_i) / \pi}$ | Handles heavy-tailed data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam — "A-R-S-Q"**: **A**rea scales with data → **R**adius scales with **S**quare **R**oot of data. The square root operation is the "magic" that makes bubble plots perceptually honest.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Bubble Plots

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Plotting bridge span vs. cost vs. traffic load |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Plotting RPM vs. temperature vs. vibration amplitude |
| **Computer Science** | Visualising algorithm performance: input size vs. execution time vs. memory |
| **Economics** | GDP per capita vs. life expectancy vs. population of countries |
| **Healthcare / Bio-statistics** | Age vs. blood pressure vs. cholesterol level of patients |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the Radius Uses a Square Root

**Goal:** Show that for the human visual system to perceive a bubble's "magnitude" linearly with the data value $z_i$, the radius must scale as $\sqrt{z_i}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the perceptual hypothesis:**

The human eye estimates the magnitude of a circular object by its **projected area**, not by its linear dimension. Therefore, the perceived magnitude $M_i$ of the $i$-th bubble satisfies:

$$
M_i \;\propto\; A_i \;=\; \pi r_i^2
$$

**Step 2 — Impose the design condition:**

For a faithful data visualisation, the perceived magnitude must be **directly proportional** to the encoded data value $z_i$:

$$
M_i \;\propto\; z_i
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two proportionalities:**

$$
\pi r_i^2 \;\propto\; z_i
$$

$$
\pi r_i^2 \;=\; k \cdot z_i
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the radius:**

$$
r_i^2 \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot z_i}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,r_i \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot z_i}{\pi}}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The **square-root** function is the natural bridge between data values (perceived by area) and pixel coordinates (drawn by radius).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Min-Max Normalisation of Bubble Sizes

**Problem:** A dataset has bubble-size values $z_i$ ranging from $z_{\min} = 100$ to $z_{\max} = 10{,}000$. We want all bubbles to have pixel radii between $r_{\min} = 4$ and $r_{\max} = 40$ pixels.

**Step 1 — Write the linear mapping:**

$$
r_i \;=\; r_{\min} + \left( \dfrac{z_i - z_{\min}}{z_{\max} - z_{\min}} \right) \cdot (r_{\max} - r_{\min})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical bounds:**

$$
r_i \;=\; 4 + \left( \dfrac{z_i - 100}{10{,}000 - 100} \right) \cdot (40 - 4)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
r_i \;=\; 4 + \left( \dfrac{z_i - 100}{9900} \right) \cdot 36
$$

$$
r_i \;=\; 4 + \dfrac{36}{9900} (z_i - 100)
$$

$$
r_i \;=\; 4 + 0.003636 \cdot (z_i - 100)
$$

**Step 4 — Verify boundary conditions:**

- When $z_i = 100$: $r_i = 4 + 0 = 4$ pixels ✓
- When $z_i = 10{,}000$: $r_i = 4 + 0.003636 \cdot 9900 = 4 + 36 = 40$ pixels ✓

$$
\boxed{\,r_i \;=\; 4 + 0.003636 \cdot (z_i - 100)\,}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Three-City Bubble Plot

Consider three cities with the following data:

| City | Rainfall (cm) | Temperature (°C) | Population (millions) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 50 | 25 | 1 |
| B | 100 | 15 | 4 |
| C | 75 | 20 | 9 |

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**
- $x_i$ = Rainfall (X-axis)
- $y_i$ = Temperature (Y-axis)
- $z_i$ = Population (bubble area)

**Step 2 — Apply min-max normalisation on $z_i$:**

- $z_{\min} = 1$, $z_{\max} = 9$
- $z_A^{\text{norm}} = (1 - 1) / 8 = 0$
- $z_B^{\text{norm}} = (4 - 1) / 8 = 3/8 = 0.375$
- $z_C^{\text{norm}} = (9 - 1) / 8 = 1$

**Step 3 — Compute pixel radii (using $r_{\min} = 4$, $r_{\max} = 40$):**

- $r_A = 4 + 0 \cdot 36 = 4$ pixels (smallest bubble)
- $r_B = 4 + 0.375 \cdot 36 = 4 + 13.5 = 17.5$ pixels
- $r_C = 4 + 1 \cdot 36 = 40$ pixels (largest bubble)

**Step 4 — Plot the bubbles:**

- City A: small bubble at $(50, 25)$
- City B: medium bubble at $(100, 15)$
- City C: large bubble at $(75, 20)$

The chart immediately reveals that **City C (the largest) sits in a moderate climate**, while **City A (the smallest) has the warmest, driest conditions** — an insight that would require reading three separate tables to extract.

## 3.4 Analytical Interpretation Matrix

| Observation | What to Look For | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Upward cluster of large bubbles | Positive correlation with magnitude | "Bigger is better" pattern |
| Diagonal trail of bubbles | Strong linear correlation | Predictive relationship |
| Random scatter of mixed sizes | No correlation | Variables are independent |
| One giant isolated bubble | Outlier | Special investigation needed |
| Many overlapping small bubbles | Data concentration | Mode / hot-spot region |
| Two distinct clusters of bubbles | Bimodal distribution | Two sub-populations exist |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a Bubble Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D with 3 variables] --> B[Variable Selection]
    B --> C1[X-axis variable x_i]
    B --> C2[Y-axis variable y_i]
    B --> C3[Size variable z_i]
    B --> C4[Optional color variable c_i]
    C3 --> D[Min-Max Normalisation]
    D --> E[Compute Pixel Radius r_i]
    C1 --> F[Plot Centre Coordinates]
    C2 --> F
    E --> G[Render Bubble at x_i y_i]
    C4 --> H[Apply Color Hue]
    F --> G
    H --> G
    G --> I[Final Bubble Plot]
    I --> J1[Pattern Detection]
    I --> J2[Outlier Identification]
    I --> J3[Cluster Recognition]
    I --> J4[Correlation Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — When to Use a Bubble Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Visualise Data] --> Q1{How many numerical variables?}
    Q1 -->|1 variable| P1[Use Histogram or Box Plot]
    Q1 -->|2 variables| P2[Use Scatter Plot]
    Q1 -->|3 variables| P3[Use Bubble Plot]
    Q1 -->|4 or more| P4[Use Bubble Plot with color OR Parallel Coordinates]
    Q3{Is the 3rd variable always positive?}
    P3 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| P5[Bubble Plot is suitable]
    Q3 -->|No, can be negative| P6[Use Treemap or consider transformation]
    Q4{Is the dataset size small or moderate?}
    P5 --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Less than 500 points| P7[Bubble Plot works well]
    Q4 -->|More than 1000 points| P8[Consider 2D Density Plot instead]
```

## 4.3 Anatomy of a Single Bubble (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    block:ID1["Bubble Anatomy"]
        A["Centre Coordinates (x_i, y_i) -- encodes Var1 and Var2"]
        B["Area of Disk -- encodes Var3"]
        C["Fill Color -- optional Var4"]
        D["Border Thickness -- optional uncertainty weight"]
        E["Transparency alpha -- optional density cue"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Information Flow from Data to Insight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Tabular Data] -->|Extract Triples| D2[x_i, y_i, z_i]
    D2 -->|Normalise| D3[z_i norm]
    D3 -->|Square Root| D4[Radius r_i]
    D4 -->|Render| D5[Visual Bubble]
    D5 -->|Perceive| D6[Analyst Eye]
    D6 -->|Cognitively Decode| D7[Insight]
    D7 -->|Act On| D8[Engineering Decision]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *"What is a Bubble Plot? State any two situations where a Bubble Plot is more useful than a conventional Scatter Plot."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Bubble Plot is a multivariate graphical technique that displays three numerical variables of a dataset simultaneously. Two variables are represented by the **X and Y coordinates** of a circle, while the third variable is represented by the **area of the circle (bubble)**.

**Two situations where it is superior to a scatter plot:**

1. **Three-variable exploration:** When the analyst must study the joint behaviour of **three continuous variables** (e.g., population vs. income vs. life expectancy across countries), a scatter plot cannot show the third dimension, whereas a bubble plot encodes it inside the marker itself.

2. **Magnitude-weighted correlation analysis:** When the importance of each data point is tied to a "weight" variable (e.g., city population when studying rainfall vs. temperature), a bubble plot conveys both the position and the magnitude in a single glance, allowing the analyst to spot "big-player" trends.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *"Explain the importance of using the area (and not the radius) of a bubble for encoding the third variable. Justify with a formula."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The human visual system perceives the **area** of a circle, not its radius. Therefore, if the data value $z_i$ is mapped to the **area** $A_i$, the perceived magnitude will be linearly proportional to the data:

$$
A_i \;=\; k \cdot z_i
$$

Using $A_i = \pi r_i^2$, the corresponding radius is:

$$
r_i \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot z_i}{\pi}}
$$

If the radius were used directly ($r_i \propto z_i$), the **area would grow quadratically** ($A_i \propto z_i^2$), causing an over-emphasis of large values and a misleading chart. Hence, the area-proportional (square-root-radius) rule ensures **perceptual honesty**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 *(Choose any ONE)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**
>
> **a)** Define a Bubble Plot. With the help of a clearly labelled diagram, describe the four data attributes that can be encoded in a Bubble Plot. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **b)** The following data represents five industrial zones with their pollutant concentration (ppm), average wind speed (km/h), and area covered (km²). Construct a min-max normalised Bubble Plot description (table of pixel radii) using $r_{\min} = 5$ and $r_{\max} = 35$ pixels. Comment on the chart. **(7 Marks)**

| Zone | Concentration (ppm) | Wind Speed (km/h) | Area (km²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z1 | 30 | 12 | 5 |
| Z2 | 50 | 8 | 25 |
| Z3 | 70 | 15 | 15 |
| Z4 | 45 | 20 | 40 |
| Z5 | 60 | 10 | 10 |

**Model Solution for (a):**

A Bubble Plot is a two-dimensional visualisation that encodes **three (or four) variables** using the position, size, and color of circular markers.

**Four encoded attributes:**

1. **X-coordinate of bubble centre** → Variable 1 (e.g., wind speed)
2. **Y-coordinate of bubble centre** → Variable 2 (e.g., concentration)
3. **Area of bubble** → Variable 3 (e.g., zone area)
4. **Fill color of bubble** → Variable 4 (e.g., categorical risk level)

**Diagram description (textual):**
