# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Power-Error Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete flow of hypothesis testing, the four outcomes, and how Power emerges as the correct-detection probability.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Hypothesis Test Setup"] --> B["H0 is TRUE\nPopulation parameter = mu0"]
    A --> C["H0 is FALSE\nPopulation parameter = mu1 != mu0"]

    B --> B1["Do Not Reject H0\nCorrect Decision\nProbability = 1 - alpha"]
    B --> B2["Reject H0\nType I Error\nProbability = alpha\nFALSE POSITIVE"]

    C --> C1["Do Not Reject H0\nType II Error\nProbability = beta\nFALSE NEGATIVE"]
    C --> C2["Reject H0\nCorrect Decision\nProbability = 1 - beta\nTHIS IS POWER"]

    B1 --> D["End: Test Conclusion"]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D

    D --> E{"Power >= 0.80?"}
    E -- "YES" --> F["Test is Adequate\nSample size sufficient"]
    E -- "NO" --> G["Increase n\nOR increase alpha\nOR use larger effect"]
    G --> H["Recompute Power"]
    H --> E

    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style B2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **green-highlighted block (C2)** represents Power — the desirable outcome. The **pink blocks** represent the two error types. The feedback loop at the bottom shows how an under-powered test prompts corrective action.

---

## 4.2 Power Curve — Visual Schematic

The following Mermaid conceptual diagram shows the two distributions (under $H_0$ and under $H_1$), the rejection region, $\alpha$, $\beta$, and Power as geometric areas.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NULL["Under H0: Bar X ~ N mu0, sigma_per_sqrt_n"]
        N1["Left Tail Area = alpha/2\n(Left Rejection Region)"]
        N2["Center Area = 1 - alpha\n(Fail-to-Reject Region)"]
        N3["Right Tail Area = alpha/2\n(Right Rejection Region)"]
    end

    subgraph ALT["Under H1: Bar X ~ N mu1, sigma_per_sqrt_n"]
        A1["Power = 1 - beta"]
        A2["Overlap Area = beta\nTYPE II ERROR"]
    end

    N1 --- N2 --- N3
    A1 --- A2

    N3 -. "Same region, different distribution" .-> A1
    N2 -. "Same region, different distribution" .-> A2

    style N1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style N3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style A2 fill:#FFA500
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Diagram:** The **rejection region is fixed** once $\alpha$ is chosen. The **alternative distribution** slides along the x-axis based on the true effect size $\delta\sqrt{n}/\sigma$. As $n$ grows, the alternative distribution moves further right, **shrinking $\beta$ and expanding Power**.

---

## 4.3 Factor-Influence Architecture

The following diagram maps the four pillars governing Power and their directional influence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["POWER OF HYPOTHESIS TEST\n1 minus beta"] --> F1["Significance Level alpha"]
    P --> F2["Sample Size n"]
    P --> F3["Effect Size delta"]
    P --> F4["Population Std Dev sigma"]

    F1 -- "alpha UP\nPower UP" --> R1["Trade-off:\nMore Type I Errors"]
    F2 -- "n UP\nPower UP" --> R2["Cost:\nLarger experiment"]
    F3 -- "delta UP\nPower UP" --> R3["Reality:\nFixed by nature"]
    F4 -- "sigma UP\nPower DOWN" --> R4["Noise:\nHarder to detect"]

    R1 --> A["Compute n for\nPower = 0.80"]
    R2 --> A
    R3 --> A
    R4 --> A

    A --> OUT["A-priori Sample Size Determination"]

    style P fill:#4682B4,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px
    style OUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** In practice, the only factor a researcher fully controls is **$n$**. Power analysis is essentially the **inverse problem** — find the minimum $n$ that achieves a desired power given $\alpha$, $\delta$, and $\sigma$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the Power of a Hypothesis Test. How is it related to Type II Error?**

**Model Answer:**

The **power of a hypothesis test** is defined as the probability of **correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis** when the alternative hypothesis is true.

$$\text{Power} = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

**Relation to Type II Error ($\beta$):**
Type II Error $\beta$ is the probability of *failing to reject* a false $H_0$. Since the sum of probabilities for a fixed alternative is unity:

$$\text{Power} + \beta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip (3 marks):** A complete answer must include: (1) definition, (2) formula, (3) relation to $\beta$, and (4) the standard benchmark value of 0.80.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List and explain any three factors that affect the Power of a Hypothesis Test.**

**Model Answer:**

The four factors affecting Power are:

1. **Significance Level ($\alpha$):** Increasing $\alpha$ expands the rejection region, increasing Power but at the cost of higher Type I error rates.

2. **Sample Size ($n$):** Larger samples reduce the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, making it easier to detect a true effect, thus increasing Power.

3. **Effect Size ($\delta$):** The true magnitude of the difference between the hypothesized and actual parameter. Larger effects are easier to detect, yielding higher Power.

4. **Population Variability ($\sigma$):** Greater variability introduces noise, making detection harder, thus reducing Power.

> [!NOTE]
> Any three of the four are acceptable. Always state the **direction** of influence (increases/decreases) for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the Power of a **one-tailed (right-tailed) one-sample Z-test** of $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ versus $H_1: \mu = \mu_1 > \mu_0$, when $\sigma$ is known. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A company claims the average weight of a cereal box is **500 g**. Past data shows $\sigma = 10$ g. A quality inspector suspects the true mean is now $\mu_1 = 495$ g. If a sample of $n = 64$ boxes is tested at $\alpha = 0.05$ (right-tailed), compute the **Power of the test**. If the desired power is 0.90, find the **minimum sample size** required. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Setup:** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma$ known.

- $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ ; $H_1: \mu = \mu_1 > \mu_0$
- Test statistic: $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$
- Rejection rule: Reject $H_0$ if $Z > Z_{\alpha}$

**Step 1 — Critical value of $\bar{X}$:**

Rejection region: $\dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} > Z_{\alpha}$

$$\bar{X} > \mu_0 + Z_{\alpha} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \equiv c$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta$ (Type II error):**

$$\beta = P(\bar{X} \leq c \mid \mu = \mu_1)$$

Standardizing using the alternative distribution $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma^2/n)$:

$$\beta = P\!\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq \frac{c - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right)$$

Substituting $c$:

$$\frac{c - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{\mu_0 - \mu_1}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} + Z_{\alpha} = -\frac{(\mu_1 - \mu_0)\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} + Z_{\alpha}$$

Define effect size: $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0 > 0$.

$$\beta = \Phi\!\left(Z_{\alpha} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Power:**

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - \Phi\!\left(Z_{\alpha} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} - Z_{\alpha}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $\mu_0 = 500$, $\mu_1 = 495$, $\sigma = 10$, $n = 64$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (right-tailed)

**Step 1 — Compute effect size and standard error:**

$$\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0 = 495 - 500 = -5 \text{ g}$$

$$\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

$$\frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} = \frac{-5 \times 8}{10} = -4.0$$

**Step 2 — Critical Z-value:**

$$Z_{\alpha} = Z_{0.05} = 1.645$$

**Step 3 — Compute Power:**

$$\text{Power} = \Phi\!\left(-4.0 - 1.645\right) = \Phi(-5.645) \approx 0.0000 \approx 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Insight:** Power is virtually zero! The sample size $n=64$ is grossly inadequate to detect a 5g drop. The variance dominates the small effect.

**Step 4 — Sample Size for Power = 0.90:**

For power = 0.90, we need $\beta = 0.10$, so $Z_{\beta} = Z_{0.10} = 1.282$.

The KTU sample size formula for one-tailed test:

$$n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha} + Z_{\beta}}{\delta / \sigma}\right)^{2}$$

Substituting $\delta/\sigma = -5/10 = -0.5$ (use absolute value: $0.5$):

$$n = \left(\frac{1.645 + 1.282}{0.5}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2.927}{0.5}\right)^{2} = (5.854)^{2} = 34.27$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{n_{\min} = 35 \text{ boxes}}$$

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Power** with a suitable example. Discuss the **trade-off between $\alpha$ and $\beta$** with the help of a diagram. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A two-tailed Z-test is performed with $H_0: \mu = 100$, $H_1: \mu \neq 100$, at $\alpha = 0.05$. The true mean is $\mu_1 = 104$, with $\sigma = 8$ and $n = 16$. Calculate the **Power** of this test using the two-tailed formula. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Concept of Power:**
The power of a hypothesis test is the probability that the test will **reject $H_0$** when the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ is actually true. Formally:

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true})$$

**Example:** A pharmaceutical company tests a new drug. $H_0$: the drug has no effect; $H_1$: the drug is effective. Power = 0.90 means that if the drug truly works, the trial has a 90% chance of detecting this.

**Trade-off between $\alpha$ and $\beta$:**

When the researcher **decreases $\alpha$** (e.g., from 0.05 to 0.01), the rejection region shrinks, making it harder to reject $H_0$. This:
- Reduces Type I errors (fewer false positives)
- But **increases $\beta$** (more false negatives)
- **Reduces Power**

Conversely, increasing $\alpha$ **shrinks $\beta$** and **raises Power**, but at the cost of more false positives.

**Diagrammatic Explanation:**

The two curves — $N(\mu_0, \sigma^2/n)$ under $H_0$ and $N(\mu_1, \sigma^2/n)$ under $H_1$ — overlap. The rejection region (area $\alpha$ in the tails of the $H_0$ curve) and $\beta$ (area of $H_1$ curve inside the acceptance region) share the same boundary. Moving the boundary right reduces $\alpha$ but increases $\beta$, and vice versa. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $\mu_0 = 100$, $\mu_1 = 104$, $\sigma = 8$, $n = 16$, $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed)

**Step 1 — Standardized Effect Size:**

$$\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0 = 4$$

$$\frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} = \frac{4 \times \sqrt{16}}{8} = \frac{4 \times 4}{8} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Critical Values (Two-tailed):**

For $\alpha = 0.05$, two-tailed: $Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$

**Step 3 — Apply Two-Tailed Power Formula:**

$$\text{Power} = \Phi\!\left(-Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) + \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(Z_{\alpha/2} - \frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right)\right]$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Power} = \Phi(-1.96 - 2.0) + [1 - \Phi(1.96 - 2.0)]$$

$$= \Phi(-3.96) + [1 - \Phi(-0.04)]$$

From standard normal tables:
- $\Phi(-3.96) \approx 0.0000375$
- $\Phi(-0.04) \approx 0.4840$
- $1 - 0.4840 = 0.5160$

$$\text{Power} \approx 0.0000 + 0.5160 = 0.5160$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power} \approx 0.516 \text{ or } 51.6\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the effect size is moderate ($\delta/\sigma = 0.5$), the two-tailed test achieves only ~52% power with $n=16$. To reach 0.80 power, $n$ would need to be increased substantially.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $\alpha$ is *set* by the researcher; $\beta$ is *computed*. Many students treat them symmetrically — this loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to round up sample size:** If $n = 34.27$, you MUST write $n = 35$. Rounding down is a frequent error.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in effect size:** $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ (not $\mu_0 - \mu_1$). Get the sign convention wrong and the whole derivation collapses.
> 
> 4. **One-tailed vs Two-tailed formulas:** Using $Z_{\alpha}$ for a two-tailed test (should be $Z_{\alpha/2}$) is a critical error.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting standardization:** $\bar{X}$ must be standardized using the *alternative* distribution to compute $\beta$, not the null.
> 
> 6. **No "interpretation" in numerical answers:** KTU evaluators award at least 1 mark for interpreting the numerical power value. Always end with: "The test has only X% power, which is below the desired 80% benchmark."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power of Hypothesis Tests**
> 
> - **Definition:** Power = $P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ false}) = 1 - \beta$.
> - **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a true $H_0$. Set by researcher. Default = 0.05.
> - **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Failing to reject a false $H_0$. Computed, not set.
> - **Standard benchmark for power:** 0.80 (i.e., $\beta = 0.20$).
> - **Four factors affecting power:** $\alpha \uparrow$, $n \uparrow$, $\delta \uparrow$, $\sigma \downarrow$ all increase Power.
> - **Right-tailed power formula:** $\text{Power} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} - Z_{\alpha}\right)$.
> - **Two-tailed power formula:** Sum of two tail-probabilities from the alternative distribution.
> - **Sample size formula (one-tailed):** $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha} + Z_{\beta}}{\delta/\sigma}\right)^{2}$.
> - **Sample size formula (two-tailed):** $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta}}{\delta/\sigma}\right)^{2}$.
> - **Always round $n$ UP** to the next integer.
> - **One-tailed tests are MORE powerful** than two-tailed tests for the same $\alpha$, $n$, and $\delta$.
> - **Effect size is a property of reality**, not a design choice. It dictates how large $n$ must be.
> - **Power analysis is done BEFORE data collection** (a-priori), not after.
> - **Low power** = high risk of Type II errors = false negatives = missed real effects.
> - **Mnemonic:** "**P**ower detects the **P**resent effect" vs. "$\alpha$ falsely accuses the innocent."

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Power of Hypothesis Tests**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) for PECHT416 — Introduction to Data Analysis*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hypothesis Tests on Population Means and Variances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hypothesis Tests on Population Means and Variances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Hypothesis Testing** is a statistical inference procedure in which two competing statements (the null hypothesis $H_0$ and the alternative hypothesis $H_1$) about an unknown population parameter are evaluated using sample data. The decision is based on a **test statistic** computed from the sample, which is compared against a sampling distribution under the assumption that $H_0$ is true.

For **population means** ($\mu$), the tests rely on the **Student's t-distribution** (when the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is unknown and the population is approximately normal). For **population variances** ($\sigma^2$), the tests rely on the **Chi-square ($\chi^2$) distribution** and the **F-distribution**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic forms the analytical backbone of inferential statistics. You MUST master four core tests:
> 1. **One-sample t-test** (testing a single mean against a known value)
> 2. **Two-sample t-test** (independent samples) and **Paired t-test**
> 3. **Chi-square test for a single variance**
> 4. **F-test for equality of two variances**

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a quality control engineer at a battery manufacturing plant. The company **claims** (the null hypothesis) that the average lifetime of a battery is **$\mu_0 = 1000$ hours**. You randomly pick 25 batteries and test them. 

- If the sample mean $\bar{x}$ is very close to 1000 (say, 1005 hours), you have **no strong reason to doubt** the company's claim. You **retain** $H_0$.
- If the sample mean is 800 hours (a big drop), the observed evidence is **too extreme** to be explained by random chance alone if the company were telling the truth. You **reject** $H_0$ and conclude that something is wrong.

> **The Core Logic:** We assume $H_0$ is innocent (true) until the data provides *overwhelming evidence* to convict it. This threshold of "overwhelming" is the **significance level $\alpha$**, which is typically **0.05** (5%) in KTU problems.

### 1.3 Components of a Hypothesis Test

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Null Hypothesis | $H_0$ | The "status quo" or "no effect" statement (e.g., $\mu = \mu_0$) |
| Alternative Hypothesis | $H_1$ or $H_a$ | The claim we want to test for (e.g., $\mu \neq \mu_0$) |
| Test Statistic | $T$ | A standardized value calculated from sample data |
| Significance Level | $\alpha$ | The probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is true (Type I error) |
| Critical Region | RR | The set of values for which we reject $H_0$ |
| P-value | $p$ | The smallest $\alpha$ at which $H_0$ can be rejected |

### 1.4 Types of Hypothesis Tests (Tail Configurations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Two-tailed test:} \quad & H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0 \\[4pt]
\textbf{Lower-tailed test:} \quad & H_0: \mu \geq \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu < \mu_0 \\[4pt]
\textbf{Upper-tailed test:} \quad & H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu > \mu_0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The null hypothesis $H_0$ ALWAYS contains the equality sign ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$). The alternative $H_1$ carries the strict inequality ($<$, $>$, or $\neq$).

### 1.5 Types of Errors in Decision Making

| Decision / Reality | $H_0$ is TRUE | $H_0$ is FALSE |
|--------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Fail to Reject $H_0$** | Correct Decision | Type II Error ($\beta$) |
| **Reject $H_0$** | Type I Error ($\alpha$) | Correct Decision (Power = $1 - \beta$) |

- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Rejecting a true null hypothesis (false alarm).
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Failing to reject a false null hypothesis (missed detection).
- **Power of the test ($1 - \beta$):** The probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are inversely related. Decreasing $\alpha$ (being more strict) increases $\beta$ (more chance of missing a real effect), and vice versa. This is a fundamental trade-off in hypothesis testing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Hypothesis Testing (Step-by-Step Procedure)

Every hypothesis test in KTU problems follows this rigorous 5-step algorithm:

1. **State the hypotheses:** Formulate $H_0$ and $H_1$ based on the research question.
2. **Choose the test statistic and significance level:** Select $t$, $\chi^2$, or $F$ based on the parameter being tested and known/unknown $\sigma$. Typical $\alpha = 0.05$.
3. **Compute the test statistic:** Calculate the standardized value from sample data.
4. **Determine the critical value(s) or p-value:** From statistical tables using the appropriate degrees of freedom.
5. **Make a decision:** 
   - **Critical value method:** Reject $H_0$ if the test statistic falls in the rejection region.
   - **P-value method:** Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$.

### 2.2 Test 1 — One-Sample t-Test (Unknown $\sigma$)

Used when we want to test a claim about a single population mean $\mu$, and the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is unknown. The sample must come from an approximately normal population.

$$
T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S / \sqrt{n}} \quad \sim \quad t_{(n-1)}
$$

**Why this works:** The numerator ($\bar{X} - \mu_0$) measures how far the sample mean is from the claimed mean. The denominator ($S/\sqrt{n}$) is the **standard error**, which represents the natural variability of sample means. The ratio tells us how many "standard errors" away from $\mu_0$ our observed $\bar{X}$ lies.

### 2.3 Test 2 — Two-Sample t-Test (Independent Samples, Unequal Variances)

Used to compare the means of **two independent populations** when both $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are unknown (the most common KTU scenario).

$$
T = \frac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{S_2^2}{n_2}}}
$$

**Degrees of freedom** (Welch–Satterthwaite approximation):
$$
df = \frac{\left( \dfrac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{S_2^2}{n_2} \right)^2}{\dfrac{\left( \dfrac{S_1^2}{n_1} \right)^2}{n_1 - 1} + \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{S_2^2}{n_2} \right)^2}{n_2 - 1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the **pooled t-test** (when $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$), the test statistic becomes:
> $$ T = \frac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)_0}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}} $$
> with $S_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1-1)S_1^2 + (n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$ and $df = n_1 + n_2 - 2$.

### 2.4 Test 3 — Paired t-Test (Dependent Samples)

Used when the two samples are **related** (e.g., before-and-after measurements on the same subjects). We reduce the problem to a one-sample test by computing the differences $D_i = X_{1i} - X_{2i}$.

$$
T = \frac{\bar{D} - D_0}{S_D / \sqrt{n}} \quad \sim \quad t_{(n-1)}
$$

where $\bar{D}$ is the mean of differences, $S_D$ is the standard deviation of differences, and $D_0 = 0$ for testing "no change."

### 2.5 Test 4 — Chi-Square Test for a Single Variance

Used to test claims about the variability ($\sigma^2$) of a single normal population.

$$
\chi^2 = \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2} \quad \sim \quad \chi^2_{(n-1)}
$$

**Why this works:** Since $(n-1)S^2/\sigma^2$ follows a chi-square distribution with $(n-1)$ degrees of freedom, we can standardize the sample variance and check if the observed value is consistent with the claimed $\sigma_0^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chi-square test is **highly sensitive to non-normality**. Always verify the normality assumption before applying this test.

### 2.6 Test 5 — F-Test for Equality of Two Variances

Used to compare the variances of two independent normal populations.

$$
F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} \quad \sim \quad F_{(n_1-1,\, n_2-1)}
$$

**Convention:** The larger sample variance is typically placed in the numerator so that $F \geq 1$. This is called the **right-tailed F-test**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Test | Hypotheses ($H_0$ vs $H_1$) | Test Statistic | Distribution | Degrees of Freedom |
|------|------------------------------|----------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **One-sample t** | $\mu = \mu_0$ vs $\mu \neq \mu_0$ | $T = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ | $n-1$ |
| **Two-sample t (pooled)** | $\mu_1 = \mu_2$ vs $\mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ | $T = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}}$ | $t$ | $n_1+n_2-2$ |
| **Paired t** | $\mu_D = 0$ vs $\mu_D \neq 0$ | $T = \dfrac{\bar{D}}{S_D/\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ | $n-1$ |
| **Chi-square variance** | $\sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2$ vs $\sigma^2 \neq \sigma_0^2$ | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | $\chi^2$ | $n-1$ |
| **F-test (variances)** | $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ vs $\sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2$ | $F = \dfrac{S_1^2}{S_2^2}$ | $F$ | $(n_1-1, n_2-1)$ |

### 2.8 Confidence Intervals (Dual Concept)

A two-sided hypothesis test at level $\alpha$ is mathematically **equivalent** to constructing a $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval. If the hypothesized value $\mu_0$ lies **outside** the CI, we reject $H_0$.

$$
\textbf{One-sample t CI:} \quad \bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

$$
\textbf{Two-sample t CI:} \quad (\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, df} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_2^2}{n_2}}
$$

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Testing if the mean diameter of bolts meets ISO specifications (one-sample t-test).
- **A/B Testing in Software:** Comparing click-through rates of two webpage designs (two-sample t-test).
- **Medical Trials:** Comparing patient blood pressure before and after a drug (paired t-test).
- **Process Stability:** Testing if the variability of a chemical process has drifted from target (chi-square test).
- **Comparing Two Machines:** Testing if the output variability of two CNC machines is equivalent (F-test).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — One-Sample t-Test

**Problem:** A coffee vending machine is set to dispense **$\mu_0 = 200$ ml** per cup. A quality inspector takes a sample of **$n = 10$** cups and records the following volumes (in ml):

$$ 198, 202, 199, 201, 200, 197, 203, 199, 200, 201 $$

At a **5% significance level**, test whether the machine is dispensing the correct amount.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \mu = 200 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 200
$$

**Step 2: Compute sample statistics.**

Sum of observations:
$$
\sum x_i = 198 + 202 + 199 + 201 + 200 + 197 + 203 + 199 + 200 + 201 = 2000
$$

Sample mean:
$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{2000}{10} = 200 \text{ ml}
$$

Sum of squared deviations from the mean ($\bar{X} = 200$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
(198-200)^2 &= 4 \\
(202-200)^2 &= 4 \\
(199-200)^2 &= 1 \\
(201-200)^2 &= 1 \\
(200-200)^2 &= 0 \\
(197-200)^2 &= 9 \\
(203-200)^2 &= 9 \\
(199-200)^2 &= 1 \\
(200-200)^2 &= 0 \\
(201-200)^2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2 = 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 9 + 9 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 30
$$

Sample variance:
$$
S^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1} = \frac{30}{9} = 3.3333
$$

Sample standard deviation:
$$
S = \sqrt{3.3333} = 1.8257 \text{ ml}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the test statistic.**
$$
T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{200 - 200}{1.8257/\sqrt{10}} = \frac{0}{0.5774} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Determine the critical value.**

For a two-tailed test with $\alpha = 0.05$ and $df = n - 1 = 9$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.025,\, 9} = 2.262
$$

**Step 5: Make a decision.**

Since $|T| = 0 < 2.262 = t_{\text{crit}}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that the machine's mean dispense volume differs from 200 ml.

> [!NOTE]
> The p-value corresponding to $T = 0$ with $df = 9$ is $p = 1.000$, which is much greater than $\alpha = 0.05$, confirming the decision.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Two-Sample t-Test

**Problem:** Two brands of electric bulbs (Brand A and Brand B) are tested for their average lifetime. The results are:

| Brand | Sample Size | Mean Lifetime (hours) | Std. Dev. (hours) |
|-------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| A     | $n_1 = 15$  | $\bar{X}_1 = 1200$     | $S_1 = 100$        |
| B     | $n_2 = 12$  | $\bar{X}_2 = 1100$     | $S_2 = 90$         |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the two brands have the **same mean lifetime** (assume unequal variances).

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the test statistic (Welch's t-test).**

Standard error:
$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_2^2}{n_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{100^2}{15} + \frac{90^2}{12}}
$$

Computing each term:
$$
\frac{100^2}{15} = \frac{10000}{15} = 666.6667
$$

$$
\frac{90^2}{12} = \frac{8100}{12} = 675.0000
$$

$$
SE = \sqrt{666.6667 + 675.0000} = \sqrt{1341.6667} = 36.6292
$$

Test statistic:
$$
T = \frac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{SE} = \frac{1200 - 1100}{36.6292} = \frac{100}{36.6292} = 2.7302
$$

**Step 3: Compute degrees of freedom (Welch–Satterthwaite).**
$$
df = \frac{(666.6667 + 675.0000)^2}{\frac{666.6667^2}{14} + \frac{675.0000^2}{11}}
$$

Numerator:
$$
(1341.6667)^2 = 1800189.7
$$

Denominator:
$$
\frac{666.6667^2}{14} = \frac{444444.5}{14} = 31746.04
$$

$$
\frac{675.0000^2}{11} = \frac{455625}{11} = 41420.45
$$

$$
df = \frac{1800189.7}{31746.04 + 41420.45} = \frac{1800189.7}{73166.49} = 24.605 \approx 24
$$

**Step 4: Determine critical value.**

For two-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$, $df = 24$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.025,\, 24} = 2.064
$$

**Step 5: Make a decision.**

Since $|T| = 2.7302 > 2.064 = t_{\text{crit}}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is significant evidence that the two brands have different mean lifetimes. Brand A appears to have a longer average lifetime.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Paired t-Test

**Problem:** A gym claims that a new fitness program reduces weight. Weights (in kg) of 8 members before and after the program:

| Member | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Before | 80 | 92 | 75 | 88 | 85 | 78 | 90 | 82 |
| After  | 78 | 90 | 74 | 86 | 83 | 77 | 88 | 81 |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the program is effective.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses (in terms of mean difference $\mu_D$).**
$$
H_0: \mu_D = 0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu_D > 0
$$

(We expect weight to **decrease**, so $D = \text{Before} - \text{After}$ should be positive.)

**Step 2: Compute differences.**
$$
D_i = \text{Before}_i - \text{After}_i:
$$
$$
D = \{2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 1\}
$$

**Step 3: Compute mean and standard deviation of $D$.**
$$
\bar{D} = \frac{2+2+1+2+2+1+2+1}{8} = \frac{13}{8} = 1.625
$$

Sum of squared deviations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(2-1.625)^2 &= 0.140625 \\
(2-1.625)^2 &= 0.140625 \\
(1-1.625)^2 &= 0.390625 \\
(2-1.625)^2 &= 0.140625 \\
(2-1.625)^2 &= 0.140625 \\
(1-1.625)^2 &= 0.390625 \\
(2-1.625)^2 &= 0.140625 \\
(1-1.625)^2 &= 0.390625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2 = 1.875
$$

Sample variance of $D$:
$$
S_D^2 = \frac{1.875}{7} = 0.26786
$$

Sample standard deviation:
$$
S_D = \sqrt{0.26786} = 0.5176
$$

**Step 4: Compute the test statistic.**
$$
T = \frac{\bar{D} - 0}{S_D / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.625}{0.5176 / \sqrt{8}} = \frac{1.625}{0.1830} = 8.879
$$

**Step 5: Determine critical value and decide.**

For upper-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$, $df = 7$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.05,\, 7} = 1.895
$$

Since $T = 8.879 > 1.895$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is strong evidence that the fitness program is effective in reducing weight.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Chi-Square Test for Variance

**Problem:** A machine fills bottles with a target standard deviation of **$\sigma_0 = 1.5$ ml**. A random sample of $n = 20$ bottles yields a sample standard deviation of $S = 2.0$ ml. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the process variance has increased.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \sigma^2 = 2.25 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \sigma^2 > 2.25
$$

**Step 2: Compute the test statistic.**
$$
\chi^2 = \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2} = \frac{19 \times (2.0)^2}{(1.5)^2} = \frac{19 \times 4}{2.25} = \frac{76}{2.25} = 33.778
$$

**Step 3: Determine critical value.**

For upper-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$, $df = 19$:
$$
\chi^2_{\text{crit}} = \chi^2_{0.05,\, 19} = 30.144
$$

**Step 4: Make a decision.**

Since $\chi^2 = 33.778 > 30.144 = \chi^2_{\text{crit}}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The variance of the filling process has significantly increased. The machine needs recalibration.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — F-Test for Two Variances

**Problem:** Two production lines produce resistors. From Line 1: $n_1 = 16$, $S_1^2 = 25$. From Line 2: $n_2 = 21$, $S_2^2 = 10$. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the variances are equal.

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the F-statistic.**

Placing the larger variance in the numerator:
$$
F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} = \frac{25}{10} = 2.5
$$

**Step 3: Determine critical value.**

For two-tailed test, $\alpha = 0.05$, $df_1 = 15$, $df_2 = 20$:
$$
F_{\text{crit}} = F_{0.025,\, 15,\, 20} = 2.76
$$

(Using $F_{0.025}$ since the test is two-tailed and the larger variance is in the numerator.)

**Step 4: Make a decision.**

Since $F = 2.5 < 2.76 = F_{\text{crit}}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the variances of the two production lines are different.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow of a Hypothesis Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define Research Question] --> h1A[Formulate H0 and H1]
    h1A --> h2A[Choose Significance Level alpha]
    h2A --> h3A[Select Appropriate Test: t, Chi-square, or F]
    h3A --> h4A[Collect Sample Data and Compute Test Statistic T]
    h4A --> h5A{Which Method?}
    h5A -->|Critical Value| cvA[Find t_crit / chi2_crit / F_crit from Tables]
    h5A -->|P-value| pvA[Compute p-value from Software/Tables]
    cvA --> cmpA{Is Test Statistic in Rejection Region?}
    pvA --> cmpB{Is p-value less than alpha?}
    cmpA -->|Yes| rejA[Reject H0]
    cmpA -->|No| retA[Fail to Reject H0]
    cmpB -->|Yes| rejA
    cmpB -->|No| retA
    rejA --> concA[Conclusion: Significant Evidence for H1]
    retA --> concB[Conclusion: Insufficient Evidence Against H0]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Selection of the Correct Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    archStart[Identify Parameter to Test] --> archQ1{What are you testing?}
    archQ1 -->|Single Mean mu| archA1[Is sigma known?]
    archQ1 -->|Two Means mu1, mu2| archB1{Are samples paired?}
    archQ1 -->|Single Variance sigma2| archC1[Use Chi-square Test]
    archQ1 -->|Two Variances sigma1^2, sigma2^2| archD1[Use F-test]
    archA1 -->|Yes| archA2[Z-test: N0,1]
    archA1 -->|No| archA3[One-Sample t-test]
    archB1 -->|No| archB2[Two-Sample t-test]
    archB1 -->|Yes| archB3[Paired t-test]
    archC1 --> archC2[Chi-square with df = n-1]
    archD1 --> archD2[F with df1 = n1-1, df2 = n2-1]
    archA2 --> archEnd[Compute and Decide]
    archA3 --> archEnd
    archB2 --> archEnd
    archB3 --> archEnd
    archC2 --> archEnd
    archD2 --> archEnd
```

### 4.3 Visualizing the Rejection Region

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regStart[Distribution under H0] --> regTwo{Two-tailed Test}
    regTwo --> regPlot[Rejection region: T less than -t_alpha/2 OR T greater than t_alpha/2]
    regPlot --> regCenter[Acceptance region: -t_alpha/2 less than T less than t_alpha/2]
    regCenter --> regEnd[If T falls in rejection region, REJECT H0]
```

### 4.4 Hypothesis Test Workflow for Population Means

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    wfStart[Sample Data] --> wfStep1[Compute Sample Mean xbar and Std Dev S]
    wfStep1 --> wfStep2[State H0 and H1]
    wfStep2 --> wfStep3[Compute Test Statistic T]
    wfStep3 --> wfStep4[Find t_crit from t-table using df = n-1]
    wfStep4 --> wfDec{Two-tailed?}
    wfDec -->|Yes| wfRej1[Reject H0 if vertical bar T vertical bar greater than t_alpha slash 2]
    wfDec -->|No, Upper| wfRej2[Reject H0 if T greater than t_alpha]
    wfDec -->|No, Lower| wfRej3[Reject H0 if T less than -t_alpha]
    wfRej1 --> wfEnd[Decision Made]
    wfRej2 --> wfEnd
    wfRej3 --> wfEnd
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms with respect to hypothesis testing:
**(a)** Null Hypothesis
**(b)** Type I error
**(c)** Level of significance

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The **null hypothesis ($H_0$)** is a statement about one or more population parameters that is assumed to be true unless there is strong sample evidence to the contrary. It represents the "no effect" or "status quo" claim and always contains the equality sign.

**(b)** A **Type I error** occurs when the null hypothesis $H_0$ is **true**, but we incorrectly **reject** it. The probability of committing a Type I error is denoted by $\alpha$ (the significance level).

**(c)** The **level of significance ($\alpha$)** is the maximum probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis that a researcher is willing to tolerate. Common values are $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) and $\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

Distinguish between a **one-tailed test** and a **two-tailed test**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | One-Tailed Test | Two-Tailed Test |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| **Alternative $H_1$** | Uses $<$ or $>$ (directional) | Uses $\neq$ (non-directional) |
| **Rejection region** | Located in one tail only | Located in both tails |
| **Critical value** | $t_{\alpha}$ or $-t_{\alpha}$ | $\pm t_{\alpha/2}$ |
| **When to use** | When direction of effect is known in advance | When direction is unknown or both directions are of interest |

**Example (One-tailed):** Testing whether a new drug is **more effective** than the old drug: $H_0: \mu_{\text{new}} \leq \mu_{\text{old}}$ vs $H_1: \mu_{\text{new}} > \mu_{\text{old}}$.

**Example (Two-tailed):** Testing whether two brands of batteries have **different** mean lifetimes: $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ vs $H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A random sample of **16** observations from a normal population has a sample mean of **78** and a sample standard deviation of **10**. Test the hypothesis that the population mean $\mu = 82$ against the alternative $\mu \neq 82$ at a **5% level of significance**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The nicotine content (in mg) of two brands of cigarettes is believed to have the same variance. Independent random samples yielded:
- Brand 1: $n_1 = 10$, $S_1^2 = 1.8$
- Brand 2: $n_2 = 8$, $S_2^2 = 0.9$

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the variances are significantly different. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \mu = 82 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu \neq 82 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}
$$

**Step 2: Given data.**
$$
n = 16, \quad \bar{X} = 78, \quad S = 10, \quad \mu_0 = 82, \quad \alpha = 0.05
$$

**Step 3: Test statistic (one-sample t-test).**
$$
T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{78 - 82}{10/\sqrt{16}} = \frac{-4}{10/4} = \frac{-4}{2.5} = -1.6
$$

**Step 4: Critical value.**

Degrees of freedom: $df = n - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15$.

For two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.025,\, 15} = 2.131
$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $|T| = 1.6 < 2.131 = t_{\text{crit}}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that the population mean differs from 82.

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2 \quad \text{(Two-tailed F-test)}
$$

**Step 2: Test statistic.**

Place the larger sample variance in the numerator:
$$
F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} = \frac{1.8}{0.9} = 2.0
$$

**Step 3: Critical value.**

Degrees of freedom: $df_1 = n_1 - 1 = 9$, $df_2 = n_2 - 1 = 7$.

For two-tailed test, we use $\alpha/2 = 0.025$ in the upper tail:
$$
F_{\text{crit}} = F_{0.025,\, 9,\, 7} = 4.82
$$

**Step 4: Decision.**

Since $F = 2.0 < 4.82 = F_{\text{crit}}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to claim that the two brands have different variances in nicotine content.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** A coaching centre claims that its students score **more than 75** on average. A sample of **25** students yields a mean score of **78** with a standard deviation of **10**. Test the claim at a **5% level of significance**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A fitness program is tested on **9** individuals. Their weights (in kg) before and after the program are:

| Person | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Before | 70 | 80 | 65 | 78 | 72 | 68 | 75 | 82 | 74 |
| After  | 68 | 78 | 64 | 76 | 70 | 67 | 73 | 80 | 72 |

Using a **paired t-test** at $\alpha = 0.05$, determine whether the program is effective. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**
$$
H_0: \mu \leq 75 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu > 75 \quad \text{(Upper-tailed test)}
$$

**Step 2: Given data.**
$$
n = 25, \quad \bar{X} = 78, \quad S = 10, \quad \mu_0 = 75, \quad \alpha = 0.05
$$

**Step 3: Test statistic.**
$$
T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{78 - 75}{10/\sqrt{25}} = \frac{3}{10/5} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5
$$

**Step 4: Critical value.**

$df = 25 - 1 = 24$.

For upper-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.05,\, 24} = 1.711
$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $T = 1.5 < 1.711 = t_{\text{crit}}$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% level, there is insufficient evidence to support the coaching centre's claim that students score more than 75 on average.

---

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses (using $D = \text{Before} - \text{After}$).**
$$
H_0: \mu_D = 0 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1: \mu_D > 0
$$

**Step 2: Compute differences.**
$$
D = \{2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 2\}
$$

**Step 3: Sample statistics of $D$.**
$$
\bar{D} = \frac{2+2+1+2+2+1+2+2+2}{9} = \frac{16}{9} = 1.7778
$$

Sum of squared deviations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(1-1.7778)^2 &= 0.6050 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(1-1.7778)^2 &= 0.6050 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494 \\
(2-1.7778)^2 &= 0.0494
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2 = 2.1560
$$

$$
S_D^2 = \frac{2.1560}{9-1} = \frac{2.1560}{8} = 0.2695
$$

$$
S_D = \sqrt{0.2695} = 0.5191
$$

**Step 4: Test statistic.**
$$
T = \frac{\bar{D} - 0}{S_D / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{1.7778}{0.5191 / \sqrt{9}} = \frac{1.7778}{0.1730} = 10.276
$$

**Step 5: Critical value and decision.**

$df = 9 - 1 = 8$. For upper-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$
t_{\text{crit}} = t_{0.05,\, 8} = 1.860
$$

Since $T = 10.276 > 1.860$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** There is very strong evidence that the fitness program is effective in reducing weight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Errors (–2 marks):** Students frequently write $df = n$ instead of $df = n - 1$ for one-sample t-tests and chi-square tests. ALWAYS use $n-1$ for these.
> 2. **F-test Misuse (–2 marks):** In the F-test, students often place the smaller variance in the numerator, which leads to $F < 1$ and incorrect comparison. **Always place the larger sample variance in the numerator** for a two-tailed test.
> 3. **Confusing Paired vs Independent Tests (–3 marks):** A paired t-test on data that should be analyzed independently (or vice versa) is a critical conceptual error. Check if subjects are "matched" or "before-after" for paired tests.
> 4. **Forgetting to State the Conclusion in Context (–1 mark):** Always conclude by relating the result back to the **original problem** (e.g., "Brand A has a longer lifetime" rather than just "Reject $H_0$").
> 5. **Two-tailed vs One-tailed Mismatch (–1 mark):** Using $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed) when the test is two-tailed. For two-tailed tests, use $\alpha/2$ when looking up critical values.
> 6. **Chi-square Normality Assumption (–1 mark):** The chi-square variance test requires the population to be **normal**. Failing to mention this assumption in a 7-mark question is a mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **One-Sample t-Test:** Tests $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ when $\sigma$ is unknown. Use $T = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}$ with $df = n - 1$.

- **Two-Sample t-Test (Independent):** Tests $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$. The pooled version assumes equal variances; the Welch version does not. Use Welch unless told otherwise.

- **Paired t-Test:** Reduce the problem to a one-sample test by computing $D_i = X_{1i} - X_{2i}$. Apply one-sample t-test on $D$ with $H_0: \mu_D = 0$.

- **Chi-Square Test for Variance:** Tests $H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2$ using $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ with $df = n-1$. **Requires normality.**

- **F-Test for Two Variances:** Tests $H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ using $F = S_1^2 / S_2^2$ with $df_1 = n_1 - 1$, $df_2 = n_2 - 1$. **Place larger variance in numerator.**

- **Decision Rule (Critical Value):** Reject $H_0$ if the test statistic falls in the rejection region (e.g., $|T| > t_{\text{crit}}$ for two-tailed).

- **Decision Rule (P-value):** Reject $H_0$ if $p < \alpha$.

- **Confidence Interval Connection:** A two-sided test at level $\alpha$ rejects $H_0$ if the hypothesized value is **outside** the $(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval.

- **Type I vs Type II:** $\alpha$ = Type I error (false positive). $\beta$ = Type II error (false negative). Power = $1 - \beta$.

- **Tail Configuration:**
  - $H_1: \neq \Rightarrow$ Two-tailed $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha/2$ in each tail
  - $H_1: > \Rightarrow$ Upper-tailed $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha$ in upper tail
  - $H_1: < \Rightarrow$ Lower-tailed $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha$ in lower tail

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Factor Experiments: Introduction to Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), Blocking, and Randomization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Factor Experiments: Introduction to Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), Blocking, and Randomization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Single Factor Experiments — Formal Definition

A **Single Factor Experiment** is an experimental study in which exactly **one independent variable (the factor)** is manipulated, and its effect on a continuous **response variable** is measured across two or more levels (treatments). When more than two treatment levels are present, pairwise t-tests become statistically invalid due to the inflated **family-wise error rate (Type I error inflation)**. To resolve this, the **Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** procedure, originally developed by **Sir Ronald Aylmer Fisher** in the 1920s, is employed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)**: A statistical hypothesis testing procedure developed by **R.A. Fisher** that partitions the total observed variability in an experiment into components attributable to **systematic (treatment) variation** and **uncontrolled (random/error) variation**, and then tests whether the systematic component is statistically significant via the **F-distribution** (Snedecor's F).

The notation standardized by KTU for a single-factor experiment with $a$ treatments and $n$ replicates per treatment is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, a; \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where:
- $y_{ij}$ = observed response for the $j$-th replicate of the $i$-th treatment.
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean.
- $\tau_i$ = true effect of the $i$-th treatment (with $\sum_{i=1}^{a} \tau_i = 0$).
- $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ = random error term, i.i.d. Normal with zero mean and constant variance.

> [!NOTE]
> The two foundational design principles that govern a valid single-factor experiment are **Randomization** (allocation of treatments to experimental units by chance, to remove bias) and **Replication** (independent repetition of each treatment, to estimate the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$).

## 1.2 Blocking — The Intuitive Picture

**Blocking** is the third foundational principle of experimental design. A **block** is a grouping of experimental units that are *similar* to each other in some nuisance characteristic (e.g., same batch of raw material, same operator, same time of day) but different from units in other blocks. By grouping similar units together and applying all treatments *within* each block, the variability *between* blocks is removed from the error term, increasing the **statistical power** to detect treatment effects.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biryani Tasting" Story

Imagine a food critic in Kerala wants to test whether **3 brands of biryani masala (A, B, C)** produce different mean aroma scores. She has **15 volunteers** to taste and rate. Instead of giving all 5 doses of Brand A in the morning, then all 5 of Brand B at noon (which confounds masala type with time-of-day hunger), she **randomizes** the assignment of masala to volunteer via a lottery. To control for the fact that some volunteers are spice-tolerant and some are not, she groups them into **5 blocks of 3** (similar spice-tolerance per block) and within each block, all 3 brands are tasted. The remaining variation *within* a block reflects the **true** brand effect. This is exactly the **Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this triad**: A well-designed experiment rests on three pillars — **Replication, Randomization, and Blocking (Local Control)**. Fisher's famous 1926 monograph on agricultural trials cemented this trio.

## 1.4 Why ANOVA Instead of Multiple t-Tests?

If we have $a = 4$ treatments, the number of pairwise comparisons is $\binom{4}{2} = 6$. If each t-test is conducted at $\alpha = 0.05$, the family-wise error rate balloons to $1 - (0.95)^6 \approx 0.265$ (a **26.5% chance** of at least one false positive). ANOVA avoids this by performing **one single overall F-test** at $\alpha = 0.05$ first; only if it is significant do we proceed to **post-hoc multiple comparisons** (Tukey's HSD, Bonferroni, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Hypotheses of Single-Factor ANOVA

The statistical hypotheses are stated as:

$$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_a = 0 \quad \text{(All treatment means are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \tau_i \neq 0 \quad \text{for at least one } i \quad \text{(At least one treatment mean differs)}$$

The test statistic is the **F-ratio**:

$$F_0 = \frac{\text{Mean Square Treatment (MST)}}{\text{Mean Square Error (MSE)}} = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$$

If $H_0$ is true, both MST and MSE estimate the same $\sigma^2$, so $F_0 \approx 1$. A value of $F_0$ significantly greater than 1 provides evidence against $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule**: Reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, N-a}$, where $F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, N-a}$ is the upper-$\alpha$ critical value from the F-distribution with $(a-1)$ numerator and $(N-a)$ denominator degrees of freedom, and $N = a \cdot n$ is the total number of observations.

## 2.2 Partitioning the Total Sum of Squares — The Heart of ANOVA

The **Total Sum of Squares (SST or $SS_T$)** is decomposed into two orthogonal components:

$$SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$$

where:

- $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ = Total variation.
- $SS_{Tr} = n\sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ = Variation *between* treatment means.
- $SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$ = Variation *within* treatments (pure error).

The notation $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$ denotes the $i$-th treatment mean, and $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ denotes the grand mean:

$$\bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}, \qquad \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \frac{1}{an}\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}$$

## 2.3 The ANOVA Table — Master Template (CRD)

| Source of Variation | Sum of Squares (SS) | Degrees of Freedom (df) | Mean Square (MS) | Expected Mean Square (EMS) | F-Statistic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Between Treatments** (Model) | $SS_{Tr}$ | $a - 1$ | $MS_{Tr} = \dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{a-1}$ | $\sigma^2 + \dfrac{n \sum \tau_i^2}{a-1}$ | $\dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$ |
| **Within Treatments** (Error) | $SS_E$ | $N - a$ | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{N-a}$ | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| **Total** | $SS_T$ | $N - 1$ | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom Logic**: $SS_T$ has $N-1$ because the grand mean $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ uses up 1 df. $SS_{Tr}$ has $a-1$ because the $a$ treatment means share the constraint $\sum \bar{y}_{i\cdot} = \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ (the grand mean is the average of the treatment means when $n$ is equal). $SS_E$ has $N-a$ by subtraction, equivalently $a(n-1)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — ANOVA & Experimental Design

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering / Data Utility |
|---|---|---|
| ANOVA Model | $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ | General Linear Model skeleton used in regression, DOE, and ML. |
| Grand Mean | $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum y_{ij}$ | Baseline against which all treatment effects are measured. |
| $SS_T$ | $\sum y_{ij}^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_{ij})^2}{N}$ | Computational shortcut (uncorrected total SS − correction factor). |
| $SS_{Tr}$ | $\sum \dfrac{T_i^2}{n} - \dfrac{(\sum y_{ij})^2}{N}$ | Uses treatment totals $T_i = \sum_j y_{ij}$. |
| $SS_E$ | $SS_T - SS_{Tr}$ | Subtraction method avoids redundant arithmetic. |
| F-Statistic | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$ | Variance ratio for overall significance of factor. |
| Critical Value | $F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, N-a}$ | From F-tables (upper tail). |
| p-value | $P(F_{a-1,\, N-a} \geq F_0)$ | Decision based on p $< \alpha$ implies rejection of $H_0$. |
| CV (Coefficient of Variation) | $\dfrac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}} \times 100\%$ | Standardized measure of experimental precision (KTU expects reporting). |
| Tukey HSD | $q_{\alpha,\, a,\, N-a} \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_E}{n}}$ | Post-hoc pairwise comparison among means. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CV Interpretation**: A CV $< 10\%$ is typical of well-controlled laboratory experiments; a CV between 10–20% is acceptable in field/industrial trials; a CV $> 30\%$ indicates poor experimental control.

## 2.5 Principles of Experimental Design — The "Three R's"

1. **Replication**: Independent repetition of the basic experiment. Provides an estimate of experimental error $\sigma^2$ and increases precision (standard error of a mean decreases as $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$).
2. **Randomization**: The allocation of treatments to experimental units is performed via a random mechanism (e.g., coin flip, random number table). This ensures independence of observations and that the error terms $\epsilon_{ij}$ are i.i.d. Normal.
3. **Blocking (Local Control)**: Grouping homogeneous experimental units into blocks and randomizing treatments within each block. The block factor is included in the model so that its variability does not inflate the error term.

The associated linear model for an **RCBD** (Randomized Complete Block Design) extends the single-factor model:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i = 1,\ldots,a;\; j = 1,\ldots,b$$

where $\beta_j$ is the $j$-th block effect ($\sum_j \beta_j = 0$).

## 2.6 RCBD ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | $SS_{Tr}$ | $a-1$ | $MS_{Tr} = SS_{Tr}/(a-1)$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Blocks | $SS_{Bl}$ | $b-1$ | $MS_{Bl} = SS_{Bl}/(b-1)$ | $MS_{Bl}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ | $MS_E = SS_E/[(a-1)(b-1)]$ | — |
| Total | $SS_T$ | $ab - 1$ | — | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> **F-test for Blocks** in RCBD is *not* of primary research interest; it is a diagnostic check confirming that blocking was effective (large $F_{Bl}$ is desirable). The KTU examiner usually asks only for the treatment F-test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sum of Squares Decomposition

The fundamental identity underlying ANOVA is the algebraic identity (note: we use `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax, but in display math the bar is the regular one):

**Step 1** — Begin with the deviation of an observation from the grand mean:

$$y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot}) + (\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})$$

**Reasoning (text)**: Every observation's distance from the grand mean can be expressed as the sum of its distance from its own treatment mean (the *within-group* piece) plus the distance of its treatment mean from the grand mean (the *between-group* piece).

**Step 2** — Square both sides and sum over all $i$ and $j$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\left[(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot}) + (\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})\right]^2$$

**Step 3** — Expand the right-hand side as $(A + B)^2 = A^2 + 2AB + B^2$:

$$= \sum_i\sum_j(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2 + 2\sum_i\sum_j(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + \sum_i\sum_j(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

**Step 4** — Show the cross-product term vanishes. Since $(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})$ is constant within the $i$-th treatment, factor it out:

$$2\sum_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})\underbrace{\sum_j(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})}_{= \; 0 \text{ (definition of mean)}} = 0$$

**Step 5** — In the last term, since $(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})$ does not depend on $j$, the inner sum is just $n$ times the term:

$$\sum_i\sum_j(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 = n\sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

**Step 6** — Combine into the canonical ANOVA identity:

$$\boxed{SS_T = SS_E + SS_{Tr}}$$

$$\sum_{i}\sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2 + n\sum_{i}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Complete CRD ANOVA

A pharmaceutical engineer tests **4 formulations (A, B, C, D)** of a sustained-release tablet, measuring the **dissolution efficiency (%)** at the 4-hour mark. **5 tablets** are randomly assigned to each formulation. The data are:

| Replicate | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 78 | 88 | 94 | 85 |
| 2 | 82 | 92 | 91 | 87 |
| 3 | 85 | 86 | 89 | 90 |
| 4 | 80 | 90 | 95 | 84 |
| 5 | 75 | 89 | 96 | 89 |
| **Total $T_i$** | 400 | 445 | 465 | 435 |
| **Mean $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$** | 80.0 | 89.0 | 93.0 | 87.0 |

Grand total: $G = 400 + 445 + 465 + 435 = 1745$. Grand mean: $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 1745 / 20 = 87.25$.

### Step A — Correction Factor (CF)

The correction factor is:

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(1745)^2}{20} = \frac{3045025}{20} = 152251.25$$

### Step B — Compute the Uncorrected Total Sum of Squares

Sum of all squared observations:

$$\sum y_{ij}^2 = (78^2 + 82^2 + \cdots + 89^2)$$

Let us compute this term by term:
- $78^2 = 6084$
- $82^2 = 6724$
- $85^2 = 7225$
- $80^2 = 6400$
- $75^2 = 5625$ → Sum for A = 32058
- $88^2 = 7744$
- $92^2 = 8464$
- $86^2 = 7396$
- $90^2 = 8100$
- $89^2 = 7921$ → Sum for B = 39625
- $94^2 = 8836$
- $91^2 = 8281$
- $89^2 = 7921$
- $95^2 = 9025$
- $96^2 = 9216$ → Sum for C = 43279
- $85^2 = 7225$
- $87^2 = 7569$
- $90^2 = 8100$
- $84^2 = 7056$
- $89^2 = 7921$ → Sum for D = 37871

$$\sum y_{ij}^2 = 32058 + 39625 + 43279 + 37871 = 152833$$

Therefore:

$$SS_T = 152833 - CF = 152833 - 152251.25 = 581.75$$

### Step C — Compute the Treatment Sum of Squares

The treatment contribution is:

$$SS_{Tr} = \sum \frac{T_i^2}{n} - CF = \frac{400^2 + 445^2 + 465^2 + 435^2}{5} - 152251.25$$

Compute the numerators:
- $400^2 = 160000$
- $445^2 = 198025$
- $465^2 = 216225$
- $435^2 = 189225$
- Sum of $T_i^2 = 160000 + 198025 + 216225 + 189225 = 763475$

Therefore:

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{763475}{5} - 152251.25 = 152695 - 152251.25 = 443.75$$

### Step D — Compute the Error Sum of Squares

By subtraction:

$$SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Tr} = 581.75 - 443.75 = 138.00$$

### Step E — Construct the ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05,\, 3,\, 16}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | 443.75 | 3 | 147.917 | **10.72** | 3.24 |
| Error | 138.00 | 16 | 8.625 | — | — |
| Total | 581.75 | 19 | — | — | — |

Computations shown:
- $MS_{Tr} = 443.75 / 3 = 147.917$
- $MS_E = 138.00 / 16 = 8.625$
- $F_0 = 147.917 / 8.625 = 17.150 / \ldots$ — recalculating: $147.917 / 8.625 = 17.150$ approximately. Let us recompute carefully: $8.625 \times 17 = 146.625$; remainder $1.292$; $1.292/8.625 = 0.150$. So $F_0 = 17.15$.

The critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $(3, 16)$ df is $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$ (standard F-table).

**Decision**: Since $F_0 = 17.15 > 3.24 = F_{crit}$, we **reject $H_0$**. There is strong evidence that at least one formulation produces a different mean dissolution efficiency.

### Step F — Coefficient of Variation (CV)

$$CV = \frac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}} \times 100\% = \frac{\sqrt{8.625}}{87.25} \times 100\% = \frac{2.937}{87.25} \times 100\% \approx 3.37\%$$

**Interpretation**: CV = 3.37% indicates excellent experimental precision.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Connection Between Topics

| Sub-Topic | Key Question Answered | Primary Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| ANOVA | "Are the means of $a \geq 3$ groups equal?" | F-distribution | p-value, decision |
| Blocking | "How do I remove a nuisance source of variation?" | Group similar units; block factor in model | Reduced MSE |
| Randomization | "How do I ensure valid inference?" | Random allocation of treatments | Independence of $\epsilon_{ij}$ |
| Replication | "How do I estimate $\sigma^2$?" | Repeat each treatment $n$ times | $MS_E$ is an estimator of $\sigma^2$ |
| Post-hoc Tests | "Which specific means differ?" | Tukey HSD, Bonferroni, LSD | Pairwise comparisons |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Single-Factor ANOVA (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Experimental Data: y_ij, i=1..a, j=1..n"] --> B["Compute Treatment Means: y_i dot and Grand Mean: y double dot"]
    B --> C["Compute Correction Factor CF = G squared divided by N"]
    C --> D["Calculate SS_T = sum y_ij squared minus CF"]
    C --> E["Calculate SS_Tr = sum T_i squared divided by n minus CF"]
    D --> F["Compute SS_E = SS_T minus SS_Tr"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Build ANOVA Table: df for Treatments is a minus 1, df for Error is N minus a"]
    G --> H["Compute F_0 = MS_Tr divided by MS_E"]
    H --> I["Compare F_0 with F critical from F distribution at alpha"]
    I --> J{"Decision: F_0 greater than F_crit?"}
    J -- "YES" --> K["Reject H_0: At least one treatment mean differs"]
    J -- "NO" --> L["Fail to Reject H_0: Insufficient evidence of differences"]
    K --> M["Proceed to Post Hoc Tests: Tukey HSD or Bonferroni"]
    L --> N["Report CV and Conclude"]
```

## 4.2 Architecture: RCBD vs CRD Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRD["CRD: Completely Randomized Design"]
        CRD1["Single Factor with a Treatments"] --> CRD2["N Experimental Units, No Blocking"]
        CRD2 --> CRD3["Randomly Assign All N Units to a Treatments"]
        CRD3 --> CRD4["Model: y_ij = mu plus tau_i plus epsilon_ij"]
        CRD4 --> CRD5["df Error = N minus a"]
    end

    subgraph RCBD["RCBD: Randomized Complete Block Design"]
        RCBD1["Single Factor with a Treatments"] --> RCBD2["Group N Units into b Homogeneous Blocks"]
        RCBD2 --> RCBD3["Within Each Block, Randomly Assign All a Treatments"]
        RCBD3 --> RCBD4["Model: y_ij = mu plus tau_i plus beta_j plus epsilon_ij"]
        RCBD4 --> RCBD5["df Error = a minus 1 times b minus 1"]
    end

    CRD5 --> RESULT["Larger MSE, Lower Power"]
    RCBD5 --> RESULT2["Smaller MSE, Higher Power, Valid Conclusion"]
```

## 4.3 The Three Pillars of Experimental Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PILLAR["Fisher's Three Principles of Experimental Design"]
    PILLAR --> P1["REPLICATION: Independent repetition, n times per treatment"]
    PILLAR --> P2["RANDOMIZATION: Random allocation of treatments to units"]
    PILLAR --> P3["BLOCKING: Local control of nuisance variability"]

    P1 --> OUT1["Estimates experimental error sigma squared and increases precision"]
    P2 --> OUT2["Validates independence assumption, removes bias from confounders"]
    P3 --> OUT3["Removes block to block variability from the error term"]

    OUT1 --> FINAL["Valid, Powerful ANOVA Inference"]
    OUT2 --> FINAL
    OUT3 --> FINAL
```

## 4.4 ANOVA Hypothesis-Testing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["State H_0: tau_1 equals tau_2 equals ... equals tau_a equals 0"]
    START --> H1["State H_1: At least one tau_i is not equal to 0"]
    H1 --> ALPHA["Choose Significance Level alpha, typically 0.05"]
    ALPHA --> STAT["Compute F_0 = MS_Tr divided by MS_E"]
    STAT --> PVAL["Compute p value = P of F_{a minus 1, N minus a} greater than or equal to F_0"]
    PVAL --> DECIDE{"Is p value less than alpha?"}
    DECIDE -- "YES" --> REJ["Reject H_0"]
    DECIDE -- "NO" --> FAIL["Fail to Reject H_0"]
    REJ --> POSTHOC["Apply Tukey HSD to Identify Differing Pairs"]
    FAIL --> CONCLUDE["Conclude: No Significant Treatment Effect Detected"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). State the basic linear model for a single-factor experiment with $a$ treatments and $n$ replicates.**

**Model Answer**:

**ANOVA** is a statistical technique, developed by **R.A. Fisher**, used to test the hypothesis that the means of three or more populations (treatments) are equal, by partitioning the total variability in the data into variability *between* treatments and variability *within* treatments, and comparing these via the **F-distribution**.

The single-factor ANOVA model is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, a, \; j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $\mu$ is the overall mean, $\tau_i$ is the $i$-th treatment effect ($\sum \tau_i = 0$), and $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ are i.i.d. random errors.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly explain the three fundamental principles of experimental design.**

**Model Answer**:

1. **Replication**: The independent repetition of each treatment under identical conditions. Replication provides an estimate of the experimental error variance $\sigma^2$ and increases the precision of treatment mean estimates (standard error of mean $\propto 1/\sqrt{n}$).

2. **Randomization**: The random allocation of treatments to experimental units. It ensures that the error terms are independently and identically distributed, thereby validating the assumptions of ANOVA and preventing systematic bias from confounding variables.

3. **Blocking (Local Control)**: Grouping experimental units that are homogeneous with respect to a nuisance variable into blocks, and randomizing treatments within each block. Blocking removes the inter-block variability from the error term, thereby reducing $MS_E$ and increasing the power of the F-test.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Module 3, 14-Mark ESE Pattern)

**(a)** Distinguish between a **Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** and a **Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD)**. State the linear model and the ANOVA table for both. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three catalysts (A, B, C) are tested for the yield of a chemical reaction. Each catalyst is used in **4 randomly selected batches**. The yields observed are:

| Batch | Catalyst A | Catalyst B | Catalyst C |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 72 | 78 | 75 |
| 2 | 76 | 82 | 80 |
| 3 | 74 | 79 | 77 |
| 4 | 70 | 81 | 74 |

Perform a one-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$ to test whether the mean yields differ significantly. Given $F_{0.05, 2, 9} = 4.26$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**CRD vs RCBD Comparison**:

| Feature | CRD | RCBD |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking | No blocking used | One blocking factor applied |
| Model | $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ | $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \epsilon_{ij}$ |
| df (Error) | $N - a$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ |
| Use Case | Homogeneous experimental units | Units vary by a known nuisance variable |
| Precision | Lower (larger $MS_E$) | Higher (smaller $MS_E$) |

**CRD ANOVA Table** ($N = an$):

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | $SS_{Tr}$ | $a-1$ | $SS_{Tr}/(a-1)$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $N-a$ | $SS_E/(N-a)$ | — |
| Total | $SS_T$ | $N-1$ | — | — |

**RCBD ANOVA Table** ($N = ab$):

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | $SS_{Tr}$ | $a-1$ | $SS_{Tr}/(a-1)$ | $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ |
| Blocks | $SS_{Bl}$ | $b-1$ | $SS_{Bl}/(b-1)$ | $MS_{Bl}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ | $SS_E/[(a-1)(b-1)]$ | — |
| Total | $SS_T$ | $ab-1$ | — | — |

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Hypotheses**:

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C, \qquad H_1: \text{At least one mean differs.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Totals**:
- $T_A = 72 + 76 + 74 + 70 = 292$
- $T_B = 78 + 82 + 79 + 81 = 320$
- $T_C = 75 + 80 + 77 + 74 = 306$
- Grand total: $G = 292 + 320 + 306 = 918$
- $N = 12$, $a = 3$, $n = 4$, $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 918/12 = 76.5$

**Step 3 — Correction Factor**:

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{918^2}{12} = \frac{842724}{12} = 70227.00$$

**Step 4 — Sum of Squares**:

$$\sum y_{ij}^2 = (72^2 + 76^2 + 74^2 + 70^2) + (78^2 + 82^2 + 79^2 + 81^2) + (75^2 + 80^2 + 77^2 + 74^2)$$

Compute:
- A: $5184 + 5776 + 5476 + 4900 = 21336$
- B: $6084 + 6724 + 6241 + 6561 = 25610$
- C: $5625 + 6400 + 5929 + 5476 = 23430$
- Total: $21336 + 25610 + 23430 = 70376$

$$SS_T = 70376 - 70227 = 149.00$$

**Step 5 — Treatment Sum of Squares**:

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{292^2 + 320^2 + 306^2}{4} - 70227 = \frac{85264 + 102400 + 93636}{4} - 70227$$

$$= \frac{281300}{4} - 70227 = 70325 - 70227 = 98.00$$

**Step 6 — Error Sum of Squares**:

$$SS_E = 149.00 - 98.00 = 51.00$$

**Step 7 — ANOVA Table**:

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | 98.00 | 2 | 49.00 | 8.65 |
| Error | 51.00 | 9 | 5.667 | — |
| Total | 149.00 | 11 | — | — |

**Step 8 — Decision**:

$F_0 = 49.00 / 5.667 = 8.65$. Since $F_0 = 8.65 > F_{0.05, 2, 9} = 4.26$, **reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion**: There is significant evidence that the mean yields differ among the three catalysts.

**CV**:

$$CV = \frac{\sqrt{5.667}}{76.5} \times 100\% = \frac{2.381}{76.5} \times 100\% \approx 3.11\%$$

This indicates good experimental precision.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Module 3, 14-Mark ESE Pattern)

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Randomization** and **Blocking** in the design of experiments. Why is blocking effective in reducing experimental error? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A study compares the effect of **four teaching methods** on student performance. The data (marks out of 100) from a CRD with 5 students per method are given below:

| Student | Method 1 | Method 2 | Method 3 | Method 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 65 | 75 | 82 | 70 |
| 2 | 70 | 78 | 88 | 72 |
| 3 | 68 | 72 | 85 | 75 |
| 4 | 72 | 80 | 80 | 68 |
| 5 | 60 | 76 | 84 | 71 |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean scores differ across methods. Given $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Randomization** is the process of allocating treatments to experimental units by a random mechanism (e.g., a random number table or software). Its purpose is twofold:
1. To ensure that the error terms $\epsilon_{ij}$ are **independent** (no hidden correlation).
2. To **break the link** between systematic nuisance factors and treatment assignment, so that the only systematic difference between groups is the treatment itself.

**Blocking** is the technique of grouping experimental units that are similar with respect to a known nuisance variable into homogeneous **blocks**, and then randomizing the application of treatments **within** each block. Examples:
- In agriculture: plots in the same soil-type are blocked together.
- In manufacturing: parts produced on the same machine are blocked together.
- In clinical trials: patients of the same age-group or gender are blocked together.

**Why is blocking effective?** Blocking is effective because it **partitions the total variability** into (a) variability *between blocks* and (b) variability *within blocks* (the residual error). The between-block variability is **removed from the error term** of the F-test for treatments, because it is accounted for by a separate block effect $\beta_j$ in the model. This reduces $MS_E$ and increases the **F-ratio** $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$, thereby increasing the **power** of the test to detect true treatment differences.

Formally, in CRD the error df is $N - a$, while in RCBD it is $(a-1)(b-1)$. Since blocking absorbs $b-1$ df of nuisance variation, the error variance estimate becomes more precise.

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute Totals**:
- $T_1 = 65 + 70 + 68 + 72 + 60 = 335$
- $T_2 = 75 + 78 + 72 + 80 + 76 = 381$
- $T_3 = 82 + 88 + 85 + 80 + 84 = 419$
- $T_4 = 70 + 72 + 75 + 68 + 71 = 356$
- Grand total: $G = 335 + 381 + 419 + 356 = 1491$
- $N = 20$, $a = 4$, $n = 5$, $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 1491/20 = 74.55$

**Step 2 — Correction Factor**:

$$CF = \frac{1491^2}{20} = \frac{2223081}{20} = 111154.05$$

**Step 3 — Sum of All Squared Observations**:

Compute per method:
- Method 1: $65^2 + 70^2 + 68^2 + 72^2 + 60^2 = 4225 + 4900 + 4624 + 5184 + 3600 = 22533$
- Method 2: $75^2 + 78^2 + 72^2 + 80^2 + 76^2 = 5625 + 6084 + 5184 + 6400 + 5776 = 29069$
- Method 3: $82^2 + 88^2 + 85^2 + 80^2 + 84^2 = 6724 + 7744 + 7225 + 6400 + 7056 = 35149$
- Method 4: $70^2 + 72^2 + 75^2 + 68^2 + 71^2 = 4900 + 5184 + 5625 + 4624 + 5041 = 25374$

$$\sum y_{ij}^2 = 22533 + 29069 + 35149 + 25374 = 112125$$

$$SS_T = 112125 - 111154.05 = 970.95$$

**Step 4 — Treatment Sum of Squares**:

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{335^2 + 381^2 + 419^2 + 356^2}{5} - 111154.05$$

- $335^2 = 112225$
- $381^2 = 145161$
- $419^2 = 175561$
- $356^2 = 126736$
- Sum: $112225 + 145161 + 175561 + 126736 = 559683$

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{559683}{5} - 111154.05 = 111936.6 - 111154.05 = 782.55$$

**Step 5 — Error Sum of Squares**:

$$SS_E = 970.95 - 782.55 = 188.40$$

**Step 6 — ANOVA Table**:

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | 782.55 | 3 | 260.85 | 22.15 |
| Error | 188.40 | 16 | 11.775 | — |
| Total | 970.95 | 19 | — | — |

**Step 7 — Decision**:

$F_0 = 260.85 / 11.775 = 22.15$. Since $F_0 = 22.15 > F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion**: There is significant evidence that the mean scores differ across the four teaching methods.

**CV**:

$$CV = \frac{\sqrt{11.775}}{74.55} \times 100\% = \frac{3.432}{74.55} \times 100\% \approx 4.60\%$$

This indicates good experimental precision (CV $< 10\%$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Forgetting to **subtract the correction factor $CF = G^2/N$** when computing $SS_T$ and $SS_{Tr}$. This single error can cost **4–5 marks** out of 7 in a numerical.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the **degrees of freedom**. For CRD, df(Treatments) = $a-1$, df(Error) = $N-a$, df(Total) = $N-1$. For RCBD, df(Error) = $(a-1)(b-1)$. Mixing these up is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: Failing to **state the hypotheses** $H_0$ and $H_1$ explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Reporting the F-ratio without comparing it to the **critical value** or **p-value**. The decision rule must be stated.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: For ANOVA validity assumptions (normality, independence, equal variances — the **"NIVE"** assumptions), some questions award 1 mark for a brief mention. State: "We assume residuals are i.i.d. Normal(0, $\sigma^2$)."
> 6. **Common Mistake #6**: When answering theory, students often write about "Blocking reduces the TOTAL variation" — *incorrect*. Blocking reduces the **error** variation (or equivalently, removes inter-block variability from the error term), not the total variation $SS_T$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **ANOVA** was developed by **R.A. Fisher** in the 1920s for agricultural field trials.
- [x] **Single-Factor Model**: $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}$ with $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ i.i.d.
- [x] **Hypotheses**: $H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_a = 0$ vs $H_1$: at least one $\tau_i \neq 0$.
- [x] **Core Identity**: $SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$ (algebraically exact).
- [x] **CRD ANOVA Table**: df Treatments = $a-1$, df Error = $N-a$, df Total = $N-1$.
- [x] **RCBD ANOVA Table**: df Treatments = $a-1$, df Blocks = $b-1$, df Error = $(a-1)(b-1)$, df Total = $ab-1$.
- [x] **F-Statistic**: $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E \sim F_{a-1,\, N-a}$ under $H_0$.
- [x] **Decision Rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, a-1, N-a}$ or equivalently if p-value $< \alpha$.
- [x] **CV Formula**: $CV = (\sqrt{MS_E}/\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}) \times 100\%$. CV $< 10\%$ = precise; CV $> 30\%$ = poor.
- [x] **Three Principles**: **Replication**, **Randomization**, **Blocking** (Local Control).
- [x] **Blocking reduces error variance**, not total variance; blocks are a *nuisance* factor.
- [x] **Randomization** ensures independence of errors and removes allocation bias.
- [x] **Replication** provides the degrees of freedom to estimate $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **ANOVA Assumptions (NIVE)**: **N**ormality, **I**ndependence, homoscedastic **V**ariance (equal variances), and **E**qual sample sizes (balanced design).
- [x] **Tukey's HSD** is the standard post-hoc test after a significant ANOVA.
- [x] **Computational Shortcuts** (use these in KTU exams to save time):
  - $SS_T = \sum y_{ij}^2 - G^2/N$
  - $SS_{Tr} = \sum T_i^2/n - G^2/N$
  - $SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Tr}$
- [x] **Expected Mean Square (EMS)** for treatment in CRD: $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sum \tau_i^2/(a-1)$. This shows that MST overestimates $\sigma^2$ when treatment effects exist.
- [x] **Fisher's Least Significant Difference (LSD)**: $LSD = t_{\alpha/2, N-a} \sqrt{2 MS_E / n}$ for pairwise comparisons.
- [x] **Family-wise error rate** for $k$ pairwise t-tests: $\alpha_{FW} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^k$. For $k=6, \alpha=0.05$, $\alpha_{FW} \approx 0.265$ — this is *why* ANOVA exists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
