SS_{AB} = \frac{12072}{2} - 5408 - 0 - 50 = 6036 - 5458 = 578
$$

**Degrees of freedom:** $df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1) = 1 \times 1 = 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even though the main effect of Temperature is *zero*, the interaction is **huge** (578). This is the classic signature of **pure interaction** — neither factor matters alone, but together they cause a massive effect. The interpretation: high temperature *helps* with Catalyst 2 but *hurts* with Catalyst 1.

---

### Step 6: Compute $SS_E$ (Error)

$$
SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB} = 784 - 0 - 50 - 578 = 156
$$

**Degrees of freedom:** $df_E = ab(n-1) = 4 \times 1 = 4$

---

### Step 7: Build the Complete ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | $F_{0.05, df_1, df_2}$ |
|--------|-----|----|------|--------|----------------------|
| Factor A (Temp) | 0 | 1 | 0.00 | 0.00 | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| Factor B (Catalyst) | 50 | 1 | 50.00 | 1.28 | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| A × B Interaction | 578 | 1 | 578.00 | **14.82** | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| Error | 156 | 4 | 39.00 | — | — |
| **Total** | **784** | **7** | — | — | — |

**F-statistic computations:**
$$
F_{0,A} = \frac{MS_A}{MS_E} = \frac{0}{39} = 0.00
$$
$$
F_{0,B} = \frac{MS_B}{MS_E} = \frac{50}{39} = 1.28
$$
$$
F_{0,AB} = \frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} = \frac{578}{39} = 14.82
$$

---

### Step 8: Conclusions at $\alpha = 0.05$

- **Factor A:** $F_0 = 0.00 < 7.71$ → **Do not reject $H_0$**. Temperature alone has *no* significant main effect.
- **Factor B:** $F_0 = 1.28 < 7.71$ → **Do not reject $H_0$**. Catalyst alone has *no* significant main effect.
- **A×B Interaction:** $F_0 = 14.82 > 7.71$ → **Reject $H_0$**. The interaction is **highly significant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The yield depends *critically* on the *combination* of temperature and catalyst. Specifically:
> - **Low Temp + Catalyst 1** → High yield (mean 32)
> - **High Temp + Catalyst 1** → Low yield (mean 15)
> - **Low Temp + Catalyst 2** → Low yield (mean 20)
> - **High Temp + Catalyst 2** → High yield (mean 37)
>
> This is a textbook **crossover interaction** — the optimal choice *reverses* depending on the level of the other factor.

---

## 3.3 Estimation of Model Parameters (Bonus)

The least-squares estimates are:
$$
\hat{\mu} = \bar{y}_{\dots} = \frac{208}{8} = 26
$$
$$
\hat{\tau}_1 = \bar{y}_{1..} - \bar{y}_{\dots} = \frac{104}{4} - 26 = 26 - 26 = 0
$$
$$
\hat{\tau}_2 = \bar{y}_{2..} - \bar{y}_{\dots} = \frac{104}{4} - 26 = 0
$$
$$
\hat{\beta}_1 = \bar{y}_{.1.} - \bar{y}_{\dots} = \frac{94}{4} - 26 = 23.5 - 26 = -2.5
$$
$$
\hat{\beta}_2 = \bar{y}_{.2.} - \bar{y}_{\dots} = \frac{114}{4} - 26 = 28.5 - 26 = 2.5
$$
$$
\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{11} = \bar{y}_{11.} - \bar{y}_{1..} - \bar{y}_{.1.} + \bar{y}_{\dots} = 32 - 26 - 23.5 + 26 = 8.5
$$
$$
\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{12} = \bar{y}_{12.} - \bar{y}_{1..} - \bar{y}_{.2.} + \bar{y}_{\dots} = 20 - 26 - 28.5 + 26 = -8.5
$$
$$
\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{21} = \bar{y}_{21.} - \bar{y}_{2..} - \bar{y}_{.1.} + \bar{y}_{\dots} = 15 - 26 - 23.5 + 26 = -8.5
$$
$$
\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{22} = \bar{y}_{22.} - \bar{y}_{2..} - \bar{y}_{.2.} + \bar{y}_{\dots} = 37 - 26 - 28.5 + 26 = 8.5
$$

**Predicted cell means** (e.g., $A_1B_1$): $\hat{\mu} + \hat{\tau}_1 + \hat{\beta}_1 + \widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{11} = 26 + 0 + (-2.5) + 8.5 = 32$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Two-Factor Factorial Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Experiment Initiated]) --> Define[Define Two Factors A and B]
    Define --> LvlA[Select a Levels of Factor A]
    Define --> LvlB[Select b Levels of Factor B]
    LvlA --> Comb[Form a x b Treatment Combinations]
    LvlB --> Comb
    Comb --> Rand[Randomize Run Order]
    Rand --> Rep[Replicate n Times]
    Rep --> Execute[Execute All abn Runs and Record y_ijk]
    Execute --> Comp[Compute Cell, Marginal, and Grand Totals]
    Comp --> CF[Compute Correction Factor CF]
    CF --> SS[Decompose into SS_A, SS_B, SS_AB, SS_E]
    SS --> MS[Compute Mean Squares MS = SS / df]
    MS --> Ftest[Compute F-statistics for A, B, A x B]
    Ftest --> Crit{Compare F0 to F critical}
    Crit -->|Reject H0| Sig[Effect is Significant]
    Crit -->|Do Not Reject| NS[No Significant Effect]
    Sig --> Inter[Examine Interaction Plots]
    NS --> Inter
    Inter --> Opt[Identify Optimal Factor Combination]
    Opt --> End([Experiment Concluded])
```

## 4.2 Data Flow in the ANOVA Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw[Raw Observations y_ijk] --> CellSum[Cell Totals y_ij.]
    Raw --> GrandSum[Grand Total y_...]
    CellSum --> FacASum[Factor A Totals y_i..]
    CellSum --> FacBSum[Factor B Totals y_j..]
    FacASum --> SScalc[Sum of Squares Computation]
    FacBSum --> SScalc
    GrandSum --> SScalc
    Raw --> SScalc
    SScalc --> SSTable[ANOVA Table with F-tests]
    SSTable --> Decision[Statistical and Engineering Decision]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map of Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Effect[Total Effect on Response y] --> MainA[Main Effect of A]
    Effect --> MainB[Main Effect of B]
    Effect --> Inter[Interaction Effect A x B]
    MainA --> M1[Add shift in mean due to changing A]
    MainB --> M2[Add shift in mean due to changing B]
    Inter --> M3[Effect of A that depends on level of B]
    M1 --> Final[Final Response]
    M2 --> Final
    M3 --> Final
```

## 4.4 Hypothetical Interaction Plot (Descriptive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowTemp[Low Temperature A1]
        direction TB
        LP1[Yield at B1 = 32]
        LP2[Yield at B2 = 20]
    end
    subgraph HighTemp[High Temperature A2]
        direction TB
        HP1[Yield at B1 = 15]
        HP2[Yield at B2 = 37]
    end
    LowTemp -.->|Slope Downward| HighTemp
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the worked example above, the **lines cross** when going from Low to High Temperature. **Crossing lines = strong interaction.** Parallel lines = no interaction.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Answers should be brief but precise (typically 50–80 words).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a two-factor factorial design. State the statistical model used to analyze data from such a design.**

**Model Answer:**

A two-factor factorial design is an experimental design in which **two factors A and B** are investigated simultaneously at all possible combinations of their levels (e.g., $a$ levels of A and $b$ levels of B), allowing estimation of main effects and their interaction.

The fixed-effects linear model is:

$$
y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + (\tau\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}
$$

where $i = 1,\ldots,a$; $j = 1,\ldots,b$; $k = 1,\ldots,n$; and $\epsilon_{ijk} \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,\sigma^2)$. Here $\tau_i$, $\beta_j$ are the main effects and $(\tau\beta)_{ij}$ is the interaction effect.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is an interaction effect between two factors? Why is it important in a factorial experiment?**

**Model Answer:**

An **interaction effect** $(\tau\beta)_{ij}$ between two factors A and B exists when the effect of one factor **depends on the level of the other factor**. Mathematically, $(\tau\beta)_{ij} \neq 0$ for some $(i,j)$.

It is important because:
1. **Detection:** Factorial designs are the only designs that allow us to detect interactions. One-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiments cannot.
2. **Interpretation:** If a strong interaction exists, interpreting the main effects in isolation is misleading.
3. **Decision-making:** The optimal combination of factor levels is determined by the interaction, not by main effects alone.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. We provide two full alternative questions, each broken into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A study was conducted to investigate the effect of **Mould Temperature** (A: 2 levels — $A_1 = 120°$C, $A_2 = 150°$C) and **Holding Pressure** (B: 2 levels — $B_1 = 50$ MPa, $B_2 = 80$ MPa) on the **tensile strength** of a polymer. Two replicates were used. The data (in MPa) are:

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 85, 90 | 95, 100 |
| $A_2$ | 110, 115 | 105, 110 |

**(a) Perform a two-factor ANOVA and test for the significance of both main effects and the interaction at $\alpha = 0.05$.** [7 marks]

**(b) Estimate the model parameters $\hat{\mu}, \hat{\tau}_i, \hat{\beta}_j, \widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{ij}$ and provide an engineering interpretation of your findings.** [7 marks]

#### Model Solution to Q3(a)

**Step 1: Cell Totals**
- $y_{11.} = 85 + 90 = 175$
- $y_{12.} = 95 + 100 = 195$
- $y_{21.} = 110 + 115 = 225$
- $y_{22.} = 105 + 110 = 215$

**Step 2: Marginal and Grand Totals**
- $y_{1..} = 175 + 195 = 370$
- $y_{2..} = 225 + 215 = 440$
- $y_{.1.} = 175 + 225 = 400$
- $y_{.2.} = 195 + 215 = 410$
- $y_{\dots} = 400 + 410 = 810$
- $N = 8$, $CF = 810^2 / 8 = 656100/8 = 82012.5$

**Step 3: $SS_T$**
$$
\sum y_{ijk}^{2} = 85^2 + 90^2 + 95^2 + 100^2 + 110^2 + 115^2 + 105^2 + 110^2
$$
$$
= 7225 + 8100 + 9025 + 10000 + 12100 + 13225 + 11025 + 12100 = 82800
$$
$$
SS_T = 82800 - 82012.5 = 787.5
$$

**Step 4: $SS_A$**
$$
SS_A = \frac{370^2 + 440^2}{4} - 82012.5 = \frac{136900 + 193600}{4} - 82012.5 = \frac{330500}{4} - 82012.5 = 82625 - 82012.5 = 612.5
$$

**Step 5: $SS_B$**
$$
SS_B = \frac{400^2 + 410^2}{4} - 82012.5 = \frac{160000 + 168100}{4} - 82012.5 = \frac{328100}{4} - 82012.5 = 82025 - 82012.5 = 12.5
$$

**Step 6: $SS_{AB}$**
$$
\sum y_{ij.}^2 = 175^2 + 195^2 + 225^2 + 215^2 = 30625 + 38025 + 50625 + 46225 = 165500
$$
$$
SS_{AB} = \frac{165500}{2} - 82012.5 - 612.5 - 12.5 = 82750 - 82637.5 = 112.5
$$

**Step 7: $SS_E$**
$$
SS_E = 787.5 - 612.5 - 12.5 - 112.5 = 50
$$

**Step 8: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | $F_{0.05}$ |
|--------|-----|----|------|--------|------------|
| A (Temp) | 612.5 | 1 | 612.50 | **49.00** | $F_{0.05,1,4} = 7.71$ |
| B (Pressure) | 12.5 | 1 | 12.50 | 1.00 | $F_{0.05,1,4} = 7.71$ |
| A × B | 112.5 | 1 | 112.50 | **9.00** | $F_{0.05,1,4} = 7.71$ |
| Error | 50.0 | 4 | 12.50 | — | — |
| Total | 787.5 | 7 | — | — | — |

**Step 9: Conclusion**
- Factor A: $F_0 = 49.00 > 7.71$ → **Significant** (Temperature has a strong main effect).
- Factor B: $F_0 = 1.00 < 7.71$ → **Not significant** (Pressure has no main effect on average).
- A×B: $F_0 = 9.00 > 7.71$ → **Significant** (interaction exists).

#### Model Solution to Q3(b)

**Estimates of model parameters:**

- $\hat{\mu} = 810/8 = 101.25$
- $\hat{\tau}_1 = 370/4 - 101.25 = 92.5 - 101.25 = -8.75$
- $\hat{\tau}_2 = 440/4 - 101.25 = 110 - 101.25 = 8.75$
- $\hat{\beta}_1 = 400/4 - 101.25 = 100 - 101.25 = -1.25$
- $\hat{\beta}_2 = 410/4 - 101.25 = 102.5 - 101.25 = 1.25$

**Interaction estimates:**
- $\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{11} = 175/2 - 92.5 - 100 + 101.25 = 87.5 - 92.5 - 100 + 101.25 = -3.75$
- $\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{12} = 195/2 - 92.5 - 102.5 + 101.25 = 97.5 - 92.5 - 102.5 + 101.25 = 3.75$
- $\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{21} = 225/2 - 110 - 100 + 101.25 = 112.5 - 110 - 100 + 101.25 = 3.75$
- $\widehat{(\tau\beta)}_{22} = 215/2 - 110 - 102.5 + 101.25 = 107.5 - 110 - 102.5 + 101.25 = -3.75$

**Predicted cell means:**
- $\hat{y}_{11.} = 101.25 + (-8.75) + (-1.25) + (-3.75) = 87.5$ ✓
- $\hat{y}_{12.} = 101.25 + (-8.75) + 1.25 + 3.75 = 97.5$ ✓
- $\hat{y}_{21.} = 101.25 + 8.75 + (-1.25) + 3.75 = 112.5$ ✓
- $\hat{y}_{22.} = 101.25 + 8.75 + 1.25 + (-3.75) = 107.5$ ✓

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- High mould temperature ($A_2$) produces higher tensile strength on average (110 vs. 92.5 MPa).
- Holding pressure has little main effect, BUT
- The interaction reveals that **high pressure helps more at low temperature** (3.75) and **hurts slightly at high temperature** (-3.75). This is a **mild crossover interaction**.
- For maximum tensile strength, use **high temperature** with **low-to-moderate pressure** (combination $A_2B_1$ yields 112.5 MPa, the highest).

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) State and explain the assumptions underlying the two-factor fixed-effects ANOVA model. Discuss the consequences of violating the assumption of additivity (no interaction).** [7 marks]

**(b) The following data come from a $2 \times 3$ factorial design (2 levels of Factor A, 3 levels of Factor B) with 3 replicates per cell. Test whether the interaction is significant at $\alpha = 0.01$.** [7 marks]

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $B_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 12, 14, 16 | 18, 20, 22 | 24, 26, 28 |
| $A_2$ | 20, 22, 24 | 22, 24, 26 | 28, 30, 32 |

#### Model Solution to Q4(a)

**Assumptions of the two-factor fixed-effects ANOVA model:**

1. **Normality:** The errors $\epsilon_{ijk}$ are normally distributed: $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity):** All $ab$ cell populations have the **same variance** $\sigma^2$.
3. **Independence:** Observations (and errors) are statistically independent.
4. **Additivity:** The model is *additive in the effects*, i.e., the factors interact only through the $(\tau\beta)_{ij}$ term, and there are no higher-order interactions.
5. **Fixed effects:** The factor levels are *fixed* (not random), and we are interested in *only those* specific levels.
6. **No measurement error** in the factor levels.

**Consequences of Violating Additivity:**
- If a true interaction exists but the model is fitted as additive, the interaction sum of squares gets **absorbed into the error**, inflating $MS_E$.
- This **reduces the F-statistic** and **reduces statistical power**, leading to incorrect "non-significant" conclusions.
- Conversely, $SS_A$ and $SS_B$ become biased because they are confounded with the interaction.
- **Mitigation:** Always include the interaction term in the model when $n \geq 2$ replicates are available.

#### Model Solution to Q4(b)

**Step 1: Cell Totals**
- $y_{11.} = 12 + 14 + 16 = 42$
- $y_{12.} = 18 + 20 + 22 = 60$
- $y_{13.} = 24 + 26 + 28 = 78$
- $y_{21.} = 20 + 22 + 24 = 66$
- $y_{22.} = 22 + 24 + 26 = 72$
- $y_{23.} = 28 + 30 + 32 = 90$

**Step 2: Marginal and Grand Totals**
- $y_{1..} = 42 + 60 + 78 = 180$
- $y_{2..} = 66 + 72 + 90 = 228$
- $y_{.1.} = 42 + 66 = 108$
- $y_{.2.} = 60 + 72 = 132$
- $y_{.3.} = 78 + 90 = 168$
- $y_{\dots} = 180 + 228 = 408$
- $N = abn = 2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18$
- $CF = 408^2/18 = 166464/18 = 9248$

**Step 3: $SS_T$**
$$
\sum y_{ijk}^{2} = (12^2+14^2+16^2) + (18^2+20^2+22^2) + (24^2+26^2+28^2) + (20^2+22^2+24^2) + (22^2+24^2+26^2) + (28^2+30^2+32^2)
$$
$$
= (144+196+256) + (324+400+484) + (576+676+784) + (400+484+576) + (484+576+676) + (784+900+1024)
$$
$$
= 596 + 1208 + 2036 + 1460 + 1736 + 2708 = 9744
$$
$$
SS_T = 9744 - 9248 = 496
$$

**Step 4: $SS_A$**
$$
SS_A = \frac{180^2 + 228^2}{3 \times 3} - 9248 = \frac{32400 + 51984}{9} - 9248 = \frac{84384}{9} - 9248 = 9376 - 9248 = 128
$$

**Step 5: $SS_B$**
$$
SS_B = \frac{108^2 + 132^2 + 168^2}{2 \times 3} - 9248 = \frac{11664 + 17424 + 28224}{6} - 9248 = \frac{57312}{6} - 9248 = 9552 - 9248 = 304
$$

**Step 6: $SS_{AB}$**
$$
\sum y_{ij.}^{2} = 42^2 + 60^2 + 78^2 + 66^2 + 72^2 + 90^2 = 1764 + 3600 + 6084 + 4356 + 5184 + 8100 = 29088
$$
$$
SS_{AB} = \frac{29088}{3} - 9248 - 128 - 304 = 9696 - 9248 - 128 - 304 = 16
$$

**Step 7: $SS_E$**
$$
SS_E = 496 - 128 - 304 - 16 = 48
$$

**Step 8: ANOVA Table (focusing on the interaction)**

| Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | $F_{0.01, df_1, df_2}$ |
|--------|-----|----|------|--------|------------------------|
| A | 128 | 1 | 128.00 | 32.00 | $F_{0.01,1,12} = 9.33$ |
| B | 304 | 2 | 152.00 | 38.00 | $F_{0.01,2,12} = 6.93$ |
| A × B | 16 | 2 | 8.00 | **2.00** | $F_{0.01,2,12} = 6.93$ |
| Error | 48 | 12 | 4.00 | — | — |
| Total | 496 | 17 | — | — | — |

**Step 9: Conclusion for the Interaction**

$$
F_{0, AB} = \frac{8.00}{4.00} = 2.00
$$

Since $F_0 = 2.00 < F_{0.01, 2, 12} = 6.93$, we **do not reject $H_0$** at the 1% significance level. The **A×B interaction is NOT significant**. The model can be reduced to an additive (main-effects-only) two-factor model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Correction Factor ($CF$):** Many students compute $\sum y_{ijk}^{2}$ but forget to subtract $CF$. Result: grossly inflated $SS_T$ → wrong ANOVA → zero marks.
> 2. **Wrong degrees of freedom:** $df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1)$, **not** $ab-1$. For $2 \times 2$, this is 1. For $2 \times 3$, this is 2.
> 3. **Confusing $SS_{AB}$ and $SS_E$:** Some students compute $SS_{AB}$ using the *cell means* formula but forget the $CF$, $SS_A$, $SS_B$ subtractions. Always use: $SS_{AB} = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_{ij.}^{2} - CF - SS_A - SS_B$.
> 4. **Misinterpreting non-significant main effect with significant interaction:** When the interaction is significant, you MUST examine cell means directly. Do **not** interpret main effects in isolation — that is a classic KTU trap.
> 5. **F-critical value:** Use the correct $F_{\alpha, df_{\text{numerator}}, df_{\text{denominator}}}$. The denominator df is always $ab(n-1)$.
> 6. **For KTU 14-mark questions,** show *every* sum of squares calculation step. Skipping intermediate arithmetic will cost 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Two-Factor Factorial Designs**

- ✅ **Model:** $y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + (\tau\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$
- ✅ **Total runs** for $2^2$ design with $n$ replicates = $4n$ (e.g., $n=2$ → 8 runs).
- ✅ **Core Identity:** $SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$
- ✅ **Degrees of freedom:** $N-1 \;=\; (a-1) + (b-1) + (a-1)(b-1) + ab(n-1)$
- ✅ **Three F-tests** must always be performed: A, B, and A×B.
- ✅ **Denominator** for every F-test = $MS_E$ with $df = ab(n-1)$.
- ✅ **CF (Correction Factor)** is the *most-skipped* term — never forget $CF = y_{\dots}^2 / N$.
- ✅ **Interaction is the "star"** of factorial designs — only factorial designs can detect it.
- ✅ **Main effects can be zero** while **interaction is huge** (the worked example above is a case in point).
- ✅ **Parallel lines in interaction plot = no interaction**; **non-parallel (especially crossing) lines = interaction present**.
- ✅ **Assumptions:** normality, homoscedasticity, independence, additivity, fixed effects.
- ✅ **Cell mean formula:** $\bar{y}_{ij.} = y_{ij.}/n$
- ✅ **Marginal mean formula:** $\bar{y}_{i..} = y_{i..}/(bn)$
- ✅ **Grand mean formula:** $\bar{y}_{\dots} = y_{\dots}/(abn)$
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, **always show** the formula, the substitution, the arithmetic, the ANOVA table, and the conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Two-Way Classification** (also called a **Two-Factor Factorial Design**) of Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), the **Effect of Interaction** refers to the joint influence of two independent factors (say, Factor $A$ and Factor $B$) on a dependent response variable, **over and above** the sum of their individual (marginal) main effects.

Mathematically, the **interaction effect** $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ between the $i$-th level of Factor $A$ and the $j$-th level of Factor $B$ is the additional contribution to the response that is not explained by the additive combination of the individual main effects $\alpha_i$ (effect of $A$) and $\beta_j$ (effect of $B$).

The complete linear statistical model with interaction is:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> An interaction is said to exist when the effect of one factor **changes** (in magnitude or direction) across the levels of the other factor. When interactions are present, the main effects alone do not tell the full story, and the researcher **must interpret them jointly**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a pharmaceutical researcher studying the effect of a **new drug (Factor A: Dose)** and **exercise (Factor B: Yes/No)** on patient recovery time.

- **Main Effect of Drug:** On average, higher dose reduces recovery time.
- **Main Effect of Exercise:** On average, exercise reduces recovery time.
- **Interaction Effect:** The drug works *fantastically* when combined with exercise but *poorly* when the patient is sedentary. Here, the effect of the drug **depends on** the level of exercise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** An interaction means **"it depends"**. The effect of Factor A is not constant — it is conditional on the level of Factor B. If you ever find yourself saying, *"Well, it works here but not there,"* you are observing an interaction.

## 1.3 Physical & Statistical Constants

The following standard ANOVA parameters govern interaction analysis:

- **Grand Mean ($\mu$):** **Overall population mean of the response**
- **Main Effect $\alpha_i$:** $\mu_i - \mu$ (deviation of $i$-th level mean from grand mean)
- **Main Effect $\beta_j$:** $\mu_{\cdot j} - \mu$ (deviation of $j$-th level mean from grand mean)
- **Interaction Effect $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$:** $\mu_{ij} - \mu - \alpha_i - \beta_j$
- **Significance Level $\alpha$:** typically **0.05** in KTU board examinations
- **Degrees of Freedom for Interaction:** $(a-1)(b-1)$, where $a$ = levels of A, $b$ = levels of B
- **Critical $F$-value:** $F_{\alpha, (a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)}$ for testing interaction significance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition of the Two-Factor Model

When two factors $A$ and $B$ influence a response variable $Y$, the data can be partitioned into the following structural layers:

### Step 1: The Grand Mean
The **baseline** of the experiment, representing the overall average of all observations.

$$\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_{ijk}$$

### Step 2: Main Effect of Factor A ($\alpha_i$)
The **average deviation** caused by moving from the grand mean to the $i$-th level of Factor $A$, averaged over all levels of $B$.

$$\alpha_i = \mu_{i\cdot} - \mu$$

### Step 3: Main Effect of Factor B ($\beta_j$)
The **average deviation** caused by moving from the grand mean to the $j$-th level of Factor B, averaged over all levels of $A$.

$$\beta_j = \mu_{\cdot j} - \mu$$

### Step 4: Interaction Effect $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$
The **residual joint influence** of $A_i$ and $B_j$ that **cannot be explained** by adding $\alpha_i + \beta_j$ to the grand mean.

$$(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \mu_{ij} - \mu - \alpha_i - \beta_j$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint (Estimability) Condition:**
> In the standard ANOVA model, we enforce: $\sum_{i} \alpha_i = 0$, $\sum_{j} \beta_j = 0$, and $\sum_{i} (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \sum_{j} (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$.

### Step 5: Random Error
$\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ — the unexplained, random variability assumed to be normally and independently distributed.

## 2.2 Three Possible Scenarios

| Scenario | Description | Interaction Plot Pattern |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **No Interaction (Additive Model)** | Effect of A is **constant** across all levels of B. | Parallel lines |
| **Ordinal Interaction** | Effect of A changes in **magnitude** but not direction across B. | Non-parallel, non-intersecting lines |
| **Disordinal Interaction** | Effect of A **reverses direction** across levels of B. | Lines **cross each other** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Component | Formula | Degrees of Freedom | Mean Square |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Between A (Main)** | $SSA = bn \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $a - 1$ | $MSA = \dfrac{SSA}{a-1}$ |
| **Between B (Main)** | $SSB = an \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $b - 1$ | $MSB = \dfrac{SSB}{b-1}$ |
| **Interaction (AB)** | $SSAB = n \sum_i \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ | $MSAB = \dfrac{SSAB}{(a-1)(b-1)}$ |
| **Error (Within)** | $SSE = \sum_i \sum_j \sum_k (Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{ij})^2$ | $ab(n-1)$ | $MSE = \dfrac{SSE}{ab(n-1)}$ |
| **Total** | $SST = \sum (Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $N - 1$ | — |
| **F-statistic for Interaction** | $F_{AB} = \dfrac{MSAB}{MSE}$ | $(a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)$ | — |
| **F-statistic for A** | $F_A = \dfrac{MSA}{MSE}$ | $a-1, ab(n-1)$ | — |
| **F-statistic for B** | $F_B = \dfrac{MSB}{MSE}$ | $b-1, ab(n-1)$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Identity:** $SST = SSA + SSB + SSAB + SSE$ — this is the **fundamental ANOVA decomposition identity** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of interaction effects is critical in:

- **Agricultural Field Trials:** Studying how fertilizer type and irrigation level **jointly** affect crop yield.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Investigating how machine speed and operator skill interact to affect defect rates.
- **Pharmaceutical Bioequivalence:** Determining how a drug's bioavailability changes with both dosage and food intake.
- **Materials Testing:** Understanding how temperature and pressure **interact** to alter material strength.
- **Software Engineering A/B Testing:** Analyzing how UI design and user experience level jointly affect conversion rates.

## 2.5 Hypothesis Testing for Interaction

The formal hypothesis test for the existence of interaction is:

$$H_0: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j \quad \text{(No Interaction)}$$

$$H_1: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} \neq 0 \quad \text{for at least one } (i, j) \quad \text{(Interaction Exists)}$$

**Decision Rule:**

- If $F_{AB} > F_{\alpha, (a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)}$ → **Reject $H_0$** → Interaction is statistically significant.
- If $F_{AB} \leq F_{\alpha, (a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)}$ → **Fail to Reject $H_0$** → No evidence of interaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** Always test for interaction **FIRST** before interpreting main effects. If the interaction is significant, do not interpret the main effects in isolation — interpret the **simple effects** (effect of A at each level of B) instead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Interaction Sum of Squares

We derive $SSAB$ step by step from first principles, using the fundamental identity:

$$Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot} = (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + (\bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) + (Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{ij})$$

This identity partitions the **total deviation** into four orthogonal components:
- Main effect of A
- Main effect of B
- Interaction effect
- Within-cell (error) deviation

**Step 1:** Square both sides and sum over $i, j, k$:

$$\begin{aligned}
SST &= \sum_{i,j,k}(Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 \\
&= bn \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 + an \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 \\
&\quad + n \sum_i \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 \\
&\quad + \sum_{i,j,k}(Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{ij})^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize each term in the expanded expression:

$$\begin{aligned}
SST &= SSA + SSB + SSAB + SSE
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the interaction sum of squares:

$$\begin{aligned}
SSAB &= n \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The four-part decomposition is **orthogonal**, meaning each term is independent and sums exactly to the total. This is the cornerstone of the ANOVA technique.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A textile engineer investigates the effect of **two factors** on fabric tensile strength:
- Factor A: **Weave Type** (3 levels: Plain, Twill, Satin) — $a = 3$
- Factor B: **Dyeing Process** (2 levels: Hot, Cold) — $b = 2$
- $n = 3$ replications per cell
- $\alpha = 0.05$

Suppose the cell means and marginal means are:

| | $B_1$ (Hot) | $B_2$ (Cold) | Row Mean $\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ (Plain) | 52 | 50 | 51 |
| $A_2$ (Twill) | 56 | 48 | 52 |
| $A_3$ (Satin) | 60 | 46 | 53 |
| **Column Mean $\bar{Y}_{\cdot j}$** | **56** | **48** | **$\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 52$** |

**Step 1: Calculate the Grand Mean**

$$\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \frac{51 + 52 + 53}{3} = 52$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Interaction Deviations**

For cell $(A_1, B_1)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{11} - \bar{Y}_{1\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 1} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 52 - 51 - 56 + 52 = -3$$

For cell $(A_1, B_2)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{12} - \bar{Y}_{1\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 2} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 50 - 51 - 48 + 52 = 3$$

For cell $(A_2, B_1)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{21} - \bar{Y}_{2\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 1} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 56 - 52 - 56 + 52 = 0$$

For cell $(A_2, B_2)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{22} - \bar{Y}_{2\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 2} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 48 - 52 - 48 + 52 = 0$$

For cell $(A_3, B_1)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{31} - \bar{Y}_{3\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 1} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 60 - 53 - 56 + 52 = 3$$

For cell $(A_3, B_2)$:
$$(\bar{Y}_{32} - \bar{Y}_{3\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot 2} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}) = 46 - 53 - 48 + 52 = -3$$

**Step 3: Compute $SSAB$ (with $n = 3$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
SSAB &= n \sum_{i,j} (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2 \\
&= 3 \cdot [(-3)^2 + 3^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 3^2 + (-3)^2] \\
&= 3 \cdot [9 + 9 + 0 + 0 + 9 + 9] \\
&= 3 \cdot 36 = 108
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Degrees of Freedom for Interaction**

$$df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$$

**Step 5: Mean Square for Interaction**

$$MSAB = \frac{SSAB}{df_{AB}} = \frac{108}{2} = 54$$

**Step 6: Decision (assuming $MSE = 15$, $df_{error} = ab(n-1) = 12$)**

$$F_{AB} = \frac{MSAB}{MSE} = \frac{54}{15} = 3.60$$

**Critical Value:** $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$

**Conclusion:** Since $F_{AB} = 3.60 < 3.89$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. There is insufficient evidence of an interaction at the 5% significance level. The main effects of Weave Type and Dyeing Process can be interpreted independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Even when $F_{AB}$ is not significant, always report it in the ANOVA table. Examiners deduct marks for omitting the interaction row.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Interaction Effect

The following block diagram maps the **logical flow** of how two factors and their interaction combine to produce a response.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Grand Mean mu] --> nodeAdd
    node2[Main Effect of A alpha_i] --> nodeAdd
    node3[Main Effect of B beta_j] --> nodeAdd
    node4[Interaction Effect alphaBeta_ij] --> nodeAdd
    node5[Random Error epsilon_ijk] --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd[Additive Combination] --> nodeOut
    nodeOut[Observed Response Y_ijk]
    node6[Hypothesis Test F_AB] --> node7{Decision}
    node7 --> node8[Reject H0 - Interaction Exists]
    node7 --> node9[Fail to Reject - No Interaction]
    node8 --> node10[Interpret Simple Effects Only]
    node9 --> node11[Interpret Main Effects Independently]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for ANOVA Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Perform Two-Way ANOVA] --> nodeF[Compute F_AB statistic]
    nodeF --> nodeC{Compare with F critical}
    nodeC -->|F_AB > F_crit| nodeSig[Interaction is SIGNIFICANT]
    nodeC -->|F_AB <= F_crit| nodeNotSig[Interaction NOT Significant]
    nodeSig --> nodeSimple[Examine Simple Effects of A at each level of B]
    nodeNotSig --> nodeMain[Interpret Main Effects of A and B separately]
    nodeMain --> nodeFA[Examine F_A: Is Factor A significant]
    nodeMain --> nodeFB[Examine F_B: Is Factor B significant]
    nodeSimple --> nodePost[Conduct Post-Hoc Tukey HSD Tests]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Interaction Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Two-Factor Experiment] --> nodeQ{Are lines parallel in interaction plot?}
    nodeQ -->|Yes| node2[No Interaction - Additive Model]
    nodeQ -->|No, but no crossing| node3[Ordinal Interaction]
    nodeQ -->|No, lines cross| node4[Disordinal Interaction]
    node2 --> nodeR1[Main effects tell full story]
    node3 --> nodeR2[Main effects partially meaningful]
    node4 --> nodeR3[Main effects MISLEADING - must use simple effects]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Interaction Plot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1[Level A1] --> nodeMean1[Cell Mean Y_ij]
    nodeA2[Level A2] --> nodeMean1
    nodeA3[Level A3] --> nodeMean1
    nodeB1[Level B1] --> nodeMean1
    nodeB2[Level B2] --> nodeMean1
    nodeMean1 --> nodeEffect[Decompose into Main + Interaction + Error]
    nodeEffect --> nodePlot[Plot Y_ij vs Factor A for each B level]
    nodePlot --> nodeResult{Line Pattern}
    nodeResult -->|Parallel| nodePara[No Interaction]
    nodeResult -->|Converging| nodeOrd[Ordinal]
    nodeResult -->|Crossing| nodeDis[Disordinal]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define interaction effect in a two-way ANOVA. When do we say that two factors interact?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In a two-way ANOVA, the **interaction effect** $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ between the $i$-th level of factor $A$ and the $j$-th level of factor $B$ is the **additional** contribution to the response that is **not explained** by the additive combination of their individual main effects $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_j$.

Mathematically:
$$(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \mu_{ij} - \mu - \alpha_i - \beta_j$$

Two factors are said to **interact** when the effect of one factor **depends on the level** of the other factor. That is, the difference in response between levels of factor $A$ is **not the same** across all levels of factor $B$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between ordinal and disordinal interaction with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Ordinal Interaction | Disordinal Interaction |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Definition** | The ranking of factor levels is **preserved** across levels of the other factor, but the magnitude of differences changes. | The **ranking reverses** — one factor is beneficial at some levels and harmful at others. |
| **Plot Pattern** | Non-parallel, **non-intersecting** lines | Lines **cross** each other |
| **Main Effects** | Still meaningful and interpretable | **Misleading** when interpreted alone |

**Example (Ordinal):** A drug reduces blood pressure at all ages, but more strongly in younger patients — the direction is consistent, but the magnitude varies.

**Example (Disordinal):** A fertilizer increases wheat yield in sandy soil but decreases yield in clay soil — the direction **reverses** depending on soil type.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **mathematical model** of a two-way ANOVA with interaction. Define all the terms and state the constraint conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to study the effect of **three catalysts (A1, A2, A3)** and **two temperatures (B1, B2)** on the yield of a chemical reaction, the following data are obtained (3 replications per cell):

| | B1 | B2 |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | 10, 12, 14 | 8, 9, 10 |
| A2 | 13, 14, 15 | 11, 12, 13 |
| A3 | 16, 17, 18 | 14, 15, 16 |

Perform a two-way ANOVA with interaction and test for the **significance of interaction** at $\alpha = 0.05$. Given $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Model Equation:**
$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

**Term Definitions:**

- $Y_{ijk}$ = observation in the $k$-th replicate of the $i$-th level of $A$ and $j$-th level of $B$
- $\mu$ = overall grand mean
- $\alpha_i$ = main effect of $i$-th level of factor $A$
- $\beta_j$ = main effect of $j$-th level of factor $B$
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect of $(i,j)$-th cell
- $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ = random error, $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

**Constraint Conditions:**
- $\sum_{i=1}^{a} \alpha_i = 0$
- $\sum_{j=1}^{b} \beta_j = 0$
- $\sum_{i=1}^{a} (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$ for all $j$
- $\sum_{j=1}^{b} (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$ for all $i$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Compute Cell Means and Totals**

Cell totals:
- A1B1 = 36, A1B2 = 27
- A2B1 = 42, A2B2 = 36
- A3B1 = 51, A3B2 = 45

Cell means:
- A1B1 = 12, A1B2 = 9
- A2B1 = 14, A2B2 = 12
- A3B1 = 17, A3B2 = 15

**Step 2: Compute Grand Mean**

$$\bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \frac{36+27+42+36+51+45}{18} = \frac{237}{18} = 13.1667$$

**Step 3: Row and Column Means**

Row means: $\bar{Y}_{1\cdot} = 10.5$, $\bar{Y}_{2\cdot} = 13$, $\bar{Y}_{3\cdot} = 16$

Column means: $\bar{Y}_{\cdot 1} = 14.333$, $\bar{Y}_{\cdot 2} = 12$

**Step 4: Compute $SSAB$**

Interaction deviations $(\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{A1B1: } & 12 - 10.5 - 14.333 + 13.167 = 0.333 \\
\text{A1B2: } & 9 - 10.5 - 12 + 13.167 = -0.333 \\
\text{A2B1: } & 14 - 13 - 14.333 + 13.167 = -0.167 \\
\text{A2B2: } & 12 - 13 - 12 + 13.167 = 0.167 \\
\text{A3B1: } & 17 - 16 - 14.333 + 13.167 = -0.167 \\
\text{A3B2: } & 15 - 16 - 12 + 13.167 = 0.167
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
SSAB &= 3 \cdot (0.333^2 + (-0.333)^2 + (-0.167)^2 + 0.167^2 + (-0.167)^2 + 0.167^2) \\
&= 3 \cdot (0.111 + 0.111 + 0.028 + 0.028 + 0.028 + 0.028) \\
&= 3 \cdot 0.333 = 1.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute $SSA$**

$$\begin{aligned}
SSA &= 6 \cdot [(10.5 - 13.167)^2 + (13 - 13.167)^2 + (16 - 13.167)^2] \\
&= 6 \cdot [7.111 + 0.028 + 8.028] \\
&= 6 \cdot 15.167 = 91.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compute $SSB$**

$$\begin{aligned}
SSB &= 9 \cdot [(14.333 - 13.167)^2 + (12 - 13.167)^2] \\
&= 9 \cdot [1.361 + 1.361] = 9 \cdot 2.722 = 24.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Compute $SST$**

$$\begin{aligned}
SST &= \sum (Y_{ijk} - 13.167)^2 = 188.83
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Compute $SSE$**

$$SSE = SST - SSA - SSB - SSAB = 188.83 - 91.0 - 24.5 - 1.0 = 72.33$$

**Step 9: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|--------|------|------|------|------|
| Factor A | 91.0 | 2 | 45.5 | 7.55 |
| Factor B | 24.5 | 1 | 24.5 | 4.07 |
| Interaction AB | 1.0 | 2 | 0.5 | 0.083 |
| Error | 72.33 | 12 | 6.03 | — |
| Total | 188.83 | 17 | — | — |

**Step 10: Decision on Interaction**

$$F_{AB} = 0.083 < F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$$

**Conclusion:** **Fail to reject $H_0$**. There is **no significant interaction** between catalyst and temperature. The main effects can be interpreted independently.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **interaction plot**. How is it used to **graphically detect** the presence of an interaction between two factors? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **ordinal and disordinal interactions**? Construct a hypothetical $2 \times 2$ dataset (with cell means only) that illustrates each type, and explain how the interaction plot would look in each case. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Interaction Plot:**
An **interaction plot** (also called a **profile plot**) is a graphical tool used in factorial designs to visually detect the presence and nature of interaction between two factors. The cell mean response is plotted on the **y-axis**, the levels of Factor $A$ are on the **x-axis**, and **separate lines** are drawn for each level of Factor $B$.

**How it Detects Interaction:**

- **Parallel Lines → No Interaction:** The effect of factor $A$ is the same at all levels of $B$ (additive model).
- **Non-Parallel but Non-Crossing Lines → Ordinal Interaction:** The effect of $A$ changes in magnitude but not direction across $B$.
- **Crossing Lines → Disordinal Interaction:** The effect of $A$ reverses direction across levels of $B$.

**Construction Procedure:**
1. Compute the cell mean $\bar{Y}_{ij}$ for each combination.
2. Place levels of $A$ on the x-axis and cell means on the y-axis.
3. Draw one line per level of $B$, connecting the cell means.
4. Visually inspect the **slope** and **intersection pattern** of the lines.

**Decision Rule:** The more **non-parallel** the lines, the **stronger** the interaction.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Ordinal Interaction Example:**

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 20 | 15 |
| $A_2$ | 30 | 20 |

Here, $A_2$ gives higher response than $A_1$ at both levels of $B$, but the **gap is larger** at $B_1$ (gap = 10) than at $B_2$ (gap = 5). The lines do **not cross**; they are non-parallel but preserve the ordering. This is **ordinal interaction**.

**Disordinal Interaction Example:**

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 20 | 35 |
| $A_2$ | 30 | 25 |

At $B_1$, $A_2 > A_1$. At $B_2$, $A_1 > A_2$. The **ranking reverses** — the lines **cross**. This is a **disordinal interaction**, indicating that the effect of $A$ **completely reverses** depending on the level of $B$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often interpret main effects even when the interaction is significant. Always **test and interpret interaction FIRST**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **constraint conditions** ($\sum \alpha_i = 0$, $\sum \beta_j = 0$, $\sum (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$) in the model definition — costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **ordinal** with **disordinal** interaction. Remember: *crossing lines = disordinal; converging/diverging lines = ordinal*.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting the interaction row from the ANOVA table — even if not significant, the interaction $SS$ and $df$ **must** be reported.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Using incorrect degrees of freedom: $df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1)$ — not $(a-1) + (b-1)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Interactions**

- **Model:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$
- **Interaction Definition:** Effect of A that **depends on the level of B**.
- **Three types:** No interaction (parallel lines), Ordinal (non-parallel, non-crossing), Disordinal (crossing lines).
- **Degrees of Freedom for Interaction:** $(a-1)(b-1)$.
- **Hypothesis Test:** $H_0: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$ for all $i, j$.
- **F-statistic:** $F_{AB} = \dfrac{MSAB}{MSE}$ with $df = [(a-1)(b-1), ab(n-1)]$.
- **SS Identity:** $SST = SSA + SSB + SSAB + SSE$ (must be **orthogonal**).
- **SSAB Formula:** $SSAB = n \sum_i \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$
- **Order of Interpretation:** Test interaction **first** → if significant, interpret **simple effects**; if not significant, interpret **main effects**.
- **Constraint Conditions:** $\sum \alpha_i = 0$, $\sum \beta_j = 0$, $\sum_i (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$, $\sum_j (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$.
- **Significance Level:** Use $\alpha = 0.05$ unless otherwise specified in KTU board exams.
- **Practical Implication:** Significant interaction means factors do **not act independently** — the joint effect must be studied.
- **Tukey's One-df Test:** Used when interaction has only 1 degree of freedom (i.e., $2 \times 2$ design) to test for non-additivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ANOVA in Factorial Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ANOVA in Factorial Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) in Factorial Design** is a statistical methodology used to simultaneously examine the effects of two or more independent categorical variables (called **factors**) and their **interaction effects** on a continuous response variable. In a **two-factor factorial design**, every level of factor *A* is combined with every level of factor *B*, producing a complete crossed structure of *a × b* treatment combinations, each replicated *n* times, yielding a total of *N = a × b × n* observations.

The total observed variability in the response is partitioned into distinct, statistically independent components:

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{A} + SS_{B} + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}$$

where each sum of squares (SS) corresponds to a unique source of variation attributable to factor *A*, factor *B*, the **interaction effect** *AB*, and the **residual (within-cell) error**, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the **two-way ANOVA (two-factor) with interaction** model. Students must master the decomposition of variability, the construction of the ANOVA table, and the interpretation of F-statistics for **main effects and interaction effects** in equal-replication designs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Recipe Experiment

Imagine a chef trying to perfect a curry recipe. She has two factors to study:

- **Factor A: Spice Level** (Low, High) — 2 levels (*a = 2*)
- **Factor B: Cooking Time** (Short, Long) — 2 levels (*b = 2*)

She makes **3 batches** (*n = 3*) at every possible combination, giving 12 curry samples. After sensory scoring each batch, she asks three questions:

1. *Does* **spice level** *alone affect the score?* → This is the **main effect of A**.
2. *Does* **cooking time** *alone affect the score?* → This is the **main effect of B**.
3. *Does the combination* **matter** *— e.g., does high spice only taste great when cooked long?* → This is the **interaction A×B**.

**ANOVA in Factorial Design** is precisely the mathematical tool that slices the total "score variability" of the 12 samples into four neat piles, one for each question, and tells her — with a confidence number — which piles are big enough to claim a real effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A *factorial* design is called "factorial" because it tests ALL combinations, not just one factor at a time. This is what allows the *interaction* effect to be detected — something a one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) experiment can never reveal.

## 1.3 Notation Conventions (KTU Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| *a* | Number of levels of factor A |
| *b* | Number of levels of factor B |
| *n* | Number of replicates per cell |
| *N* | Total observations = *a × b × n* |
| $x_{ijk}$ | *k*-th observation in cell (i, j) |
| $\bar{x}_{ij.}$ | Mean of cell (i, j) |
| $\bar{x}_{i..}$ | Mean across levels of B for level *i* of A |
| $\bar{x}_{.j.}$ | Mean across levels of A for level *j* of B |
| $\bar{x}_{...}$ | Grand mean of all *N* observations |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Model (Fixed-Effects Two-Way ANOVA with Interaction)

For the *k*-th replicate in the cell formed by the *i*-th level of A and the *j*-th level of B, the observation is modeled as:

$$x_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = overall (grand) mean effect
- $\alpha_i$ = main effect of the *i*-th level of factor A
- $\beta_j$ = main effect of the *j*-th level of factor B
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect of level *i* of A with level *j* of B
- $\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ = random error, independent and identically distributed

**Constraint conditions** (to ensure identifiability):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{a}\alpha_i = 0, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{b}\beta_j = 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \text{ for each } j, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{b}(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \text{ for each } i$$

## 2.2 The Logic of Variability Decomposition (Step-by-Step)

The methodological "why" behind the ANOVA breakdown:

- **Step 1 — Capture Total Variability:** Compute $SS_{Total}$, the sum of squared deviations of every observation from the grand mean. This is the *raw* noise the experiment must explain.
- **Step 2 — Allocate to Factor A:** Compare the means of the *a* rows. If they differ a lot, factor A is doing something. The squared spread, weighted by *bn*, is $SS_A$.
- **Step 3 — Allocate to Factor B:** Compare the means of the *b* columns. If they differ a lot, factor B is doing something. The squared spread, weighted by *an*, is $SS_B$.
- **Step 4 — Allocate to Interaction:** After removing the row and column means, the residual cell-mean variation that **cannot** be explained by A or B alone signals an interaction. The squared pattern mismatch, weighted by *n*, is $SS_{AB}$.
- **Step 5 — Allocate to Error:** Whatever remains — the spread of replicates *within* a single cell — is $SS_E$, the irreducible experimental noise used as the benchmark.
- **Step 6 — Test Each Effect:** Form the **F-ratio** = (MS of effect) / (MS of error). If the F-ratio is large relative to $F_{critical}$ from the F-distribution, the effect is **statistically significant**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In manufacturing, the interaction effect often reveals whether a process parameter (e.g., temperature) and a material property (e.g., humidity) produce a *non-additive* joint influence on product strength — information critical for robust design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom** |
|---|---|---|
| $SS_{Total}$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{ijk} - \bar{x}_{...})^2$ | $abn - 1$ |
| $SS_A$ | $\displaystyle bn\sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{x}_{i..} - \bar{x}_{...})^2$ | $a - 1$ |
| $SS_B$ | $\displaystyle an\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{x}_{.j.} - \bar{x}_{...})^2$ | $b - 1$ |
| $SS_{AB}$ | $\displaystyle n\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{x}_{ij.} - \bar{x}_{i..} - \bar{x}_{.j.} + \bar{x}_{...})^2$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ |
| $SS_{Error}$ | $\displaystyle SS_{Total} - SS_A - SS_B - SS_{AB}$ | $ab(n-1)$ |
| $MS_A$ | $SS_A / (a-1)$ | — |
| $MS_B$ | $SS_B / (b-1)$ | — |
| $MS_{AB}$ | $SS_{AB} / [(a-1)(b-1)]$ | — |
| $MS_E$ | $SS_E / [ab(n-1)]$ | — |
| $F_A$ | $MS_A / MS_E$ | $(a-1,\, ab(n-1))$ |
| $F_B$ | $MS_B / MS_E$ | $(b-1,\, ab(n-1))$ |
| $F_{AB}$ | $MS_{AB} / MS_E$ | $((a-1)(b-1),\, ab(n-1))$ |

**Decision Rule (at significance level $\alpha$):**
- Reject $H_0$ for factor A if $F_A > F_{\alpha,\, a-1,\, ab(n-1)}$
- Reject $H_0$ for factor B if $F_B > F_{\alpha,\, b-1,\, ab(n-1)}$
- Reject $H_0$ for interaction if $F_{AB} > F_{\alpha,\, (a-1)(b-1),\, ab(n-1)}$

## 2.4 Computational Shortcut (Raw-Data Form, KTU-Favored for Speed)

When data is small, the *correction factor* approach speeds computation:

$$C = \frac{T^2}{abn}$$

$$SS_{Total} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\sum_{k}x_{ijk}^2 - C$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{bn}\sum_{i}T_{i..}^2 - C$$

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{an}\sum_{j}T_{.j.}^2 - C$$

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i}\sum_{j}T_{ij.}^2 - C$$

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$$

$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells}$$

where $T = \sum x_{ijk}$, $T_{i..}$ = row *i* total, $T_{.j.}$ = column *j* total, $T_{ij.}$ = cell (*i*,*j*) total.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This raw-data shortcut is exactly what appears in KTU numerical problems. The correction factor $C$ absorbs the grand mean in one shot, leaving only squared deviations to be computed — a *huge* time saver in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Justification of the Sum-of-Squares Decomposition

The decomposition identity

$$SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$$

is **not assumed** — it is algebraically derivable. We show this below.

**Starting point:** Define the identity for any observation $x_{ijk}$:

$$x_{ijk} - \bar{x}_{...} = (\bar{x}_{i..} - \bar{x}_{...}) + (\bar{x}_{.j.} - \bar{x}_{...}) + (\bar{x}_{ij.} - \bar{x}_{i..} - \bar{x}_{.j.} + \bar{x}_{...}) + (x_{ijk} - \bar{x}_{ij.})$$

Squaring both sides and summing over all *i, j, k*:

- The **cross-product terms vanish** because $\sum_j (\bar{x}_{.j.} - \bar{x}_{...}) = 0$, $\sum_i (\bar{x}_{i..} - \bar{x}_{...}) = 0$, etc. (This is the *orthogonality* of factorial designs.)
- Squaring and summing each of the four bracketed terms reproduces the four sum-of-squares on the right side.

Hence the partition is an exact algebraic identity — the foundation that makes factorial ANOVA a valid decomposition.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (2 × 2 Factorial, *n* = 3)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A chemical engineer studies the effect of two factors on the yield (%) of a reaction:

- **Factor A (Temperature):** A1 = 60 °C, A2 = 80 °C  →  *a = 2*
- **Factor B (Catalyst):** B1 = Type I, B2 = Type II  →  *b = 2*
- **Replicates:** *n = 3* per cell, total *N = 12* runs

**Raw data table** (yield in %):

| Cell | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Rep 3 | Cell Total $T_{ij.}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1B1 | 12 | 15 | 14 | **41** |
| A1B2 | 18 | 17 | 20 | **55** |
| A2B1 | 22 | 25 | 21 | **68** |
| A2B2 | 28 | 30 | 27 | **85** |

**Marginal totals and means:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{1..} &= 41 + 55 = 96, \quad \bar{x}_{1..} = 96/6 = 16.000 \\
T_{2..} &= 68 + 85 = 153, \quad \bar{x}_{2..} = 153/6 = 25.500 \\
T_{.1.} &= 41 + 68 = 109, \quad \bar{x}_{.1.} = 109/6 = 18.167 \\
T_{.2.} &= 55 + 85 = 140, \quad \bar{x}_{.2.} = 140/6 = 23.333 \\
T_{...} &= 41 + 55 + 68 + 85 = 249, \quad \bar{x}_{...} = 249/12 = 20.750
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Correction Factor

$$C = \frac{T^2}{abn} = \frac{249^2}{2 \times 2 \times 3} = \frac{62001}{12} = 5166.750$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — $SS_{Total}$

Sum of squares of all raw observations:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x_{ijk}^2 &= 12^2 + 15^2 + 14^2 + 18^2 + 17^2 + 20^2 \\
&\quad + 22^2 + 25^2 + 21^2 + 28^2 + 30^2 + 27^2 \\
&= 144 + 225 + 196 + 324 + 289 + 400 \\
&\quad + 484 + 625 + 441 + 784 + 900 + 729 \\
&= 5541
\end{aligned}$$

$$SS_{Total} = 5541 - 5166.750 = 374.250$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — $SS_A$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_A &= \frac{1}{bn}\sum_i T_{i..}^2 - C = \frac{1}{6}\left(96^2 + 153^2\right) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{1}{6}(9216 + 23409) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{32625}{6} - 5166.750 \\
&= 5437.500 - 5166.750 = 270.750
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — $SS_B$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= \frac{1}{an}\sum_j T_{.j.}^2 - C = \frac{1}{6}\left(109^2 + 140^2\right) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{1}{6}(11881 + 19600) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{31481}{6} - 5166.750 \\
&= 5246.833 - 5166.750 = 80.083
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — $SS_{Cells}$ and $SS_{AB}$

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{Cells} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_i\sum_j T_{ij.}^2 - C \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(41^2 + 55^2 + 68^2 + 85^2) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(1681 + 3025 + 4624 + 7225) - 5166.750 \\
&= \frac{16555}{3} - 5166.750 \\
&= 5518.333 - 5166.750 = 351.583
\end{aligned}$$

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B = 351.583 - 270.750 - 80.083 = 0.750$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — $SS_{Error}$

$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells} = 374.250 - 351.583 = 22.667$$

**Cross-check using the alternative within-cell formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_E &= \sum_i\sum_j\sum_k (x_{ijk} - \bar{x}_{ij.})^2 \\
&= \left[(12-13.667)^2 + (15-13.667)^2 + (14-13.667)^2\right] \\
&\quad + \left[(18-18.333)^2 + (17-18.333)^2 + (20-18.333)^2\right] \\
&\quad + \left[(22-22.667)^2 + (25-22.667)^2 + (21-22.667)^2\right] \\
&\quad + \left[(28-28.333)^2 + (30-28.333)^2 + (27-28.333)^2\right] \\
&= 2.778 + 1.778 + 0.111 + 0.111 + 1.778 + 2.778 \\
&\quad + 0.444 + 5.444 + 2.778 + 0.111 + 2.778 + 1.778 \\
&= 22.667 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Construct the Complete ANOVA Table

| **Source of Variation** | **SS** | **df** | **MS** | **F-ratio** | **$F_{0.05, \cdot, 8}$** | **Decision** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Factor A (Temperature) | 270.750 | 1 | 270.750 | **95.60** | 5.32 | **Significant** |
| Factor B (Catalyst) | 80.083 | 1 | 80.083 | **28.28** | 5.32 | **Significant** |
| Interaction A×B | 0.750 | 1 | 0.750 | **0.26** | 5.32 | Not Significant |
| Error (Within Cells) | 22.667 | 8 | 2.833 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **374.250** | **11** | — | — | — | — |

### 3.2.9 Step 8 — Interpretation (Engineering Verdict)

- **Temperature has a very strong effect** on yield: increasing from 60 °C to 80 °C raises yield by roughly 9.5 percentage points on average.
- **Catalyst type has a moderate effect**: switching from Type I to Type II gives ~5.2 percentage points gain.
- **No detectable interaction**: the effect of temperature appears consistent across both catalyst types (and vice versa). The two factors act *additively* on yield.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The engineer can safely optimize temperature and catalyst *independently*. Since no interaction exists, the optimal setting is simply the combination of *best temperature* (80 °C) with *best catalyst* (Type II) — yielding the highest expected response.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Flow of Two-Way ANOVA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT RAW DATA a x b CELLS WITH n REPLICATES"] --> B["COMPUTE MARGINALS T_i ROW, T_j COL, T_ij CELL, T GRAND"]
    B --> C["CALCULATE CORRECTION FACTOR C EQUALS T SQUARED DIVIDED BY a b n"]
    C --> D["CALCULATE SS TOTAL EQUALS SUM X SQUARED MINUS C"]
    C --> E["CALCULATE SS A EQUALS SUM T_i SQUARED DIVIDED BY b n MINUS C"]
    C --> F["CALCULATE SS B EQUALS SUM T_j SQUARED DIVIDED BY a n MINUS C"]
    C --> G["CALCULATE SS CELLS EQUALS SUM T_ij SQUARED DIVIDED BY n MINUS C"]
    G --> H["SS AB EQUALS SS CELLS MINUS SS A MINUS SS B"]
    D --> I["SS ERROR EQUALS SS TOTAL MINUS SS CELLS"]
    E --> J["BUILD ANOVA TABLE WITH df, MS, F RATIOS"]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{"COMPARE F WITH F CRITICAL AT ALPHA EQUALS 0 POINT 0 5"}
    K -->|F A exceeds critical| L["REJECT H ZERO FACTOR A SIGNIFICANT"]
    K -->|F B exceeds critical| M["REJECT H ZERO FACTOR B SIGNIFICANT"]
    K -->|F AB exceeds critical| N["REJECT H ZERO INTERACTION SIGNIFICANT"]
    K -->|All F below critical| O["FAIL TO REJECT ALL EFFECTS NOT SIGNIFICANT"]
    L --> P["ENGINEERING INTERPRETATION AND OPTIMIZATION"]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Source-of-Variation Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["TOTAL VARIABILITY SS TOTAL"] --> S2["BETWEEN TREATMENT VARIABILITY SS CELLS"]
    S1 --> S3["WITHIN TREATMENT VARIABILITY SS ERROR"]
    S2 --> S4["MAIN EFFECT A SS A"]
    S2 --> S5["MAIN EFFECT B SS B"]
    S2 --> S6["INTERACTION EFFECT AB SS AB"]
    S3 --> S7["REPLICATE NOISE INSIDE EACH CELL"]
    S4 --> T1["TESTS IF ROW MEANS DIFFER"]
    S5 --> T2["TESTS IF COLUMN MEANS DIFFER"]
    S6 --> T3["TESTS IF CELL PATTERN DEVIATES FROM ROW PLUS COLUMN ADDITIVE MODEL"]
    S7 --> T4["BASELINE NOISE FOR ALL F TESTS"]
```

## 4.3 Factorial Structure: Crossed Levels of Two Factors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["FACTOR A LEVEL 1"] --> AB11["CELL A1 B1"]
    F1 --> AB12["CELL A1 B2"]
    F1 --> AB13["CELL A1 B3"]
    F2["FACTOR A LEVEL 2"] --> AB21["CELL A2 B1"]
    F2 --> AB22["CELL A2 B2"]
    F2 --> AB23["CELL A2 B3"]
    F3["FACTOR A LEVEL 3"] --> AB31["CELL A3 B1"]
    F3 --> AB32["CELL A3 B2"]
    F3 --> AB33["CELL A3 B3"]
    G1["FACTOR B LEVEL 1"] --> AB11
    G1 --> AB21
    G1 --> AB31
    G2["FACTOR B LEVEL 2"] --> AB12
    G2 --> AB22
    G2 --> AB32
    G3["FACTOR B LEVEL 3"] --> AB13
    G3 --> AB23
    G3 --> AB33
    AB11 --> REP1["n REPLICATES PER CELL"]
    AB12 --> REP1
    AB13 --> REP1
    AB21 --> REP1
    AB22 --> REP1
    AB23 --> REP1
    AB31 --> REP1
    AB32 --> REP1
    AB33 --> REP1
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Interpreting the Completed ANOVA Table

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ANOVA TABLE COMPLETED"] --> A1{"IS F AB SIGNIFICANT"}
    A1 -->|YES| B1["INTERACTION EXISTS FACTORS DO NOT ACT INDEPENDENTLY"]
    B1 --> B2["INTERPRET MAIN EFFECTS WITH CAUTION OR NOT AT ALL"]
    B2 --> B3["PLOT INTERACTION PROFILE TO FIND OPTIMUM COMBINATION"]
    A1 -->|NO| C1["NO INTERACTION FACTORS ACT ADDITIVELY"]
    C1 --> C2{"IS F A SIGNIFICANT"}
    C2 -->|YES| D1["FACTOR A HAS MAIN EFFECT ON RESPONSE"]
    C2 -->|NO| D2["FACTOR A HAS NO DETECTABLE EFFECT"]
    C1 --> C3{"IS F B SIGNIFICANT"}
    C3 -->|YES| E1["FACTOR B HAS MAIN EFFECT ON RESPONSE"]
    C3 -->|NO| E2["FACTOR B HAS NO DETECTABLE EFFECT"]
    D1 --> F1["OPTIMIZE BY CHOOSING BEST LEVEL OF A INDEPENDENTLY"]
    D2 --> F2["IGNORE FACTOR A IN OPTIMIZATION"]
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define a two-factor factorial design. State the statistical model and identify the terms that measure the interaction between two factors.**

**Model Answer:**

A two-factor factorial design is an experimental plan in which every level of factor *A* is tested in combination with every level of factor *B*, producing a complete set of *a × b* treatment cells, each replicated *n* times. The fixed-effects statistical model is:

$$x_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

The interaction term $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ measures the *joint* effect of level *i* of A with level *j* of B that *cannot* be attributed to either factor alone. When $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \ne 0$, the effect of A depends on the level of B (and vice versa) — i.e., the factors do not act additively.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**State the hypotheses tested by the F-ratio for the main effect of factor A and for the A×B interaction in a two-way ANOVA.**

**Model Answer:**

**For the main effect of factor A:**

$$H_0: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \cdots = \alpha_a = 0 \quad \text{(Factor A has no effect)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } \alpha_i \ne 0 \quad \text{(Factor A has an effect)}$$

**For the A × B interaction:**

$$H_0: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \;\; \forall\; i,j \quad \text{(No interaction; factors act additively)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } (\alpha\beta)_{ij} \ne 0 \quad \text{(Interaction exists)}$$

The test statistic in both cases is $F = MS_{effect} / MS_E$, compared against $F_{\alpha, df_1, df_2}$ from the F-distribution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Marks Loss:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract *C*** when computing $SS_{Total}$, $SS_A$, $SS_B$ — leads to inflated SS and wrong F-ratios (**−2 to −3 marks**).
> 2. **Wrong degrees of freedom:** Using $ab - 1$ instead of $ab(n-1)$ for the error df is the single most frequent error (**−1 mark**).
> 3. **Reporting the conclusion without comparing to the F-critical value** from the F-table — students often say "significant" without ever mentioning the critical value (**−1 mark**).
> 4. **Interpreting the interaction first** before checking main effects — a significant interaction *changes* how main effects should be read; many students miss this nuance (**−2 marks**).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A textile engineer studies the effect of **weave type (A: Plain, Twill)** and **dye temperature (B: 60 °C, 90 °C)** on cloth **color-fastness rating**. Three replicate samples are tested in each cell, yielding the following data:

| Cell | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Rep 3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1B1 (Plain, 60 °C) | 28 | 32 | 30 |
| A1B2 (Plain, 90 °C) | 36 | 34 | 38 |
| A2B1 (Twill, 60 °C) | 42 | 40 | 44 |
| A2B2 (Twill, 90 °C) | 50 | 48 | 52 |

Set up the ANOVA table at $\alpha = 0.05$ and test for the significance of factor A, factor B, and the A×B interaction. Use $F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cell, row, column, and grand totals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{11} &= 28+32+30 = 90 \\
T_{12} &= 36+34+38 = 108 \\
T_{21} &= 42+40+44 = 126 \\
T_{22} &= 50+48+52 = 150 \\
T_{1..} &= 90+108 = 198,\quad T_{2..} = 126+150 = 276 \\
T_{.1.} &= 90+126 = 216,\quad T_{.2.} = 108+150 = 258 \\
T_{...} &= 198+276 = 474
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Correction factor:**

$$C = \frac{474^2}{2 \times 2 \times 3} = \frac{224676}{12} = 18723.000$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squares of raw observations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x^2 &= 28^2+32^2+30^2+36^2+34^2+38^2 \\
&\quad +42^2+40^2+44^2+50^2+48^2+52^2 \\
&= 784+1024+900+1296+1156+1444+1764+1600+1936+2500+2304+2704 \\
&= 19412
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — $SS_{Total}$:**

$$SS_{Total} = 19412 - 18723 = 689.000$$

**Step 5 — $SS_A$:**

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{6}(198^2 + 276^2) - 18723 = \frac{1}{6}(39204 + 76176) - 18723 = \frac{115380}{6} - 18723 = 19230 - 18723 = 507.000$$

**Step 6 — $SS_B$:**

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{6}(216^2 + 258^2) - 18723 = \frac{1}{6}(46656 + 66564) - 18723 = \frac{113220}{6} - 18723 = 18870 - 18723 = 147.000$$

**Step 7 — $SS_{Cells}$ and $SS_{AB}$:**

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{1}{3}(90^2 + 108^2 + 126^2 + 150^2) - 18723 = \frac{1}{3}(8100 + 11664 + 15876 + 22500) - 18723 = \frac{58140}{3} - 18723 = 19380 - 18723 = 657.000$$

$$SS_{AB} = 657 - 507 - 147 = 3.000$$

**Step 8 — $SS_E$:**

$$SS_E = 689 - 657 = 32.000$$

**Step 9 — ANOVA table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05, 1, 8}$ | Decision |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A (Weave) | 507.000 | 1 | 507.000 | 126.75 | 5.32 | **Significant** |
| B (Temperature) | 147.000 | 1 | 147.000 | 36.75 | 5.32 | **Significant** |
| A × B | 3.000 | 1 | 3.000 | 0.75 | 5.32 | Not Significant |
| Error | 32.000 | 8 | 4.000 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **689.000** | **11** | — | — | — | — |

**Conclusion:** Weave type and dye temperature both have statistically significant effects on color-fastness, but their interaction is not significant — the two factors act **additively**. Twill weave at 90 °C produces the highest rating.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain, with the help of a sketch, how an **interaction plot** is interpreted. Why is it *dangerous* to interpret main effects when the interaction is significant?

**Model Answer:**

An **interaction plot** is a line graph in which:

- The x-axis lists the levels of one factor (say, factor B).
- Separate lines are drawn, one for each level of factor A.
- The y-axis shows the cell mean response.

**Interpretation rules:**

1. **Parallel lines ⇒ no interaction** (factors act additively). The vertical shift between lines represents the *main effect of A*; the horizontal trend represents the *main effect of B*.
2. **Non-parallel lines (lines crossing or fanning out) ⇒ interaction present.** The effect of A depends on the level of B (and vice versa).

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
Response
   |       A2 (rising steeply)
   |      /
   |     /          A2
   |    /          /
   |   /     A1  /
   |  /  __/  __/
   | / _/  _/
   |/__/  _/         A1 (gentler slope)
   |____________________
        B1          B2
        Factor B
```

In the sketch, line A1 and line A2 have **different slopes** — the gain from B1 to B2 is larger at A2 than at A1 — hence an **interaction** exists.

**Why main effects are dangerous when interaction is significant:**

When $F_{AB}$ is significant, the *average* main effect of A is a misleading single number. For example, the mean response at A1 may be lower than at A2 *overall*, but at level B1, A1 might actually be *higher* than A2. Reporting only the "main effect of A" hides this reversal and could lead the engineer to choose a suboptimal level. The safe practice is to **interpret interaction plots** and pick the *best cell combination* directly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** The following data come from an experiment on the **corrosion rate (mpy)** of a metal under two factors: **pH (A: Acidic, Neutral)** and **Inhibitor concentration (B: 0%, 2%, 4%)** — a **2 × 3** design with **two replicates per cell**. Construct the ANOVA table and test the significance of all effects at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use $F_{0.05, 1, 6} = 5.99$ and $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$.

| Cell | Rep 1 | Rep 2 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A1B1 (Acidic, 0%) | 5.4 | 5.0 |
| A1B2 (Acidic, 2%) | 4.0 | 3.8 |
| A1B3 (Acidic, 4%) | 3.2 | 3.0 |
| A2B1 (Neutral, 0%) | 2.6 | 2.2 |
| A2B2 (Neutral, 2%) | 2.0 | 1.8 |
| A2B3 (Neutral, 4%) | 1.6 | 1.4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cell totals and marginals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{11} &= 10.4,\quad T_{12} = 7.8,\quad T_{13} = 6.2 \\
T_{21} &= 4.8,\quad T_{22} = 3.8,\quad T_{23} = 3.0 \\
T_{1..} &= 24.4,\quad T_{2..} = 11.6 \\
T_{.1.} &= 15.2,\quad T_{.2.} = 11.6,\quad T_{.3.} = 9.2 \\
T_{...} &= 36.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Correction factor:** $a = 2, b = 3, n = 2, abn = 12$

$$C = \frac{36^2}{12} = \frac{1296}{12} = 108.000$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squares of raw observations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum x^2 &= 5.4^2+5.0^2+4.0^2+3.8^2+3.2^2+3.0^2+2.6^2+2.2^2+2.0^2+1.8^2+1.6^2+1.4^2 \\
&= 29.16+25.00+16.00+14.44+10.24+9.00+6.76+4.84+4.00+3.24+2.56+1.96 \\
&= 127.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — $SS_{Total} = 127.20 - 108.00 = 19.200$**

**Step 5 — $SS_A$:**

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{bn}\sum T_{i..}^2 - C = \frac{1}{6}(24.4^2 + 11.6^2) - 108 = \frac{1}{6}(595.36 + 134.56) - 108 = \frac{729.92}{6} - 108 = 121.653 - 108 = 13.653$$

**Step 6 — $SS_B$:**

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{an}\sum T_{.j.}^2 - C = \frac{1}{4}(15.2^2 + 11.6^2 + 9.2^2) - 108 = \frac{1}{4}(231.04 + 134.56 + 84.64) - 108 = \frac{450.24}{4} - 108 = 112.56 - 108 = 4.560$$

**Step 7 — $SS_{Cells}$ and $SS_{AB}$:**

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{1}{n}\sum\sum T_{ij}^2 - C = \frac{1}{2}(10.4^2+7.8^2+6.2^2+4.8^2+3.8^2+3.0^2) - 108 = \frac{1}{2}(108.16+60.84+38.44+23.04+14.44+9.00) - 108 = \frac{253.92}{2} - 108 = 126.96 - 108 = 18.960$$

$$SS_{AB} = 18.960 - 13.653 - 4.560 = 0.747$$

**Step 8 — $SS_E$:** $SS_E = 19.200 - 18.960 = 0.240$

**Step 9 — ANOVA table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{0.05, \cdot, 6}$ | Decision |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A (pH) | 13.653 | 1 | 13.653 | 341.33 | 5.99 | **Significant** |
| B (Inhibitor) | 4.560 | 2 | 2.280 | 57.00 | 5.14 | **Significant** |
| A × B | 0.747 | 2 | 0.374 | 9.34 | 5.14 | **Significant** |
| Error | 0.240 | 6 | 0.040 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **19.200** | **11** | — | — | — | — |

**Conclusion:** All three effects — pH, inhibitor concentration, and their interaction — are statistically significant at the 5% level. Since the interaction is significant, the engineer **must examine the cell means** rather than rely on main-effect interpretations. The lowest corrosion rate occurs at *A2 (Neutral pH) with B3 (4% inhibitor)*.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the data in part (a), draw the **interaction plot** and discuss what the pattern of the lines implies about the engineering process.

**Model Answer:**

**Cell means:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{11} &= 5.20,\quad \bar{x}_{12} = 3.90,\quad \bar{x}_{13} = 3.10 \\
\bar{x}_{21} &= 2.40,\quad \bar{x}_{22} = 1.90,\quad \bar{x}_{23} = 1.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Interaction plot (text-rendered):**

```
Corrosion (mpy)
   5.5 |*
   5.0 | *\
   4.5 |  \
   4.0 |   * \
   3.5 |    \*
   3.0 |     \  *
   2.5 |      \    *  (A2 = Neutral)
   2.0 |       \  /
   1.5 |        \/  *
   1.0 |
       +-----|-----|-----|
            B1=0%  B2=2%  B3=4%
                  Inhibitor
```

**Legend:** The steeper descending line corresponds to factor level **A1 (Acidic)**; the gentler descending line corresponds to **A2 (Neutral)**.

**Interpretation:**

- Both lines slope *downward* — increasing inhibitor concentration reduces corrosion in both pH environments (consistent main effect of B).
- The **two lines are not parallel** — the corrosion drops more sharply as inhibitor increases in the acidic environment (5.20 → 3.10, a drop of 2.10) than in the neutral environment (2.40 → 1.50, a drop of 0.90).
- This **non-parallelism** is the visual signature of a **significant A × B interaction** — inhibitor concentration is more *effective* in the acidic medium than in the neutral medium.

**Engineering Implication:** The inhibitor is doing heavy lifting in the acidic environment where corrosion is naturally high. In the neutral environment, the absolute corrosion is already low, so additional inhibitor yields smaller absolute gains. Practically, the engineer should:

1. **Use more inhibitor** in acidic conditions for maximum protection.
2. The "best single combination" is **Neutral pH with 4% inhibitor** — but if the process *must* run in acidic conditions, the engineer should also use 4% inhibitor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ANOVA in Factorial Design**

### Core Concepts
- **Factorial design** tests *all* combinations of factor levels → this is what enables detection of **interactions**.
- Two-factor design produces **a × b** treatment cells with **n** replicates each, total **N = a × b × n**.
- The model is $x_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$.
- Constraint conditions on the effects ensure identifiability (e.g., $\sum \alpha_i = 0$).

### Variability Decomposition (The Identity)
- $SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$
- $SS_E$ is the *within-cell* variability — the benchmark for all F-tests.
- $SS_{AB}$ is computed either directly (using cell means) or as $SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$.

### Degrees of Freedom
- $df_A = a - 1$, $df_B = b - 1$, $df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1)$, $df_E = ab(n-1)$, $df_{Total} = abn - 1$.
- **df additivity check:** $(a-1) + (b-1) + (a-1)(b-1) + ab(n-1) = abn - 1$ ✓

### F-Tests (Always vs. $MS_E$)
- $F_A = MS_A / MS_E$, $F_B = MS_B / MS_E$, $F_{AB} = MS_{AB} / MS_E$.
- Each F is compared to $F_{\alpha, df_{effect}, ab(n-1)}$.
- The **interaction is always tested first** — if significant, it modifies the interpretation of the main effects.

### Computational Shortcut (Correction Factor)
- $C = T^2 / (abn)$
- $SS_{Total} = \sum x_{ijk}^2 - C$
- $SS_A = \dfrac{1}{bn}\sum T_{i..}^2 - C$
- $SS_B = \dfrac{1}{an}\sum T_{.j.}^2 - C$
- $SS_{Cells} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum\sum T_{ij.}^2 - C$, then $SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$, $SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells}$

### Interpretation Hierarchy
1. Look at the **interaction** first.
2. If interaction is **significant** → discuss cell means directly; main effects are *conditional*.
3. If interaction is **not significant** → interpret main effects as *additive contributions*; the optimal level of A and B can be chosen **independently**.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Forgetting the correction factor.
- Mis-computing $df_E$ (use $ab(n-1)$, not $ab - 1$).
- Comparing F to the wrong critical value (use the correct numerator df).
- Reporting "significant" without citing the F-critical value from the F-table.
- Interpreting main effects when interaction is significant without caveats.
- Confusing $SS_{Cells}$ with $SS_{AB}$ — $SS_{Cells}$ contains main effects too.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Mile Advice for KTU Exam:** Always show **all four steps explicitly** in your answer: (1) Hypotheses, (2) ANOVA table with all SS, df, MS, F, (3) Critical F-value, (4) Decision + engineering interpretation. This is the marker-friendly structure that secures full marks in the 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Factorial Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT416 - INTRODUCTION TO DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** General Factorial Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **General Factorial Design** (often called the *a × b Factorial Design* in its two-factor form) is a Design of Experiments (DoE) strategy in which **two or more independent variables (factors)** are investigated simultaneously, with each factor set at **two or more levels**, and **all possible combinations of factor levels** are observed. It is termed *"general"* because it allows factors at any number of levels (a, b, c, …) and any number of replicates (n), unlike the *special* 2^k or 3^k designs that restrict level counts.

Mathematically, the two-factor general factorial design tests the hypothesis that the mean response is affected by:
- The **main effect** of factor A,
- The **main effect** of factor B, and
- The **interaction effect** A×B (i.e., whether the effect of A depends on the level of B, and vice versa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The 14-mark problems in this module routinely focus on the **two-factor, fixed-effects model with replication (a×b design, n ≥ 2)**. Master this before extending to three-factor designs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **chef developing a new recipe for biryani** and you want to know:
- How does the **cooking time** (Factor A: 30 / 60 / 90 min) affect taste?
- How does the **type of rice** (Factor B: Basmati / Sona Masoori) affect taste?
- Does the rice variety change the optimum cooking time (interaction)?

A *General Factorial Design* tells you to **cook every combination** (3 × 2 = 6 recipes), each **twice** (to average out accidental saltiness), and then use ANOVA to scientifically prove which factor — and which combination — truly matters. The brilliance of factorial design is that you obtain the interaction effect "for free" from the same experiment you ran for the main effects.

## 1.3 When to Use General Factorial Designs

| Scenario | Recommended Design |
|----------|--------------------|
| 2 factors, both at 2 levels | 2² Factorial (Special Case) |
| 2 factors, different level counts | **General a×b Factorial** |
| 3 or more factors, any level counts | **General a×b×c Factorial** |
| Screening many factors | 2^k Fractional Factorial |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Engineers prefer the *general* form over the *2^k* form whenever one factor is naturally continuous (e.g., temperature, pressure, time) and the other is categorical (e.g., machine, operator, supplier). It captures **curvature in response** without the cost of response-surface methodology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Model (Two-Factor Fixed-Effects Model)

For a two-factor factorial design with factor A at *a* levels and factor B at *b* levels, replicated *n* times per cell, the observation model is:

$$y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + (\tau\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

where the components are:
- $y_{ijk}$ = the response from the $k^{th}$ replicate at level $i$ of A and level $j$ of B
- $\mu$ = the overall (grand) mean response
- $\tau_i$ = main effect of the $i^{th}$ level of factor A (deviation of Aᵢ from $\mu$)
- $\beta_j$ = main effect of the $j^{th}$ level of factor B
- $(\tau\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect between Aᵢ and Bⱼ
- $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ = random error, $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, i.i.d.

**Index ranges:** $\; i = 1, 2, \dots, a;\; j = 1, 2, \dots, b;\; k = 1, 2, \dots, n;\; N = abn$ total runs.

**Identifiability constraints (fixed effects):**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{a}\tau_i = 0, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{b}\beta_j = 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\tau\beta)_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^{b}(\tau\beta)_{ij} = 0$$

## 2.2 Hypothesis Test Framework

| Effect | Null Hypothesis | Alternative | Reject H₀ if F₀ > F_{α, df_treat, df_E} |
|--------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------------------------------|
